2. **Cross-Validation:** Uses k folds to obtain a robust estimate of generalisation error. Helps in hyperparameter tuning (e.g., choosing $\lambda$) without leaking test data. Reduces dependence on a single train/validation split.

3. **Early Stopping:** Monitors validation error during training. Stops when validation error begins to rise (i.e., when the model starts to overfit). Acts as an implicit regulariser.

4. **More Training Data / Data Augmentation:** Larger datasets constrain the model more tightly, reducing variance. Augmentation (rotations, flips, noise injection) artificially expands the dataset.

5. **Ensembling (Bagging):** Trains many high-variance models on bootstrapped samples and averages their predictions. Variance reduces roughly as $1/M$ for $M$ models, with only a small bias increase.

6. **Dropout (Neural Networks):** Randomly disables a fraction of neurons during each training step. Prevents co-adaptation and effectively trains an ensemble of sub-networks.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of **k-fold cross-validation**. How does it help in **detecting and preventing overfitting**? Describe the algorithm with a suitable example where $k = 5$.

**Model Solution (Part b):**

*Concept of k-Fold Cross-Validation (2 marks):*
k-fold cross-validation is a resampling technique used to evaluate a model's generalisation ability and to tune hyperparameters. The dataset is partitioned into $k$ equal (or nearly equal) subsets called **folds**. The model is trained $k$ times — each time using $k-1$ folds for training and the remaining 1 fold for validation. The final performance is the **average of the $k$ validation errors**.

*Algorithm Steps (2 marks):*
1. Shuffle the dataset randomly.
2. Split it into $k$ disjoint folds of (approximately) equal size: $F_{1}, F_{2}, \ldots, F_{k}$.
3. For $i = 1$ to $k$:
   - Train the model on $F_{1} \cup F_{2} \cup \ldots \cup F_{i-1} \cup F_{i+1} \cup \ldots \cup F_{k}$.
   - Validate on $F_{i}$, recording the validation error $E_{i}$.
4. Compute the **mean cross-validation error**: $E_{CV} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} E_{i}$.

*Example with k = 5 (2 marks):*
Suppose we have 1000 samples and choose $k = 5$. Each fold has 200 samples.
- **Iteration 1:** Train on Folds 2, 3, 4, 5 (800 samples), validate on Fold 1 (200 samples) → record $E_{1}$.
- **Iteration 2:** Train on Folds 1, 3, 4, 5, validate on Fold 2 → record $E_{2}$.
- **Iteration 3:** Train on Folds 1, 2, 4, 5, validate on Fold 3 → record $E_{3}$.
- **Iteration 4:** Train on Folds 1, 2, 3, 5, validate on Fold 4 → record $E_{4}$.
- **Iteration 5:** Train on Folds 1, 2, 3, 4, validate on Fold 5 → record $E_{5}$.
- Compute $E_{CV} = (E_{1}+E_{2}+E_{3}+E_{4}+E_{5})/5$.

*How it Detects and Prevents Overfitting (1 mark):*
- If the model is overfit, the cross-validation error will be **high** (training error will be low) — providing a clear signal.
- The **variance of $E_{i}$ across folds** indicates model stability: high variance across folds indicates sensitivity to training data (overfitting).
- For **prevention**, cross-validation is used to tune hyperparameters (e.g., $\lambda$, polynomial degree, tree depth) — selecting values that minimise $E_{CV}$ rather than the training error. This steers the model towards the **good fit regime**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Examiner's Warning:**
> 1. **Confusing overfitting with low training accuracy:** Overfitting is defined by the *gap* between training and test performance, not by training accuracy alone. Students who say "overfitting = low training error" lose marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the independence assumption** in the bias-variance derivation: This is the most commonly skipped step.
> 3. **Listing techniques without explanation:** KTU expects *justification* — never just write a bullet list. Each prevention technique must come with a *mechanism of action*.
> 4. **Drawing diagrams without labels:** Unlabelled axes lose at least 1 mark in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Mixing up L1 and L2 regularisation:** L1 → Lasso → sparsity; L2 → Ridge → shrinkage. Conflating them is a frequent error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Generalisation** = model performs well on *unseen* data from the same distribution.
- **Overfitting** = low training error, high test error, **high variance, low bias**.
- **Underfitting** = high training error, high test error, **high bias, low variance**.
- The **bias-variance decomposition**: $E[(y-\hat{f})^{2}] = \text{Bias}^{2} + \text{Variance} + \sigma^{2}$.
- **$\sigma^{2}$** is the irreducible noise floor — cannot be removed by any model.
- **Causes of overfitting:** complex model, little data, noisy labels, irrelevant features, overtraining.
- **Detection methods:** hold-out validation, learning curves, k-fold cross-validation, test-set evaluation.
- **Prevention techniques:** more data, regularisation (L1/L2), early stopping, dropout, ensembling (bagging), feature selection, simpler model, data augmentation.
- **L1 (Lasso):** $\lambda \Sigma \vert w_{i} \vert$ → induces **sparsity** (feature selection).
- **L2 (Ridge):** $\lambda \Sigma w_{i}^{2}$ → induces **weight shrinkage**.
- **k-Fold CV:** train on $k-1$ folds, validate on 1; average the $k$ errors.
- **Optimal model** = point of minimum sum of bias² + variance, NOT minimum training error.
- **Always quote the decomposition formula** in any 14-mark question on this topic — it is a guaranteed 2–3 marks.
- **Draw a labelled diagram** of the three fitting regimes — guaranteed 1–2 marks.
- **Remember the analogy:** "Memorising past papers vs understanding the subject" — examiners love it.
- **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This topic maps to **CO2** (Understand generalisation principles and apply techniques to control model complexity).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LASSO and RIDGE Regularization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT602 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LASSO and RIDGE Regularization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Regularization?

In the context of **Supervised Machine Learning**, **Regularization** is a mathematical technique used to prevent a model from **overfitting** the training data by explicitly penalizing model complexity. It achieves this by adding a penalty term (called a *regularizer*) to the standard loss function, thereby shrinking the magnitude of the model coefficients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Regularization is a shrinkage estimator technique that adds a penalty term $\lambda \cdot \Omega(\mathbf{w})$ to the residual sum of squares (RSS), forcing the learning algorithm to prefer simpler models with smaller coefficient magnitudes to improve generalization on unseen data.

Mathematically, the generalized regularized objective is:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = L(\mathbf{w}) + \lambda \cdot \Omega(\mathbf{w})$$

where:
- $L(\mathbf{w})$ = **Loss function** (e.g., Mean Squared Error)
- $\Omega(\mathbf{w})$ = **Regularization penalty**
- $\lambda$ = **Regularization hyperparameter** (controls the strength of the penalty)
- $\mathbf{w}$ = **Weight vector** of the model

## 1.2 The Two Principal Variants: RIDGE and LASSO

### 1.2.1 RIDGE Regularization (L2 Regularization)

**Ridge Regression**, also known as **L2 Regularization** or **Tikhonov Regularization**, adds the **squared magnitude of coefficients** as a penalty term to the loss function.

$$J_{Ridge}(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} w_j^2$$

The L2 norm penalizes the **Euclidean norm** of the weight vector, encouraging all weights to be small but **never exactly zero**.

### 1.2.2 LASSO Regularization (L1 Regularization)

**LASSO** stands for **Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator**. It adds the **absolute magnitude of coefficients** as a penalty term.

$$J_{LASSO}(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert w_j \vert$$

The L1 norm penalizes the **Manhattan norm** of the weight vector. Its unique ability is to drive certain coefficients **exactly to zero**, effectively performing **automatic feature selection**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** L1 (LASSO) produces *sparse models*, while L2 (Ridge) produces *dense models with small coefficients*. This is the central comparative point examiners love to test.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy: The "Two Coaches" Story

Imagine you are training two athletes (students) for a marathon:

- **Ridge Coach (L2)**: Says, *"Do not run too fast in any single direction. Spread your energy evenly across all parts of your training."* The result? All muscles are used moderately — nothing is eliminated, but nothing is extreme. This corresponds to **shrinking all coefficients** towards zero.

- **LASSO Coach (L1)**: Says, *"Use ONLY the most essential muscles. Completely stop using the useless ones."* The result? Some muscles are activated strongly, while the rest are completely turned off (coefficients become **exactly 0**). This corresponds to **feature selection**.

## 1.4 Why Do We Need Regularization? — The Bias-Variance Connection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Insight:** Regularization deliberately introduces a small amount of **bias** into the model in exchange for a **significant reduction in variance**. This is the famous **Bias-Variance Tradeoff**, and it is the mathematical justification for both LASSO and RIDGE.

| Property | No Regularization | Ridge (L2) | LASSO (L1) |
|----------|------------------|------------|------------|
| **Bias** | Low | Moderate | Moderate to High |
| **Variance** | High | Reduced | Significantly Reduced |
| **Interpretability** | Poor with many features | Poor (all features kept) | Excellent (sparse) |
| **Multicollinearity** | Fails | Handles well | Handles well |
| **Feature Selection** | No | No | Yes (Automatic) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bias-Variance Decomposition

The expected prediction error of a model at a point $\mathbf{x}$ can be decomposed as:

$$E[(y - \hat{f}(\mathbf{x}))^2] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}))}_{\text{Systematic Error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}(\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}))}_{\text{Model Sensitivity}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}$$

**Interpretation for KTU:**
- **High bias** = Model is too simple → Underfitting
- **High variance** = Model is too complex → Overfitting
- **Regularization** aims to find the "sweet spot" by trading a tolerable increase in bias for a massive reduction in variance.

## 2.2 Operational Logic: How Regularization Works Step-by-Step

### For Ridge (L2):
1. Compute the **standard OLS loss**: $\text{RSS} = \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$
2. Compute the **L2 penalty**: $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} w_j^2$
3. Sum them to form $J_{Ridge}(\mathbf{w})$
4. Take the gradient and set it to zero (or use gradient descent)
5. The penalty **shrinks weights proportionally to their magnitude** — large weights are penalized more, but no weight is ever forced to zero.

### For LASSO (L1):
1. Compute the **standard OLS loss**: $\text{RSS} = \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$
2. Compute the **L1 penalty**: $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert w_j \vert$
3. Sum them to form $J_{LASSO}(\mathbf{w})$
4. The penalty applies a **constant penalty** for any non-zero weight, which is why small weights get pushed **exactly to zero**.

## 2.3 Closed-Form Solution for Ridge Regression

Ridge has a beautiful **closed-form solution** because the L2 norm is differentiable everywhere:

$$\hat{\mathbf{w}}_{Ridge} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property:** The term $+\lambda \mathbf{I}$ makes the matrix $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I}$ always **invertible**, even when $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ is singular (e.g., when $p > n$ or features are collinear). This is the **primary computational advantage of Ridge**.

## 2.4 Why LASSO Produces Sparsity — The Subgradient Explanation

The L1 norm $|w_j|$ is **not differentiable** at $w_j = 0$. Its subgradient is:

$$\frac{\partial \vert w_j \vert}{\partial w_j} = \begin{cases} -1 & \text{if } w_j < 0 \\ [-1, 1] & \text{if } w_j = 0 \\ +1 & \text{if } w_j > 0 \end{cases}$$

When the LASSO objective is minimized, the optimality condition for each weight becomes:

$$-\mathbf{x}_j^T (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}) + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(w_j) = 0$$

If the absolute value of the "no-penalty" solution $|w_j^{OLS}|$ is **less than $\lambda$**, the penalty forces $w_j = 0$ exactly. This is mathematically why LASSO performs **soft-thresholding**.

## 2.5 The Constraint Formulation (Geometric View)

Both Ridge and LASSO can be viewed as **constrained optimization problems**:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}} \text{RSS}(\mathbf{w}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \Omega(\mathbf{w}) \leq t$$

- **Ridge:** $\sum_{j=1}^{p} w_j^2 \leq t$ (a **sphere** in weight space)
- **LASSO:** $\sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert w_j \vert \leq t$ (a **diamond/rhombus** in weight space)

The contours of the RSS loss are ellipses centered at the OLS solution. The optimal point is where the smallest ellipse **touches** the constraint region. The corners of the LASSO diamond (with sharp points on the axes) are more likely to be touched, producing **sparse solutions**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize all entries in this table. Questions on regularization almost always reduce to selecting the right formula.

| # | Concept | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | General Regularized Loss | $J(\mathbf{w}) = L(\mathbf{w}) + \lambda \Omega(\mathbf{w})$ | Foundation equation |
| 2 | Ridge Objective | $J_{Ridge} = \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda \sum w_j^2$ | L2 penalty |
| 3 | LASSO Objective | $J_{LASSO} = \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda \sum \vert w_j \vert$ | L1 penalty |
| 4 | Ridge Closed Form | $\hat{\mathbf{w}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$ | Always solvable |
| 5 | LASSO Soft Threshold | $w_j = \text{sign}(w_j^{OLS})(\vert w_j^{OLS} \vert - \lambda)_+$ | Sparsity operator |
| 6 | Ridge Penalty Term | $\lambda \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2$ | Squared L2 norm |
| 7 | LASSO Penalty Term | $\lambda \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_1$ | L1 norm |
| 8 | Effective DOF Ridge | $\text{df}(\lambda) = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \frac{d_j^2}{d_j^2 + \lambda}$ | $d_j$ are singular values |
| 9 | Bias-Variance Tradeoff | $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Decomposition |
| 10 | Elastic Net | $J = \text{RSS} + \lambda_1 \sum \vert w_j \vert + \lambda_2 \sum w_j^2$ | Hybrid L1 + L2 |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Bioinformatics / Genomics:** LASSO is used to identify disease-associated genes from thousands of features when sample size is small.
2. **Financial Risk Modeling:** Ridge is used to handle correlated macroeconomic indicators (multicollinearity) in credit scoring.
3. **Computer Vision:** Both are used in deep CNNs as **weight decay** (Ridge in neural network context).
4. **Signal Processing:** Ridge regression is used for channel equalization in noisy communication channels.
5. **Autonomous Driving:** Feature selection via LASSO helps in real-time object detection model compression.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ridge Regression Closed-Form Solution

**Starting Point:** The Ridge objective function in matrix form.

$$J_{Ridge}(\mathbf{w}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w})^T (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}) + \lambda \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 1: Expand the RSS term.**

$$J_{Ridge}(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y} - 2\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w} + \lambda \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 2: Take the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ and set it to zero.**

The first term $\mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y}$ is constant and disappears upon differentiation. The second term differentiates to $-2\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$. The third term differentiates to $2\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w}$. The fourth term differentiates to $2\lambda \mathbf{w}$.

$$\frac{\partial J_{Ridge}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = -2\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w} + 2\lambda \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 3: Rearrange the equation to isolate $\mathbf{w}$.**

$$2\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w} + 2\lambda \mathbf{w} = 2\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$$

$$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w} + \lambda \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$$

$$(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I}) \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\mathbf{w}$ by left-multiplying with the inverse matrix.**

$$\boxed{\hat{\mathbf{w}}_{Ridge} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}}$$

**Why this matters:** The presence of $\lambda \mathbf{I}$ guarantees that even when $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ is singular (e.g., when the number of features exceeds the number of samples, $p > n$), the matrix $(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})$ is **always positive definite** and therefore invertible.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gradient Descent Update Rule for Ridge

**Starting Point:** The Ridge cost function for a single training example (stochastic gradient descent view).

$$J_{Ridge}(w) = (y - \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{w})^2 + \lambda w^2$$

**Step 1: Compute the partial derivative with respect to a single weight $w_j$.**

$$\frac{\partial J_{Ridge}}{\partial w_j} = -2x_j(y - \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{w}) + 2\lambda w_j$$

**Step 2: Substitute the prediction error $\mathbf{e} = (y - \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{w})$.**

$$\frac{\partial J_{Ridge}}{\partial w_j} = -2x_j \cdot e + 2\lambda w_j$$

**Step 3: Apply the gradient descent update rule with learning rate $\eta$.**

$$w_j^{(t+1)} = w_j^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial J_{Ridge}}{\partial w_j}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the gradient.**

$$w_j^{(t+1)} = w_j^{(t)} - \eta \cdot (-2x_j e + 2\lambda w_j^{(t)})$$

**Step 5: Simplify and group terms.**

$$w_j^{(t+1)} = w_j^{(t)} (1 - 2\eta \lambda) + 2\eta x_j e$$

$$\boxed{w_j^{(t+1)} = (1 - 2\eta \lambda) \cdot w_j^{(t)} + 2\eta x_j e}$$

**Engineering Insight:** The factor $(1 - 2\eta \lambda)$ is always less than 1. This means **before** the data-driven update is applied, the weight is first **shrunk** by a multiplicative factor. This is precisely why Ridge is also called **Weight Decay** in deep learning literature.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: LASSO Soft-Thresholding Rule

**Starting Point:** LASSO objective using orthogonality assumption ($\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{I}$).

$$J_{LASSO}(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{j=1}^{p} (w_j^{OLS} - w_j)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert w_j \vert$$

**Step 1: Differentiate with respect to $w_j$ and set to zero.**

$$-2(w_j^{OLS} - w_j) + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(w_j) = 0$$

**Step 2: Solve for $w_j$.**

$$w_j = w_j^{OLS} - \frac{\lambda}{2} \cdot \text{sign}(w_j)$$

**Step 3: Apply case analysis.**

**Case A:** If $w_j > 0$, then $\text{sign}(w_j) = +1$:

$$w_j = w_j^{OLS} - \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

This is valid only if $w_j > 0$, i.e., $w_j^{OLS} > \lambda/2$.

**Case B:** If $w_j < 0$, then $\text{sign}(w_j) = -1$:

$$w_j = w_j^{OLS} + \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

This is valid only if $w_j < 0$, i.e., $w_j^{OLS} < -\lambda/2$.

**Case C:** If $\vert w_j^{OLS} \vert \leq \lambda/2$, then $w_j = 0$ (sparsity).

**Step 4: Combine all cases using the soft-thresholding operator.**

$$\boxed{w_j = \text{sign}(w_j^{OLS}) \cdot \max\left(0, \ \vert w_j^{OLS} \vert - \frac{\lambda}{2}\right)}$$

**Why this matters:** The $\max(0, \cdot)$ function is the **soft-thresholding operator**. It explicitly sets small coefficients to exactly zero, which is the mathematical source of LASSO's feature selection property.

## 3.4 Numerical Example: Ridge vs LASSO on Toy Data

Consider a regression problem with three features, OLS estimates: $w_1^{OLS} = 0.5$, $w_2^{OLS} = 0.2$, $w_3^{OLS} = 3.0$, and $\lambda = 1$.

**Ridge solution (orthogonal case):**

$$w_j^{Ridge} = \frac{w_j^{OLS}}{1 + \lambda} = \frac{w_j^{OLS}}{2}$$

- $w_1 = 0.25$, $w_2 = 0.10$, $w_3 = 1.50$ → All **shrunk**, none zero.

**LASSO solution (orthogonal case, soft-threshold with $\lambda/2 = 0.5$):**

- $w_1 = \text{sign}(0.5) \cdot \max(0, 0.5 - 0.5) = +1 \cdot 0 = 0$
- $w_2 = \text{sign}(0.2) \cdot \max(0, 0.2 - 0.5) = +1 \cdot 0 = 0$
- $w_3 = \text{sign}(3.0) \cdot \max(0, 3.0 - 0.5) = +1 \cdot 2.5 = 2.5$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** LASSO eliminated features 1 and 2 entirely (sparsity), while Ridge only reduced their magnitudes. LASSO kept the strong feature 3, but also shrunk it. This is the practical essence of feature selection by LASSO.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) | Ridge (L2) | LASSO (L1) | Elastic Net |
|-----------|------------------------------|------------|------------|-------------|
| Penalty Term | None | $\lambda \sum w_j^2$ | $\lambda \sum \vert w_j \vert$ | Both combined |
| Closed-Form Solution | Yes ($\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$ | Yes | No (iterative) | No |
| Sparsity | No | No | **Yes** | Yes |
| Feature Selection | No | No | **Yes** | Yes |
| Handles $p > n$ | No | **Yes** | Yes (selects $n$ only) | Yes |
| Handles Multicollinearity | Poorly | **Excellent** | Good | **Excellent** |
| Differentiable | Yes | Yes | No (at 0) | No (at 0) |
| Computationally | Trivial | Trivial | Coordinate descent | Coordinate descent |
| Best Use Case | Low-dimensional, clean data | High multicollinearity | High-dimensional, sparse truth | Correlated groups of features |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Regularization in the Machine Learning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Input Data X, y] --> B[Split into Train and Test Sets]
    B --> C[Train Model without Regularization]
    B --> D[Train Model with Ridge L2]
    B --> E[Train Model with LASSO L1]
    C --> F[Evaluate Test MSE]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{High Variance Detected}
    G -- Yes --> H[Increase lambda to Shrink Weights]
    G -- No --> I[Model is Well Regularized]
    H --> D
    H --> E
    I --> J[Deploy Final Model]
    F --> K[Select Model with Lowest Validation Error]
    K --> J
```

## 4.2 Geometric Interpretation: Constraint Regions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[OLS Objective RSS w] --> B[Minimize RSS subject to Constraint]
    B --> C{Ridge L2: Sphere Constraint}
    B --> D{LASSO L1: Diamond Constraint}
    C --> E[Contour touches sphere smoothly]
    D --> F[Contour touches diamond at corner]
    E --> G[All weights non-zero, dense solution]
    F --> H[Some weights exactly zero, sparse solution]
    G --> I[Final Ridge Model]
    H --> J[Final LASSO Model with Feature Selection]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of LASSO Solver (Coordinate Descent)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize all weights wj to 0] --> B[Set lambda and tolerance]
    B --> C[Loop over each feature j from 1 to p]
    C --> D[Compute partial residual rj excluding feature j]
    D --> E[Compute unregularized coefficient zj]
    E --> F{Is |zj| greater than lambda by 2?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Set wj = sign of zj times |zj| minus lambda by 2]
    F -- No --> H[Set wj = 0 exact sparsity]
    G --> I{Convergence reached?}
    H --> I
    I -- No --> C
    I -- Yes --> J[Output Sparse Weight Vector w]
    J --> K[Identify Selected Features indices where wj is non-zero]
```

## 4.4 Hyperparameter Lambda Behavior Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[lambda equals 0] --> B[OLS No Penalty, High Variance]
    C[lambda is small] --> D[Slight Shrinkage, Near OLS]
    E[lambda is moderate] --> F[Optimal Bias Variance Balance]
    G[lambda is large] --> H[All weights near zero, High Bias]
    I[lambda equals infinity] --> J[All weights exactly zero, Null Model]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define L1 and L2 Regularization. State the key difference between LASSO and Ridge regression in terms of their penalty terms and their effect on model coefficients.**

**Model Answer:**

L1 Regularization (LASSO) adds the sum of absolute values of coefficients as a penalty: $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert w_j \vert$.

L2 Regularization (Ridge) adds the sum of squared coefficients as a penalty: $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} w_j^2$.

**Key Differences:**
- **Penalty:** L1 uses absolute values, L2 uses squared values.
- **Effect on coefficients:** L1 can drive coefficients to **exactly zero** (producing sparse models and performing automatic feature selection). L2 only **shrinks** coefficients towards zero but never makes them exactly zero (producing dense models with all features retained).
- **Differentiability:** L2 is differentiable everywhere; L1 is not differentiable at zero.
- **Solution:** Ridge has a closed-form solution; LASSO requires iterative methods like coordinate descent.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of the hyperparameter $\lambda$ in regularized regression. What happens when $\lambda = 0$ and when $\lambda \to \infty$?**

**Model Answer:**

The hyperparameter $\lambda$ (sometimes denoted $\alpha$) controls the **strength** of the regularization penalty applied to the model coefficients.

- When **$\lambda = 0$**: The penalty term vanishes completely. The objective reduces to the ordinary least squares (OLS) loss, and the model reverts to standard linear regression with **no regularization**. This often leads to overfitting, especially in high-dimensional settings.

- When **$\lambda \to \infty$**: The penalty term dominates the loss function. To minimize $J(\mathbf{w})$, the model is forced to push all coefficients $\mathbf{w}$ towards (or to) zero. The model becomes extremely simple, essentially predicting the mean of the target variable. This causes severe **underfitting**.

- For an **optimal $\lambda$** (typically found via cross-validation), the model achieves the best bias-variance tradeoff, balancing underfitting and overfitting.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the closed-form solution for Ridge Regression from the regularized objective function. Explain why the $\lambda \mathbf{I}$ term guarantees numerical stability even when $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ is singular. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the Bias-Variance tradeoff in the context of regularization. Explain with appropriate equations how Ridge and LASSO manage this tradeoff. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Ridge Closed-Form

**Step 1:** Write the Ridge objective in matrix form:

$$J_{Ridge}(\mathbf{w}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w})^T(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}) + \lambda \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 2:** Expand:

$$J_{Ridge}(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y} - 2\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w} + \lambda \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $\mathbf{w}$:

$$\frac{\partial J_{Ridge}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = -2\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w} + 2\lambda \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 4:** Set the gradient to zero:

$$(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I}) \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $\mathbf{w}$:

$$\boxed{\hat{\mathbf{w}}_{Ridge} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}}$$

**Numerical Stability Explanation:**

The matrix $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ becomes **singular** (non-invertible) in two common situations:
1. When the number of features $p$ exceeds the number of samples $n$ (the "high-dimensional" case, e.g., genomics).
2. When two or more features are **perfectly collinear** (linearly dependent).

By adding $\lambda \mathbf{I}$ (a multiple of the identity matrix), the diagonal entries of $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ are increased by $\lambda$. This ensures that:
- All eigenvalues of $(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})$ are strictly positive (since the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ are non-negative, and we add $\lambda > 0$ to each).
- The matrix becomes **positive definite** and therefore **invertible**.

This is the **primary computational advantage** of Ridge regression over OLS in ill-conditioned problems.

#### Part (b) — Bias-Variance Tradeoff and Regularization

**Step 1:** State the decomposition:

$$\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f}(\mathbf{x}))^2] = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})) + \text{Var}(\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})) + \sigma^2$$

**Step 2:** Explain the unregularized case (OLS):

With no penalty, OLS is an **unbiased estimator** (its bias is zero in expectation) but has **high variance** when:
- Features are correlated
- $p$ is large relative to $n$
- The data is noisy

This leads to **overfitting**: excellent training performance, poor test performance.

**Step 3:** Explain how Ridge manages the tradeoff:

Ridge adds $\lambda \sum w_j^2$ to the loss. This:
- **Introduces bias** because coefficients are systematically shrunk away from the OLS values.
- **Reduces variance** significantly, especially in multicollinear or high-dimensional settings.
- The net effect is a lower **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** on unseen data, as long as $\lambda$ is properly tuned (e.g., via cross-validation).

**Step 4:** Explain how LASSO manages the tradeoff:

LASSO adds $\lambda \sum \vert w_j \vert$ to the loss. This:
- Introduces slightly **more bias** than Ridge (because of the harsher L1 penalty).
- Produces a **sparser model** by zeroing out irrelevant features, which can further **reduce variance** by eliminating noise variables.
- LASSO is particularly effective when the true model is **sparse** (only a few features are actually relevant).

**Step 5:** Conclude with the design philosophy:

> Regularization deliberately trades a tolerable increase in bias for a substantial reduction in variance, producing a model with **better generalization to unseen data** than the unregularized OLS estimator.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the LASSO (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator) method. Derive the soft-thresholding rule for the LASSO estimator under the orthogonality assumption. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare LASSO, Ridge, and Elastic Net regularization techniques with respect to their penalty functions, sparsity, computational solution, and suitability for different data scenarios. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — LASSO and Soft-Thresholding

**Step 1:** State the LASSO objective:

$$J_{LASSO}(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{w})^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert w_j \vert$$

**Step 2:** Explain the meaning: LASSO minimizes the residual sum of squares subject to an L1-norm constraint on the coefficient vector. It performs both **shrinkage** (like Ridge) and **continuous variable selection** (unlike Ridge) — features with small contributions are eliminated entirely.

**Step 3:** Assume orthogonality ($\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{I}$), so the RSS decomposes as $\sum_j (w_j^{OLS} - w_j)^2$:

$$J_{LASSO}(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{j=1}^{p} (w_j^{OLS} - w_j)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert w_j \vert$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate w.r.t. a single $w_j$ and set to zero:

$$-2(w_j^{OLS} - w_j) + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(w_j) = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow w_j = w_j^{OLS} - \frac{\lambda}{2}\text{sign}(w_j)$$

**Step 5:** Apply case analysis to derive the soft-thresholding rule:

$$\boxed{w_j^{LASSO} = \text{sign}(w_j^{OLS}) \cdot \max\left(0, \ \vert w_j^{OLS} \vert - \frac{\lambda}{2}\right)}$$

**Interpretation:**
- If $\vert w_j^{OLS} \vert > \lambda/2$: shrink the coefficient towards zero by $\lambda/2$.
- If $\vert w_j^{OLS} \vert \leq \lambda/2$: set the coefficient **exactly to zero** (sparsity).
- The soft-thresholding operator produces **piecewise-linear shrinkage** with a discontinuity at zero.

**Step 6:** Conclude by explaining why LASSO does not have a closed-form solution: the absolute value function is **not differentiable at zero**, making the system of normal equations non-linear in $\mathbf{w}$. Iterative methods like **coordinate descent** or **LARS (Least Angle Regression)** are used in practice.

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table Discussion

**Step 1:** State the three penalty functions:
- Ridge: $\lambda_2 \sum w_j^2$
- LASSO: $\lambda_1 \sum \vert w_j \vert$
- Elastic Net: $\lambda_1 \sum \vert w_j \vert + \lambda_2 \sum w_j^2$

**Step 2:** Compare on the four criteria:

**Sparsity:**
- Ridge: **Not sparse** — all features retained with small weights.
- LASSO: **Sparse** — irrelevant features get zero weights.
- Elastic Net: **Sparse** — inherits sparsity from L1 part.

**Computational Solution:**
- Ridge: **Closed form** via $(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$.
- LASSO: **Iterative** via coordinate descent, LARS, or proximal gradient methods.
- Elastic Net: **Iterative**, typically solved by coordinate descent on the combined objective.

**Suitability for Data Scenarios:**
- **Ridge** is best when most features contribute somewhat, and features are **highly correlated** (e.g., multicollinear sensor data, macroeconomic indicators). It stabilizes coefficient estimates.
- **LASSO** is best when only a **small subset of features** is truly relevant and the true model is **sparse** (e.g., gene selection, signal processing with sparse sources).
- **Elastic Net** is best when there are **correlated groups of features** — it encourages group-level sparsity via L1 while maintaining L2's stability for correlated variables (e.g., text classification with correlated word features).

**Step 3:** Conclude:

> Elastic Net combines the strengths of both LASSO (sparsity, feature selection) and Ridge (handles multicollinearity, stable solutions). It is often the **default choice** in modern high-dimensional ML pipelines, with LASSO and Ridge as special cases corresponding to setting $\lambda_2 = 0$ or $\lambda_1 = 0$, respectively.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the L1/L2 distinction in the penalty term** — Writing $\lambda \sum w_j$ instead of $\lambda \sum \vert w_j \vert$ for LASSO. The absolute value bars are **mandatory**.
>
> 2. **Skipping gradient steps in derivations** — Examiners expect every algebraic transition. Writing the final formula without showing the differentiation steps will cost 3–4 marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing closed-form availability** — Ridge has a closed form; LASSO does not. Many students incorrectly state that LASSO also has a closed-form solution.
>
> 4. **Mis-stating the role of $\lambda$** — Students often write "$\lambda$ is the learning rate." It is the **regularization strength parameter**, completely different from the learning rate $\eta$ in gradient descent.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the orthogonality assumption** — The soft-thresholding derivation explicitly requires $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{I}$. Without this assumption, the formula is more complex.
>
> 6. **Not stating the bias-variance tradeoff explicitly** — A 14-mark question on regularization **must** include the bias-variance decomposition equation for full marks.
>
> 7. **Writing the constraint form incorrectly** — Ridge uses a **sphere** ($\sum w_j^2 \leq t$); LASSO uses a **diamond/rhombus** ($\sum \vert w_j \vert \leq t$). Mixing these up costs 2 marks easily.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for LASSO and RIDGE Regularization:**

- **Core Idea:** Both LASSO and Ridge add a penalty term to the loss function to control model complexity and prevent overfitting.
- **Ridge (L2):** Penalty = $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} w_j^2$. Shrinks coefficients towards zero but **never to zero**. Has a **closed-form solution**: $\hat{\mathbf{w}} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$.
- **LASSO (L1):** Penalty = $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert w_j \vert$. Produces **sparse models** by setting small coefficients **exactly to zero**. Solved iteratively using **coordinate descent** or **LARS**.
- **Soft-Thresholding (LASSO):** $w_j = \text{sign}(w_j^{OLS}) \cdot \max(0, \ \vert w_j^{OLS} \vert - \lambda/2)$.
- **Weight Decay View of Ridge:** Gradient update $w_j \leftarrow (1 - 2\eta\lambda)w_j + 2\eta x_j e$ — equivalent to multiplicative shrinkage.
- **Hyperparameter $\lambda$:**
  - $\lambda = 0$ → OLS (no regularization, possible overfitting)
  - $\lambda \to \infty$ → All weights → 0 (severe underfitting)
  - Optimal $\lambda$ chosen by **k-fold cross-validation**.
- **Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** Regularization increases bias slightly but reduces variance more, yielding lower test MSE.
- **Numerical Stability:** $\lambda \mathbf{I}$ in Ridge's closed form makes the matrix invertible even when $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ is singular (e.g., $p > n$ or perfect multicollinearity).
- **Geometric View:** Ridge constraint = sphere, LASSO constraint = diamond. Diamond corners (on axes) cause sparsity.
- **Elastic Net:** Combines L1 and L2 penalties — $\lambda_1 \sum \vert w_j \vert + \lambda_2 \sum w_j^2$ — ideal for correlated groups of features.
- **When to Use What:**
  - Use **Ridge** when features are correlated and most contribute to the prediction.
  - Use **LASSO** when only a few features are truly relevant (sparse truth).
  - Use **Elastic Net** when features are correlated AND you want feature selection.
- **Differentiation Property:** L2 is differentiable everywhere; L1 is non-differentiable at zero (requires subgradients).
- **Standardization Required:** Always **standardize features** (mean = 0, std = 1) before applying LASSO or Ridge, otherwise features with larger scales will be unfairly penalized less.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Idea of Training, Testing, Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT602 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Idea of Training, Testing, Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In any **supervised Machine Learning (ML) workflow**, the available labelled dataset $D$ of size $N$ is partitioned into three disjoint subsets. Each subset plays a *distinct, non-overlapping* role in the learning pipeline:

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Training Set ($D_{train}$):** The portion of data used to *fit* the model parameters (i.e., to learn the mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$ by minimizing a loss function $\mathcal{L}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Validation Set ($D_{val}$):** The portion of data used to *tune* the **hyperparameters** of the model (learning rate $\eta$, regularisation strength $\lambda$, depth of tree, number of hidden units, etc.) and to perform model selection.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Testing Set ($D_{test}$):** A *completely held-out*, unseen portion of data used to estimate the **generalisation error** of the *final* chosen model after all training and tuning is complete.

Formally, if $N$ is the total number of samples:

$$D = D_{train} \cup D_{val} \cup D_{test}, \quad D_{train} \cap D_{val} = D_{val} \cap D_{test} = D_{train} \cap D_{test} = \emptyset$$

And the cardinalities obey:

$$\vert D_{train} \vert + \vert D_{val} \vert + \vert D_{test} \vert = N$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Student Preparing for an Exam"

Think of the ML model as a **student**, and the data as **study material**.

| Real-world Analogy | ML Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Textbook chapters & class notes** used to actually *learn* the subject | **Training Set** — model learns weights $\theta$ from this |
| **Mock tests / sample papers** used to judge readiness and decide how much to revise | **Validation Set** — used for hyperparameter tuning & model selection |
| **The final university exam** — a paper the student has *never seen* before | **Testing Set** — used to estimate true, unbiased performance |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Takeaway:** If a student "memorises" the mock test answers (overfits on the validation set), the *final* exam score will suffer. This is why the **test set must NEVER be used for any form of tuning** — it must remain strictly unseen until the very end.

### 1.3 Why Three Sets? Why Not Just Two?

A common KTU question is: *"Why do we need a separate validation set when we already have a test set?"*

The answer is rooted in the **information leak** problem:

- If we use $D_{test}$ repeatedly to choose between models, we *implicitly* learn something about $D_{test}$.
- The reported test accuracy then becomes **optimistically biased**.
- The validation set acts as a "**sacrificial**" set — we *allow* information leakage into it, but we keep $D_{test}$ pristine for the final unbiased report.
- **Industry standard split:** **70% Train / 15% Validation / 15% Test** (or 60/20/20 for smaller datasets).

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly treat "validation" and "test" as synonyms. They are **not the same**. Validation = tuning. Test = final unbiased reporting. This is a guaranteed 2-mark penalty in KTU ESE answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Pipeline — Step by Step

1. **Data Acquisition:** Collect labelled dataset $D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
2. **Random Shuffling & Splitting:** Partition $D$ into $D_{train}$, $D_{val}$, $D_{test}$ using a deterministic or random seed for **reproducibility**.
3. **Model Hypothesis Selection:** Choose a family of models $\mathcal{H}$ (linear, polynomial, neural network, decision tree, etc.).
4. **Training Phase:** Minimise the empirical risk on $D_{train}$:
$$\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} \frac{1}{\vert D_{train} \vert} \sum_{(x_i, y_i) \in D_{train}} \mathcal{L}\big(f_\theta(x_i), y_i\big)$$
5. **Validation Phase:** Evaluate each candidate model (with different hyperparameters) on $D_{val}$. Select the configuration that minimises validation error.
6. **Final Retraining (optional but recommended):** Retrain the chosen model on $D_{train} \cup D_{val}$ to leverage the extra data.
7. **Testing Phase:** Report the **generalisation error** on $D_{test}$ *only once*. Lock the result.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 2 (Random Shuffling):** Prevents chronological or class-based bias. Without shuffling, if data is sorted by label, the test set may contain only one class.
- **Step 4 (Empirical Risk Minimisation — ERM):** The foundational principle of statistical learning. We approximate the true risk $R(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P}[\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y)]$ by the empirical average over $D_{train}$.
- **Step 5 (Validation):** Estimates the **generalisation gap** — how much the model is overfitting.
- **Step 6 (Retraining):** After committing to a hyperparameter set, validation data is no longer "needed" for selection, so we can use it for training to get a slightly stronger final model.
- **Step 7 (Lock Test Set):** Once reported, the test error becomes the **unbiased estimator** of true model performance.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> The following table is the **must-memorise** cheat sheet for any KTU ESE question on this topic. Reproducing these equations accurately fetches full marks.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Empirical Risk (Training Error) | $\hat{R}_{train}(\theta) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)$ | Measures fit on data the model has seen |
| 2 | Generalisation Error (True Risk) | $R(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim P}[\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y)]$ | True performance on the data distribution |
| 3 | Generalisation Gap | $\text{Gap} = R(\theta) - \hat{R}_{train}(\theta)$ | Detects overfitting |
| 4 | Bias²-Variance Decomposition | $\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Diagnoses model behaviour |
| 5 | K-Fold CV Error Estimate | $E_{CV} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} E_k$ | Replaces the need for a fixed validation set |
| 6 | Leave-One-Out CV (LOOCV) | $E_{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(f_{-i}(x_i), y_i)$ | Special case of K-Fold with $K=N$ |
| 7 | Standard Hold-out Split Ratio | $\vert D_{train} \vert \approx 0.7N, \quad \vert D_{val} \vert \approx 0.15N, \quad \vert D_{test} \vert \approx 0.15N$ | Default for medium-to-large datasets |
| 8 | Stratified Sampling Constraint | $P_{train}(y=c) = P_{val}(y=c) = P_{test}(y=c) = P(y=c)$ | Preserves class distribution (critical for imbalanced data) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare Diagnostics:** Validation set is used to tune the threshold of a tumour classifier; the test set mimics deployment on *new hospital data*.
- **Autonomous Driving:** The training set is millions of driven miles; the validation set is a *curated* set of edge cases (rain, night, jaywalkers); the test set is a closed-course evaluation never shown to engineers.
- **Recommendation Systems (Netflix, YouTube):** Training = historical watch logs; Validation = A/B test on a small user cohort; Test = business KPI (watch time, retention).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Generalisation Gap Bound (PAC-Learning Style)

We want to show, with high probability, that the generalisation error $R(\theta)$ is close to the empirical training error $\hat{R}_{train}(\theta)$. This is the **cornerstone theorem** KTU examiners love.

**Starting Point:** By the **Hoeffding's Inequality**, for any single hypothesis $h$ and $n$ i.i.d. samples:

$$P\left(\vert R(h) - \hat{R}_{train}(h) \vert \geq \epsilon \right) \leq 2 \exp\left(-2n\epsilon^2\right)$$

**Step 1:** Define the failure probability $\delta$ as:

$$\delta = 2 \exp\left(-2n\epsilon^2\right)$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $\epsilon$ by taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln \delta = \ln 2 - 2n\epsilon^2$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate $\epsilon^2$:

$$2n\epsilon^2 = \ln 2 - \ln \delta = \ln\left(\frac{2}{\delta}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Divide by $2n$ and take the square root:

$$\epsilon = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2n}\ln\left(\frac{2}{\delta}\right)}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back into the original inequality to obtain the **Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) Generalisation Bound**:

$$R(h) \leq \hat{R}_{train}(h) + \sqrt{\frac{1}{2n}\ln\left(\frac{2}{\delta}\right)}$$

**Interpretation:** With probability at least $1 - \delta$, the true error is bounded above by the training error plus a *complexity term* that **shrinks as $n$ grows**. This is *why* more data helps generalisation.

> [!NOTE]  
> The square-root term is the **confidence interval** — it quantifies the "**trust gap**" between training performance and real-world performance, which is exactly what the validation set empirically estimates.

### 3.2 Derivation of K-Fold Cross-Validation Error

When data is scarce, a single train/validation split has high **variance** in its error estimate. K-Fold CV is the standard remedy.

**Step 1:** Partition $D$ of size $N$ into $K$ *equal-sized*, *non-overlapping* folds: $D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_K$, where each $\vert D_k \vert \approx N/K$.

**Step 2:** For each fold $k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$:
- Train the model on $D \setminus D_k$ (the union of the remaining $K-1$ folds).
- Compute the error $E_k$ on the held-out fold $D_k$:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{\vert D_k \vert}\sum_{(x_i, y_i) \in D_k} \mathcal{L}\big(f_{\theta_k}(x_i), y_i\big)$$

**Step 3:** Aggregate the $K$ errors by simple averaging:

$$E_{CV}^{(K)} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} E_k$$

**Step 4:** The **standard error** of the CV estimate is:

$$SE_{CV} = \frac{\sigma_E}{\sqrt{K}}$$

where $\sigma_E$ is the standard deviation of the per-fold errors $\{E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_K\}$.

**Step 5:** Special case — **LOOCV** when $K = N$:

$$E_{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\big(f_{\theta_{-i}}(x_i), y_i\big)$$

**Trade-off Summary (KTU must-know):**

| $K$ Value | Bias of Estimate | Variance of Estimate | Computation Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| $K = 2$ (Hold-out) | High | High | Very Low |
| $K = 5$ or $K = 10$ | Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| $K = N$ (LOOCV) | Low (almost unbiased) | High | Very High ($N$ trainings) |

### 3.3 Derivation of Bias² + Variance + Irreducible Error

Let the true relationship be $y = f(x) + \epsilon$ where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, and let $\hat{f}(x)$ be the model's prediction. The expected squared error at point $x$ is:

**Step 1:** Write the squared loss:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \mathbb{E}\left[(f(x) + \epsilon - \hat{f}(x))^2\right]$$

**Step 2:** Add and subtract $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$ inside the bracket:

$$= \mathbb{E}\left[\big((f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]) + (\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x)) + \epsilon\big)^2\right]$$

**Step 3:** Expand the trinomial squared. The cross-terms vanish because $\mathbb{E}[\epsilon] = 0$ and $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$ (deterministic constant w.r.t. the randomness of $\hat{f}$):

$$= (f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2 + \mathbb{E}[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2] + \mathbb{E}[\epsilon^2]$$

**Step 4:** Identify the three terms:

$$\underbrace{(f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Error}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering Insight:** The validation error is an empirical proxy for the **Bias² + Variance** sum. The test error includes the irreducible $\sigma^2$ as well. The **training-validation gap** is the variance; the **validation error itself** reflects the bias.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram: The ML Data Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how a single dataset $D$ is split and routed through the three phases of the ML workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Labelled Dataset D of size N] --> B[Random Shuffling with Fixed Seed]
    B --> C{Train / Validation / Test Split}
    C --> D1[Training Set D_train 70 percent]
    C --> D2[Validation Set D_val 15 percent]
    C --> D3[Test Set D_test 15 percent LOCKED]
    D1 --> E1[Model Training ERM Minimisation]
    E1 --> F1[Trained Model Candidate 1]
    E1 --> F2[Trained Model Candidate 2]
    E1 --> F3[Trained Model Candidate K]
    F1 --> G[Hyperparameter Tuning on D_val]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Best Model Selected]
    H --> I[Optional Retrain on D_train plus D_val]
    I --> J[Final Unbiased Evaluation on D_test]
    J --> K[Reported Generalisation Error]
    D3 -. NEVER TOUCHED .-> J
```

### 4.2 K-Fold Cross-Validation Schematic (K = 5)

The following diagram illustrates the cyclic rotation of validation folds across 5 iterations, where every sample eventually becomes part of the validation set exactly once.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph iteration1["Iteration k = 1"]
        T1A[Train Fold 2]
        T1B[Train Fold 3]
        T1C[Train Fold 4]
        T1D[Train Fold 5]
        V1[Val Fold 1]
    end
    subgraph iteration2["Iteration k = 2"]
        T2A[Train Fold 1]
        T2B[Train Fold 3]
        T2C[Train Fold 4]
        T2D[Train Fold 5]
        V2[Val Fold 2]
    end
    subgraph iteration3["Iteration k = 3"]
        T3A[Train Fold 1]
        T3B[Train Fold 2]
        T3C[Train Fold 4]
        T3D[Train Fold 5]
        V3[Val Fold 3]
    end
    subgraph iteration4["Iteration k = 4"]
        T4A[Train Fold 1]
        T4B[Train Fold 2]
        T4C[Train Fold 3]
        T4D[Train Fold 5]
        V4[Val Fold 4]
    end
    subgraph iteration5["Iteration k = 5"]
        T5A[Train Fold 1]
        T5B[Train Fold 2]
        T5C[Train Fold 3]
        T5D[Train Fold 4]
        V5[Val Fold 5]
    end
    iteration1 --> R[Average all 5 errors]
    iteration2 --> R
    iteration3 --> R
    iteration4 --> R
    iteration5 --> R
    R --> OUT[E_CV is the final estimate]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow: When to Use Which Validation Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Choose Validation Strategy] --> Q1{Is dataset size N > 100000?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> S1[Use Simple 70 / 15 / 15 Hold-out]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is N between 1000 and 100000?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> S2[Use 5 fold or 10 fold Cross Validation]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is N less than 1000?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> S3[Use Leave One Out CV or Repeated K Fold]
    S1 --> E[End]
    S2 --> E
    S3 --> E
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading Guide for KTU Exams:** When a question says *"small dataset"*, the expected answer involves **K-Fold CV** (typically $K = 5$ or $K = 10$). When it says *"large dataset"*, the expected answer is a **simple hold-out** with three explicit splits.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
*Distinguish between the training set, validation set, and test set in a machine learning workflow. Why is a separate validation set necessary when we already have a test set?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Training set:** Used to fit the model parameters $\theta$ by minimising the empirical loss $\hat{R}_{train}$. **(1 mark)**
- **Validation set:** Used to tune *hyperparameters* (learning rate, regularisation strength, model depth) and to perform *model selection* among candidate hypotheses. **(1 mark)**
- **Test set:** A strictly held-out subset used **only once** at the end to estimate the unbiased generalisation error $R(\theta)$.
- **Why separate?** If the test set is repeatedly used for model selection, the reported test accuracy becomes *optimistically biased* (information leakage). The validation set acts as a "sacrificial" set that absorbs the leakage, keeping the test estimate honest. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper 1]**
*What is K-Fold Cross-Validation? Mention the role of the parameter $K$ and the bias-variance implication of choosing $K = 5$ versus $K = N$.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition:** K-Fold CV partitions the dataset $D$ into $K$ equal folds. The model is trained $K$ times, each time holding out a different fold for validation, and the final error is the average of the $K$ fold errors. **(1 mark)**
- **Role of $K$:** $K$ controls the *size* of the training-vs-validation split in each iteration. **(0.5 marks)**
- **$K = 5$:** Moderate bias, low variance, computationally cheap. Standard default. **(0.5 marks)**
- **$K = N$ (LOOCV):** Almost unbiased (trains on $N-1$ samples each time) but **high variance** because the training sets are nearly identical, leading to highly correlated models. Also computationally expensive ($N$ trainings). **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule (Module 2 — Internal Choice):** *Answer any **ONE** full question from the choice given. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

#### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the role of training, validation, and test sets in a supervised machine learning pipeline. Discuss why the test set must remain strictly unseen until the final evaluation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine learning engineer has a dataset of $N = 12{,}000$ samples for a binary classification task. She uses a 5-Fold Cross-Validation procedure on a Decision Tree classifier. The per-fold accuracies obtained are:

| Fold $k$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy $A_k$ | 0.91 | 0.88 | 0.93 | 0.90 | 0.93 |

Compute the **mean cross-validation accuracy** and the **standard error (SE)**. Based on this, would you recommend deploying this model? Justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define the **empirical risk** and the **true risk** of a hypothesis. Using Hoeffding's inequality, derive the PAC-style generalisation bound:

$$R(h) \leq \hat{R}_{train}(h) + \sqrt{\frac{1}{2n}\ln\left(\frac{2}{\delta}\right)}$$

State clearly what each symbol represents and explain why this bound justifies the use of a **validation set**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Hold-out method**, **K-Fold Cross-Validation**, and **Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation** with respect to: (i) bias of error estimate, (ii) variance of error estimate, (iii) computational cost, and (iv) suitability for small versus large datasets. Present your answer in a structured tabular form. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solutions for Part B

#### Solution to Q.A:

**(a) — 7 Marks:**

The standard data pipeline proceeds as follows:

- **Training Set:** Used to learn the model parameters $\theta$ by minimising the empirical risk $\hat{R}_{train}(\theta)$. The model "sees" these examples and adjusts its weights. **(1 mark)**
- **Validation Set:** Used to evaluate model performance *after* training, solely for the purpose of **hyperparameter tuning** (e.g., picking $K$ in KNN, depth in a tree, $\lambda$ in regularisation). **(1 mark)**
- **Test Set:** A pristine, held-out set used **exactly once** at the end. **(1 mark)**
- **Why unseen?** If the test set is used for any decision (hyperparameter selection, feature engineering, threshold tuning), it *contaminates* the estimate — the reported error underestimates the true generalisation error. This is called **data leakage** or **peeking**. **(2 marks)**
- **Block diagram:** Draw the flowchart from Section 4.1 with the three branches and the "LOCKED" annotation on the test set. **(2 marks)**

**(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** Compute the mean cross-validation accuracy:

$$\bar{A} = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{k=1}^{5} A_k = \frac{0.91 + 0.88 + 0.93 + 0.90 + 0.93}{5} = \frac{4.55}{5} = 0.910$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 2:** Compute the squared deviations from the mean:

| Fold $k$ | $A_k$ | $A_k - \bar{A}$ | $(A_k - \bar{A})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.91 | $+0.00$ | $0.0000$ |
| 2 | 0.88 | $-0.03$ | $0.0009$ |
| 3 | 0.93 | $+0.02$ | $0.0004$ |
| 4 | 0.90 | $-0.00$ | $0.0000$ |
| 5 | 0.93 | $+0.02$ | $0.0004$ |
| **Sum** | | | **$0.0017$** |

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3:** Compute the sample standard deviation:

$$\sigma_A = \sqrt{\frac{1}{K-1}\sum_{k=1}^{K}(A_k - \bar{A})^2} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0017}{4}} = \sqrt{0.000425} \approx 0.0206$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Compute the standard error of the mean:

$$SE = \frac{\sigma_A}{\sqrt{K}} = \frac{0.0206}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{0.0206}{2.236} \approx 0.0092$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Recommendation:** The 95% confidence interval is approximately $\bar{A} \pm 1.96 \times SE = 0.910 \pm 0.018$, i.e., the model accuracy lies between **0.892 and 0.928** with 95% confidence. Since the lower bound (0.892) is still comfortably above any reasonable business threshold (e.g., 0.85), the model **can be recommended for deployment** — but with a note that the validation accuracy has low variance (good sign of stability). **(1 mark)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B:

**(a) — 7 Marks:**

- **Empirical Risk** $\hat{R}_{train}(h)$: the average loss over the $n$ training samples. **(1 mark)**
- **True Risk** $R(h)$: the expected loss over the *true* data distribution $P(x, y)$. **(1 mark)**
- **Hoeffding's Inequality** statement: for any $\epsilon > 0$ and i.i.d. samples, the probability that the empirical mean deviates from the true mean by more than $\epsilon$ is bounded by $2\exp(-2n\epsilon^2)$. **(1 mark)**
- **Derivation** (follow the 5 steps from Section 3.1 of these notes): set the RHS equal to $\delta$, take logs, solve for $\epsilon$, substitute back. The final bound expresses $R(h) \leq \hat{R}_{train}(h) + \text{confidence term}$. **(2 marks)**
- **Justification for validation set:** The confidence term depends on the hypothesis class complexity. We use the validation set to **empirically estimate** this gap — when the gap is large, we know we are overfitting and need more regularisation or more data. **(2 marks)**

**(b) — 7 Marks:**

| Criterion | Hold-Out (e.g., 70/30) | K-Fold CV | LOOCV ($K = N$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Bias of estimate** | High (uses only 70% of data for training) | Moderate (uses $(K-1)/K$ of data) | Lowest (uses $N-1$ samples) |
| **(ii) Variance of estimate** | High (single split is noisy) | Low (averages over $K$ splits) | Highest (training sets are near-duplicates) |
| **(iii) Computational cost** | Very Low (1 training) | Moderate ($K$ trainings) | Very High ($N$ trainings) |
| **(iv) Best for** | Large $N$ ($> 10^5$) | Medium $N$ ($10^3$ to $10^5$) | Small $N$ ($< 10^3$), low-noise problems |

**(7 marks — 1.75 per row, with explicit justifications in full-sentence form.)**

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating "validation" with "test"** — losing 2–3 marks instantly. Validation is for *tuning*; test is for *reporting*. Spell this out.
> 2. **Forgetting to lock the test set** — your answer must explicitly state that the test set is "touched only once at the end."
> 3. **Wrong sample variance formula** — KTU expects the **unbiased** estimator $\frac{1}{K-1}$, not $\frac{1}{K}$. Using the wrong divisor costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the SE calculation** — many students compute the mean CV accuracy but forget the standard error. Full marks require both.
> 5. **Mermaid diagrams in answer sheets** — draw a **hand-drawn block diagram** with arrows; do not waste time trying to draw a mermaid in the exam.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The dataset $D$ is split into **three disjoint** parts: $D_{train}$, $D_{val}$, $D_{test}$.
- ✅ **Training set** = learn parameters $\theta$ via **Empirical Risk Minimisation (ERM)**.
- ✅ **Validation set** = tune **hyperparameters** and select between competing models.
- ✅ **Test set** = strictly held out, used **only once** for unbiased final reporting.
- ✅ Standard split ratio is **70 / 15 / 15** for large datasets; for small datasets, use **K-Fold CV** ($K = 5$ or $K = 10$).
- ✅ The **PAC generalisation bound** is $R(h) \leq \hat{R}_{train}(h) + \sqrt{\frac{1}{2n}\ln(2/\delta)}$ — derived from **Hoeffding's Inequality**.
- ✅ **K-Fold CV error**: $E_{CV} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} E_k$ with standard error $SE = \sigma_E / \sqrt{K}$.
- ✅ **LOOCV** is the $K = N$ special case — minimum bias, maximum variance, maximum cost.
- ✅ **Bias² + Variance + Irreducible Error** decomposition is the theoretical foundation for understanding *why* a model overfits or underfits.
- ✅ **Stratified sampling** preserves class distribution — critical for imbalanced datasets.
- ✅ **Reproducibility:** Always use a **fixed random seed** when splitting data.
- ✅ Final, golden rule: *Never let the test set influence any decision before the final evaluation — not even "just one quick check."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation Measures - Classification - Precision, Recall, Accuracy, F-Measure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT602 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Evaluation Measures - Classification - Precision, Recall, Accuracy, F-Measure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In supervised Machine Learning, once a classification model is trained, its performance on unseen data must be **quantified rigorously** rather than judged subjectively. For binary classification problems, **Evaluation Measures** are scalar metrics derived from a **Confusion Matrix** — a 2×2 contingency table that cross-tabulates the *Predicted Labels* against the *Ground Truth Labels*. The four canonical metrics assessed in the KTU 2024 PCCDT602 syllabus are:

- **Accuracy** — the fraction of total correct predictions.
- **Precision** — the quality of positive predictions (Positive Predictive Value).
- **Recall** — the coverage of actual positive cases (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate).
- **F-Measure** — the harmonic balance between Precision and Recall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The KTU 2024 scheme mandates that students must be able to *construct* a confusion matrix from raw predictions and *derive* each metric mathematically. Memorising formulas without understanding the underlying contingency table is the most common reason for losing marks.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Screening" Intuition

Imagine a **disease screening test** deployed in a hospital for a deadly illness:

- Out of **1000 patients**, only **50** actually have the disease (the *positives*).
- The test flags **70** people as diseased (its *positive predictions*).

Now ask two different questions:

1. **"Of all the people my test flagged, how many are actually sick?"**
   This is **Precision** — it tells us how trustworthy a *positive alarm* is. If the test flags 70 but only 40 are truly sick, precision is 40/70 ≈ 57%.

2. **"Of all the people who are actually sick, how many did my test catch?"**
   This is **Recall** — it tells us how *comprehensive* the test is. If 50 are truly sick but we caught only 40, recall is 40/50 = 80%.

3. **"Of all 1000 patients, how many did the test get right overall?"**
   This is **Accuracy** — including the healthy people correctly identified. If 930 healthy and 40 sick were caught correctly, accuracy is 970/1000 = 97% — sounds great, **but it hides the fact that 10 sick patients walked home undetected!**

4. **"What single number balances my alarm quality with my coverage?"**
   This is the **F-Measure** — a unified score that punishes the model if either precision or recall collapses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Accuracy alone is **misleading on imbalanced datasets** (e.g., 99% accuracy can be achieved by always predicting "no disease"). Precision and Recall expose the *asymmetric cost* of errors — a concept that is central to the KTU Module 2 assessment pattern.

---

### 1.3 The Confusion Matrix — The Foundation

Every evaluation measure in this module is built from a single 2×2 matrix:

|                  | **Actual Positive (P)** | **Actual Negative (N)** |
|------------------|-------------------------|-------------------------|
| **Predicted Positive** | **True Positive (TP)**  | **False Positive (FP)** |
| **Predicted Negative** | **False Negative (FN)** | **True Negative (TN)**  |

- **TP** — Model said "Positive", reality was "Positive". ✅
- **TN** — Model said "Negative", reality was "Negative". ✅
- **FP** — Model said "Positive", reality was "Negative". ❌ *(Type I Error — "False Alarm")*
- **FN** — Model said "Negative", reality was "Positive". ❌ *(Type II Error — "Missed Detection")*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 ESE questions, students are often given a raw table of predictions and actuals and asked to *first construct* the confusion matrix, *then* compute the metrics. Always show the matrix as part of your working — examiners award 2 marks for the matrix alone.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Construct the Confusion Matrix.**
For a binary classifier, iterate through every test instance and populate the 2×2 table based on the match (or mismatch) between the predicted class $\hat{y}_i$ and the true class $y_i$.

**Step 2 — Extract the Four Counts.**
Let the total number of test samples be $N = TP + TN + FP + FN$. These four numbers are the **only inputs** to all four evaluation measures in this topic.

**Step 3 — Compute the Ratios.**
Each metric is a *ratio* (dimensionless, ranging from 0 to 1) that captures a different *perspective* on the same matrix.

**Step 4 — Interpret Using Domain Cost.**
The choice of metric depends on whether **False Positives** or **False Negatives** are more expensive in the real-world application.

---

### 2.2 Why These Four? The "Why" Behind Each Metric

| Metric | Question It Answers | When It Matters Most |
|--------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Accuracy** | "What fraction of all predictions are correct?" | Balanced classes; equal error costs |
| **Precision** | "Of those I flagged positive, how many are truly positive?" | FP is costly (e.g., spam filter, criminal sentencing) |
| **Recall** | "Of all the actual positives, how many did I catch?" | FN is costly (e.g., cancer detection, fraud, security threats) |
| **F-Measure** | "What is a single balanced score of precision and recall?" | You need **one** number to compare/rank models |

---

### 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute value bars, such as $\vert x \vert$, are written using `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. Do **not** use the raw pipe symbol `|` inside any table cell.

| # | Metric | Formula | Range | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|--------|---------|-------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Overall correctness ratio; biased on imbalanced data |
| 2 | **Error Rate** | $\text{ERR} = 1 - \text{Acc} = \dfrac{FP + FN}{N}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Complement of accuracy; useful in safety-critical systems |
| 3 | **Precision (P)** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Reliability of a positive alert; "purity" of positive class |
| 4 | **Recall (R)** / Sensitivity / TPR | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Coverage of the positive class; "completeness" of detection |
| 5 | **Specificity (TNR)** | $\text{Spec} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Correct rejection rate of negatives; complement of FPR |
| 6 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \dfrac{2\,TP}{2\,TP + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Harmonic mean of P and R; penalises extreme imbalance |
| 7 | **General F$_\beta$-Score** | $F_\beta = \dfrac{(1+\beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Weighted balance; $\beta > 1$ favours recall, $\beta < 1$ favours precision |
| 8 | **Macro-F1** | $F_1^{\text{macro}} = \dfrac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} F_1^{(k)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Unweighted average across $K$ classes; treats all classes equally |
| 9 | **Weighted-F1** | $F_1^{\text{wt}} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot F_1^{(k)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Class-frequency weighted average; preferred for skewed data |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In production ML systems at companies like Google, Amazon, and in medical-AI startups, **F1-score is the de-facto standard** for model selection when classes are imbalanced. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can justify *why* F1 was chosen over Accuracy in a given scenario.

---

### 2.4 Why Harmonic Mean? (Theoretical Justification)

The arithmetic mean $\frac{P + R}{2}$ of two metrics can be **deceptively high** — e.g., $P = 1.0, R = 0.1$ yields an arithmetic mean of $0.55$, which sounds "okay" despite the model catching only 10% of positives. The **harmonic mean** $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$ is **small when either P or R is small**, because it is dominated by the *minimum* behaviour. This makes F1 a **conservative, balanced** single-number summary — exactly the property required by KTU 2024 problem statements that demand a "balanced performance measure".

Mathematically, the harmonic mean satisfies:

$$H(a, b) \le A(a, b) \le M(a, b)$$

where $H$ is harmonic, $A$ is arithmetic, and $M$ is the maximum — confirming $F_1$ is always the *most punishing* mean of the two.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of F1 from Precision and Recall

We begin from the definitions of Precision and Recall in terms of the confusion matrix entries.

**Step 1: Write down the definitions.**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \qquad R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the F1 formula.**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the expressions for P and R into the numerator and denominator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot \left(\frac{TP}{TP + FP}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{TP}{TP + FN}\right)}{\frac{TP}{TP + FP} + \frac{TP}{TP + FN}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Simplify the numerator.**

The numerator becomes:

$$\text{Num} = 2 \cdot \frac{TP^2}{(TP + FP)(TP + FN)}$$

**Step 5: Simplify the denominator by finding a common denominator.**

The denominator $\frac{TP}{TP + FP} + \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ has the common denominator $(TP + FP)(TP + FN)$. Adding the two fractions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Den} &= \frac{TP(TP + FN) + TP(TP + FP)}{(TP + FP)(TP + FN)} \\
&= \frac{TP^2 + TP \cdot FN + TP^2 + TP \cdot FP}{(TP + FP)(TP + FN)} \\
&= \frac{2\,TP^2 + TP(FP + FN)}{(TP + FP)(TP + FN)} \\
&= \frac{TP\,(2\,TP + FP + FN)}{(TP + FP)(TP + FN)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Divide numerator by denominator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot \dfrac{TP^2}{(TP + FP)(TP + FN)}}{\dfrac{TP\,(2\,TP + FP + FN)}{(TP + FP)(TP + FN)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The complex fractions $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)$ **cancel out** (since they appear in both numerator and denominator):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP \cdot (2\,TP + FP + FN)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Cancel one factor of $TP$ from numerator and denominator.**

$$
\boxed{F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2\,TP + FP + FN}}
$$

This is the **direct computation formula** for F1, requiring **only** the confusion matrix entries — no need to compute P and R separately. KTU examiners award full marks if you show this final boxed identity in your answer script.

---

### 3.2 General F$_\beta$ Derivation

Starting from the weighted harmonic mean:

$$F_\beta = \frac{(1 + \beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$$

For $\beta = 1$: $(1+1) = 2$, recovering the standard F1.

For $\beta = 2$ (recall-weighted): a model with low recall is penalised twice as hard as a model with low precision — used in **medical diagnosis** where missing a patient is twice as bad as a false alarm.

For $\beta = 0.5$ (precision-weighted): a model with low precision is penalised more — used in **spam filters** where we cannot afford to block legitimate emails.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A binary classifier is evaluated on a test set. The confusion matrix produced is:

|                  | **Actual Positive** | **Actual Negative** |
|------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Predicted Positive** | $TP = 50$           | $FP = 10$           |
| **Predicted Negative** | $FN = 5$            | $TN = 100$          |

**Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1.**

**Step 1: Total samples.**

$$N = TP + TN + FP + FN = 50 + 100 + 10 + 5 = 165$$

**Step 2: Accuracy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{50 + 100}{165} = \frac{150}{165} = \frac{10}{11} \approx 0.9091
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Precision.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.8333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Recall.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{50}{50 + 5} = \frac{50}{55} = \frac{10}{11} \approx 0.9091
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: F1 — using the P,R definition (verification).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.8333 \times 0.9091}{0.8333 + 0.9091}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute numerator and denominator numerically.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Num} &= 2 \times 0.8333 \times 0.9091 = 2 \times 0.7575 = 1.5150 \\
\text{Den} &= 0.8333 + 0.9091 = 1.7424 \\
F_1 &= \frac{1.5150}{1.7424} \approx 0.8696
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Verify using the direct formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2\,TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \times 50}{2 \times 50 + 10 + 5} = \frac{100}{115} = \frac{20}{23} \approx 0.8696
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **Both methods agree** ($0.8696$), confirming the derivation in Section 3.1 is correct.

**Final Summary Table:**

| Metric | Value | Percentage |
|--------|-------|------------|
| Accuracy | $0.9091$ | $90.91\%$ |
| Precision | $0.8333$ | $83.33\%$ |
| Recall | $0.9091$ | $90.91\%$ |
| F1-Score | $0.8696$ | $86.96\%$ |

**Interpretation:** The model is **slightly biased toward Recall** (catches 91% of positives) at the cost of lower Precision (83% of its positive alarms are correct). The F1 of 0.87 reflects this *balanced* trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Confusion Matrix Block Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart visualises how a single test instance is routed into one of the four cells of the confusion matrix, and how these cells aggregate into the four evaluation metrics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Test Instance x_i]) --> Q1{Predicted Class}
    Q1 -- Positive --> Q2{Actual Class}
    Q1 -- Negative --> Q3{Actual Class}
    Q2 -- Positive --> TP[True Positive TP]
    Q2 -- Negative --> FP[False Positive FP]
    Q3 -- Positive --> FN[False Negative FN]
    Q3 -- Negative --> TN[True Negative TN]
    TP --> Agg[Aggregate Counts]
    FP --> Agg
    FN --> Agg
    TN --> Agg
    Agg --> M1[Accuracy]
    Agg --> M2[Precision P]
    Agg --> M3[Recall R]
    Agg --> M4[F1 Score]
    M1 --> Out([Final Evaluation Report])
    M2 --> Out
    M3 --> Out
    M4 --> Out
```

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Metric Selection Decision Tree

This diagram guides the student in choosing **which metric to report** based on the dataset and application characteristics — a frequently tested concept in KTU 2024 ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root{Is the dataset balanced?}
    Root -- Yes --> R1{Is error cost symmetric?}
    Root -- No --> R3[Use F1 Score or PR AUC]
    R1 -- Yes --> A1[Report Accuracy]
    R1 -- No --> A2{Which error is costlier?}
    A2 -- False Positive --> A3[Report Precision]
    A2 -- False Negative --> A4[Report Recall]
    A3 --> Fbeta{F_beta Needed?}
    A4 --> Fbeta
    Fbeta -- Yes --> Fb1[Choose beta less than 1]
    Fbeta -- No --> F1out[Report F1 Score]
    Fb1 --> FbOut[Emphasize Precision]
    R3 --> Multi{More than 2 classes?}
    Multi -- Yes --> Macro[Use Macro or Weighted F1]
    Multi -- No --> F1out
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Precision–Recall Trade-off Block Schematic

The fundamental tension between Precision and Recall is the heart of KTU 2024 Module 2 questions. The following block diagram illustrates the *asymmetric trade-off* mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Threshold[Decision Threshold]
        T[Threshold t in 0 to 1]
    end
    T -- Increase t --> High[Higher Precision Lower Recall]
    T -- Decrease t --> Low[Lower Precision Higher Recall]
    High --> F1a[Single F1 optimum may shift]
    Low --> F1a
```
