```mermaid
    F1a --> Final[Optimal operating point]
    style High fill:#fde2e2
    style Low fill:#dceefb
    style Final fill:#d8f3dc
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Raising the threshold** $\tau$ makes the model *stingier* with positive predictions → fewer FP, possibly more FN → **Precision ↑, Recall ↓**.
- **Lowering the threshold** makes the model *more permissive* → more TP captured, but also more FP → **Recall ↑, Precision ↓**.
- The **F1-Score** peaks at the threshold $\tau^*$ that optimally balances both — the *operating point* chosen for deployment.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`**
*Define Precision and Recall. Why is Accuracy alone insufficient for evaluating classifiers on imbalanced datasets?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Precision** is defined as the ratio of correctly predicted positive observations to the total predicted positives:
$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$
- **Recall** is defined as the ratio of correctly predicted positive observations to all actual positives:
$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$
- **Accuracy insufficiency on imbalanced data:** Consider a dataset with 95% negatives and 5% positives. A trivial classifier that *always* predicts "Negative" achieves 95% accuracy — yet it has **0% Recall** and is useless in practice (e.g., cancer detection). Accuracy is dominated by the majority class and *hides* the model's failure on the minority class. (1 mark for the example)

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
*State the formula for the F1-Score. Show that it is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- Formula: $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \dfrac{2\,TP}{2\,TP + FP + FN}$ (1 mark)
- **Harmonic mean proof:** The harmonic mean of two numbers $a, b$ is $H(a,b) = \dfrac{2ab}{a+b}$. Substituting $a = P$ and $b = R$ directly yields the F1 formula, confirming F1 is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. (2 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the four entries of a **Confusion Matrix** for a binary classifier. Using a suitable example, demonstrate how Accuracy, Precision, and Recall are computed from it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the F1-Score formula $F_1 = \dfrac{2\,TP}{2\,TP + FP + FN}$ starting from the definition $F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$. Justify why the **harmonic mean** is preferred over the arithmetic mean for combining Precision and Recall. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Confusion Matrix and Metric Computation (7 marks):**

- **TP, TN, FP, FN definitions** (2 marks):
  - **TP**: predicted positive, actually positive.
  - **TN**: predicted negative, actually negative.
  - **FP** (Type I): predicted positive, actually negative — *false alarm*.
  - **FN** (Type II): predicted positive, actually negative — *missed detection*.
- **Worked Example** (2 marks): Use the matrix $TP=50, FP=10, FN=5, TN=100$.
- **Computations** (3 marks):
$$\text{Acc} = \frac{50+100}{165} = 0.9091, \quad P = \frac{50}{60} = 0.8333, \quad R = \frac{50}{55} = 0.9091$$
- **Interpretation** (built into the explanation): Acc masks the 5 missed positives; P < R shows the model is more conservative in positive predictions.

**(b) F1 Derivation and Harmonic Mean Justification (7 marks):**

- Start with $F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$ and substitute $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$, $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ (1 mark).
- Simplify numerator: $2 \cdot \dfrac{TP^2}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}$ (1 mark).
- Simplify denominator: $\dfrac{TP(2TP + FP + FN)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}$ (2 marks).
- Cancel common factor and $TP$ to obtain $F_1 = \dfrac{2TP}{2TP+FP+FN}$ (1 mark).
- **Harmonic vs Arithmetic Mean Justification** (2 marks): Arithmetic mean $\frac{P+R}{2}$ allows $P=1, R=0$ to score 0.5 — misleading. Harmonic mean collapses to 0 whenever *either* P or R is 0, providing a *conservative* and *truthful* single-number summary. Also, $H \le A$ always, so F1 is the most punishing combination.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** For a binary classification problem on an imbalanced medical dataset (1% positive cases), discuss why **Accuracy is a misleading metric**. Which metrics are more appropriate, and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a spam-detection system with the following confusion matrix: $TP = 180, FP = 20, FN = 30, TN = 770$. Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. If the product team demands a system that *never* misclassifies a legitimate email as spam, which metric should be optimised, and what change to the model threshold would achieve this? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Accuracy on Imbalanced Data (7 marks):**

- **The trap:** With 1% positives, a "predict all negative" classifier achieves **99% Accuracy** but **0% Recall** — it identifies zero sick patients. (2 marks)
- **Why Accuracy fails:** It treats FP and FN as equally costly and weights each by class frequency, so the majority class dominates. (2 marks)
- **Better metrics:** **Precision** (signal purity of positive alerts) and **Recall** (coverage of actual positives) are class-conditional — they measure performance on the minority class directly. **F1-Score** unifies them. (2 marks)
- **Additional metric:** For highly imbalanced data, **PR-AUC** (Precision-Recall Area Under Curve) is preferred over ROC-AUC. (1 mark)

**(b) Spam-Detection Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

- **Total** $N = 180 + 20 + 30 + 770 = 1000$ (1 mark).
- **Accuracy** $= \dfrac{180 + 770}{1000} = \dfrac{950}{1000} = 0.95$ (1 mark).
- **Precision** $= \dfrac{180}{180 + 20} = \dfrac{180}{200} = 0.90$ (1 mark).
- **Recall** $= \dfrac{180}{180 + 30} = \dfrac{180}{210} = \dfrac{6}{7} \approx 0.857$ (1 mark).
- **F1-Score** $= \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.90 \cdot 0.857}{0.90 + 0.857} = \dfrac{1.5426}{1.757} \approx 0.878$ (1 mark).
- **Metric to optimise:** The constraint "*never misclassify a legitimate email as spam*" means **FP must be minimised → Maximise Precision**. To increase Precision, **raise the decision threshold** $\tau$ — the model will only flag emails as spam when it is highly confident, reducing FP at the cost of increased FN (lower Recall). (2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to draw the confusion matrix first** — KTU 2024 ESE questions often give raw `(actual, predicted)` pairs. Constructing the matrix is the *first* step; skipping it costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Precision and Recall** — Precision uses the *predicted* positive column (denominator = $TP + FP$); Recall uses the *actual* positive row (denominator = $TP + FN$). Swapping these is the most common error.
> 3. **Reporting Accuracy on imbalanced data without caveat** — Examiners deduct marks if you quote "95% accuracy" on a 1%-positive medical dataset without commenting on its inadequacy.
> 4. **Not simplifying the F1 formula** — You must show BOTH the $F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$ form *and* the simplified $\dfrac{2TP}{2TP+FP+FN}$ form. Reporting only one is considered incomplete.
> 5. **Arithmetic mean trap** — Writing $F_1 = \dfrac{P+R}{2}$ loses 1 mark immediately. Always use the harmonic mean form.
> 6. **Ignoring units and ranges** — All four metrics are dimensionless and lie in $[0, 1]$. If your calculation gives a value $> 1$ or $< 0$, an error has occurred — recheck.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The Confusion Matrix is the foundation** — every metric in this module is a ratio of its four cells ($TP, TN, FP, FN$).
- **Accuracy** $= \dfrac{TP+TN}{N}$ — simple, but **dangerous on imbalanced data**; treat with caution.
- **Precision** $= \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ — answers "*Of my positive alarms, how many are real?*"; optimise when **FP is costly**.
- **Recall** $= \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — answers "*Of all real positives, how many did I catch?*"; optimise when **FN is costly**.
- **F1-Score** $= \dfrac{2PR}{P+R} = \dfrac{2TP}{2TP+FP+FN}$ — the **harmonic mean** of P and R; use when you need a **single balanced number**.
- **F$_\beta$ generalisation:** $\beta > 1$ weights Recall more; $\beta < 1$ weights Precision more; $\beta = 1$ recovers F1.
- **Macro-F1** averages F1 across classes equally; **Weighted-F1** weights by class frequency — use Weighted-F1 for skewed multi-class problems.
- **Threshold trade-off:** Raising the decision threshold $\tau$ **increases Precision** but **decreases Recall**; lowering it does the reverse. The F1-optimal threshold is the deployment operating point.
- **For KTU 2024 ESE answers:** Always **show the confusion matrix**, **state the formula symbolically**, **substitute numerically**, and **interpret the result** in plain English. This four-part structure is what examiners look for.
- **Memorise the identities:** $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$, $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$, $F_1 = \dfrac{2TP}{2TP+FP+FN}$ — these three formulas cover ~80% of all Module 2 marks in the KTU 2024 scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (ROC), Area Under Curve (AUC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT602 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (ROC), Area Under Curve (AUC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2 — Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) & Area Under Curve (AUC)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve**
> The **ROC curve** is a two-dimensional graphical plot that illustrates the diagnostic ability of a binary classifier system as its discrimination threshold is systematically varied. It is constructed by plotting the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** on the Y-axis against the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** on the X-axis, parameterized by every possible decision threshold $t \in [0, 1]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC)**
> **AUC** is a single scalar metric that aggregates the overall performance of a classifier across **all possible thresholds**. Geometrically, it represents the area under the ROC curve in the unit square $[0,1] \times [0,1]$. Mathematically, $\text{AUC} \in [0, 1]$, where **1.0** denotes a perfect classifier and **0.5** denotes a purely random classifier.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The COVID Rapid Test

Imagine a rapid antigen test being used to screen a population for a virus. The medical regulator wants to know: *"How trustworthy is this test if I keep changing the detection cutoff?"*

- If the cutoff (threshold) is set **very low** → the test flags almost everyone as positive. It catches every sick person (high **TPR**) but also wrongly flags many healthy people (high **FPR**).
- If the cutoff is set **very high** → the test only flags the most obvious cases. It misses many sick people (low **TPR**) but rarely flags healthy ones (low **FPR**).

The **ROC curve traces the entire trade-off** between these two extremes. **AUC** then summarizes this trade-off into a single number: *"What is the probability that this test, given a random sick person and a random healthy person, will rank the sick person higher?"*

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **TPR (Sensitivity / Recall):** $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **FPR (Fall-out):** $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = 1 - \text{Specificity}$
- **TNR (Specificity):** $\text{TNR} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$
- **FNR (Miss Rate):** $\text{FNR} = 1 - \text{TPR} = \dfrac{FN}{TP + FN}$
- **AUC:** A probability score $\in [0, 1]$.
- **Diagonal Line:** A random classifier baseline where $TPR = FPR$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always state the *threshold-independence* property in your answer. Unlike **accuracy** or **F1-score**, the ROC and AUC are invariant to the choice of a single decision threshold — this is their biggest strength and is the most-scored point in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Confusion Matrix Foundation

Every ROC curve is built on the **Confusion Matrix**, a $2 \times 2$ table summarizing predictions of a binary classifier against ground truth labels $\{+1, -1\}$.

| Predicted $\downarrow$ / Actual $\rightarrow$ | Positive (+1) | Negative (-1) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Predicted Positive (+1)** | TP (True Positive) | FP (False Positive) |
| **Predicted Negative (-1)** | FN (False Negative) | TN (True Negative) |

## 2.2 The Threshold-Sweep Logic (How ROC Is Built)

A probabilistic classifier outputs a continuous **score** $s(x) \in [0, 1]$ for every instance. A threshold $t$ converts this into a hard prediction:

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } s(x) \geq t \\ -1 & \text{if } s(x) < t \end{cases}$$

**Step-by-step construction:**

1. Train a classifier and obtain $s(x_i)$ for all $N$ test samples.
2. Sort all samples in **descending order** of score.
3. For each unique score value, treat it as a candidate threshold $t$.
4. At threshold $t$, classify all points and compute $TPR(t)$ and $FPR(t)$.
5. Plot the point $(FPR(t),\ TPR(t))$ on the 2D plane.
6. Connect adjacent points in descending-threshold order to form the ROC curve.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Range | Geometric Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| True Positive Rate | $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Height of the ROC point |
| False Positive Rate | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Width of the ROC point |
| Precision | $\text{PPV} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Not directly on ROC |
| Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP+TN}{N}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Threshold-dependent |
| AUC (Trapezoidal) | $\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \tfrac{(FPR_{i+1}-FPR_i)(TPR_{i+1}+TPR_i)}{2}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Area of all trapezoids under curve |
| AUC (Probabilistic) | $P(s(x^+) > s(x^-))$ | $[0, 1]$ | Rank statistic |
| Youden's J Statistic | $J = \text{TPR} - \text{FPR}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Optimal threshold locator |
| Gini Coefficient | $G = 2 \cdot \text{AUC} - 1$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Used in credit scoring |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse **Precision-Recall (PR) curve** with the **ROC curve**. The PR curve plots *Recall* vs *Precision* and is preferred under heavy class imbalance. The ROC curve, by contrast, remains robust even when the negative class is large.

## 2.4 Why ROC and AUC Matter in Real Engineering

- **Medical Diagnostics:** ROC analysis originated during WWII radar signal detection. Today, it is the gold standard for evaluating diagnostic biomarkers (e.g., cancer screening).
- **Credit Scoring & Banking:** Banks report the **Gini coefficient** (a rescaled AUC) as a regulatory metric for loan default models.
- **Spam Filtering:** Threshold-independent ranking helps choose operating points that balance user annoyance vs missed spam.
- **Anomaly Detection in IoT:** When positive (attack) samples are extremely rare, AUC remains interpretable even with skewed class distributions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Building an ROC Curve from a Score List

**Setup:** Consider a test set of 6 samples with the following classifier scores. Class label $y = 1$ means *positive*; $y = 0$ means *negative*.

| Sample $i$ | True Label $y_i$ | Predicted Score $s_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1 | 0.95 |
| 2 | 1 | 0.85 |
| 3 | 0 | 0.70 |
| 4 | 1 | 0.55 |
| 5 | 0 | 0.40 |
| 6 | 0 | 0.20 |

Total Positives $P = 3$, Total Negatives $N = 3$.

### Step A — Sort in Descending Order of Score

| Rank $k$ | $s_{(k)}$ | $y_{(k)}$ | Cumulative TP | Cumulative FP | $TPR_k$ | $FPR_k$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.95 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1/3 | 0/3 |
| 2 | 0.85 | 1 | 2 | 0 | 2/3 | 0/3 |
| 3 | 0.70 | 0 | 2 | 1 | 2/3 | 1/3 |
| 4 | 0.55 | 1 | 3 | 1 | 3/3 | 1/3 |
| 5 | 0.40 | 0 | 3 | 2 | 3/3 | 2/3 |
| 6 | 0.20 | 0 | 3 | 3 | 3/3 | 3/3 |

The 6 ROC points are: $(0, 0.33)$, $(0, 0.67)$, $(0.33, 0.67)$, $(0.33, 1.00)$, $(0.67, 1.00)$, $(1.00, 1.00)$.

## 3.2 Derivation of AUC via the Trapezoidal Rule

For a piecewise-linear ROC curve, AUC is computed as the sum of trapezoidal areas between consecutive points $(FPR_i, TPR_i)$ and $(FPR_{i+1}, TPR_{i+1})$.

**Area of one trapezoid** with parallel vertical sides $TPR_i$ and $TPR_{i+1}$ and horizontal width $(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i)$:

$$\Delta A_i = \frac{1}{2} (TPR_i + TPR_{i+1}) \cdot (FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i)$$

**Total AUC** is the sum over all $n-1$ consecutive segments:

$$\text{AUC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \frac{(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i) \cdot (TPR_{i+1} + TPR_i)}{2}$$

**Plug in the worked example data:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{AUC} &= \Delta A_1 + \Delta A_2 + \Delta A_3 + \Delta A_4 + \Delta A_5 \\
\Delta A_1 &= \tfrac{1}{2}(0.333 + 0.667)(0 - 0) = 0 \\
\Delta A_2 &= \tfrac{1}{2}(0.667 + 0.667)(0.333 - 0) = 0.222 \\
\Delta A_3 &= \tfrac{1}{2}(0.667 + 1.000)(0.333 - 0.333) = 0 \\
\Delta A_4 &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1.000 + 1.000)(0.667 - 0.333) = 0.333 \\
\Delta A_5 &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1.000 + 1.000)(1.000 - 0.667) = 0.333 \\
\text{AUC} &= 0 + 0.222 + 0 + 0.333 + 0.333 = 0.888
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The classifier has an **AUC = 0.888**, indicating strong discriminative power.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Probabilistic Interpretation (Mann-Whitney U Statistic)

For independent sets of positive scores $S^+ = \{s^+_1, \ldots, s^+_{n_+}\}$ and negative scores $S^- = \{s^-_1, \ldots, s^-_{n_-}\}$, define the indicator of a correct ranking:

$$I_{ij} = \mathbb{1}\!\left[s^+_i > s^-_j\right] + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathbb{1}\!\left[s^+_i = s^-_j\right]$$

The **Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney** statistic is:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n_+} \sum_{j=1}^{n_-} I_{ij}$$

Dividing by the total number of positive-negative pairs normalizes this to a probability:

$$\text{AUC} = \frac{U}{n_+ \cdot n_-}$$

This proves that **AUC equals the probability that a randomly chosen positive sample is ranked higher than a randomly chosen negative sample** by the classifier — a beautiful and intuitive interpretation.

## 3.4 Optimal Threshold via Youden's Index

The threshold $t^*$ that maximizes the vertical distance between the ROC curve and the diagonal is found by maximizing:

$$J(t) = \text{TPR}(t) - \text{FPR}(t) = \text{Sensitivity}(t) + \text{Specificity}(t) - 1$$

$$t^* = \arg\max_{t} \big[\text{TPR}(t) - \text{FPR}(t)\big]$$

For the worked example above, $J$ values are $0.333,\ 0.667,\ 0.333,\ 0.667,\ 0.333,\ 0$. The maximum $J = 0.667$ occurs at $t = 0.85$ (or $t = 0.55$), giving the optimal operating point.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ROC Curve Construction Flowchart

The following Mermaid block describes the **algorithmic flow** of constructing an ROC curve from a probabilistic classifier's outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start: Trained Probabilistic Classifier]) --> collectNode[Collect Scores s sub x for all N test samples]
    collectNode --> sortNode[Sort Samples in Descending Order of Score]
    sortNode --> initNode[Initialize TP, FP, TPR, FPR Lists as Empty]
    initNode --> loopStart{For each candidate threshold t in sorted unique scores}
    loopStart -- yes --> classifyNode[Classify all samples: s x greater or equal t as Positive]
    classifyNode --> countNode[Count TP and FP at threshold t]
    countNode --> rateNode[Compute TPR sub t and FPR sub t]
    rateNode --> appendNode[Append TPR sub t and FPR sub t to lists]
    appendNode --> loopStart
    loopStart -- no --> plotNode[Plot FPR on X axis and TPR on Y axis]
    plotNode --> connectNode[Connect consecutive points in descending threshold order]
    connectNode --> diagNode[Draw diagonal y equals x as random baseline]
    diagNode --> aucNode[Compute AUC via Trapezoidal Summation]
    aucNode --> stopNode([End: ROC Curve plus AUC Score])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ROC-AUC Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawData[Raw Test Set with True Labels] --> split[Train Test Split]
    split --> train[Train Classifier Model]
    train --> prob[Generate Continuous Probability Scores]
    prob --> thrSweep[Threshold Sweep Module]
    thrSweep --> conf[Confusion Matrix Generator]
    conf --> rateCalc[Rate Calculator: TPR and FPR]
    rateCalc --> rocPlot[ROC Curve Renderer]
    rateCalc --> aucCalc[AUC Computer: Trapezoidal Rule]
    rocPlot --> compare[Multi Model Comparator]
    aucCalc --> compare
    compare --> optimal[Optimal Threshold via Youden Index]
    compare --> report[Final Evaluation Report]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual ROC Space Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    origin([Origin: 0,0 All Negative Classifier]) --> topLeft([0,1: Perfect Classifier])
    topLeft --> bottomRight([1,0: Always Wrong Classifier])
    origin --> bottomRight
    bottomRight --> topRight([1,1: All Positive Classifier])
    topLeft --> topRight
    origin -.diagonal random baseline.-> topRight
    noteBetter[Points Above Diagonal: Better than Random]:::good
    noteWorse[Points Below Diagonal: Worse than Random]:::bad
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Class definitions used for visual styling:**
> `noteBetter` and `noteWorse` are conceptual labels attached to the regions above and below the random baseline. The shaded upper-left triangle represents the region of *useful classifiers*; the lower-right triangle represents *anti-learners* (which can be inverted to recover a useful model).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve. Explain the significance of the Area Under the Curve (AUC) with reference to a random classifier and a perfect classifier.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** The **ROC curve** is a 2D plot of **True Positive Rate (TPR)** on the Y-axis versus **False Positive Rate (FPR)** on the X-axis, generated by varying the decision threshold of a binary classifier across all possible values.
- **(1 Mark)** The **AUC** is a scalar measure between 0 and 1 that quantifies the entire two-dimensional area underneath the ROC curve.
- **(1 Mark)** A **perfect classifier** achieves AUC = **1.0** (curve passes through point (0, 1)). A **random classifier** produces a straight diagonal $y = x$ line with AUC = **0.5**. Any classifier with AUC > 0.5 performs better than random chance.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List and define the four entries of a Confusion Matrix. Hence derive the formula for TPR and FPR in terms of these entries.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** The four entries are: **TP** (correctly predicted positives), **TN** (correctly predicted negatives), **FP** (negatives wrongly classified as positive — *Type I error*), and **FN** (positives wrongly classified as negative — *Type II error*).
- **(1 Mark)** **TPR (Sensitivity):** the fraction of actual positives correctly identified,

$$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

- **(1 Mark)** **FPR (Fall-out):** the fraction of actual negatives incorrectly flagged,

$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = 1 - \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = 1 - \text{Specificity}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ Forgetting to mention the **threshold-independence** property of ROC and AUC (–2 marks).
> - ❌ Confusing **Precision-Recall curves** with **ROC curves** (–2 marks).
> - ❌ Failing to state the **diagonal baseline** at AUC = 0.5 (–1 mark).
> - ❌ Plotting ROC without sorting samples in **descending score order** (–1 mark).
> - ❌ Using **Accuracy** as a substitute for AUC in imbalanced datasets (–2 marks).

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the construction of the ROC curve for a binary classifier. Discuss the significance of the points (0, 0), (1, 1), and (0, 1) on the ROC space. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A binary classifier is evaluated on 8 samples. The scores and true labels are given below. Construct the ROC curve and compute the AUC using the trapezoidal rule. Also find the optimal threshold using Youden's J statistic. **(7 Marks)**

| Sample | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Score | 0.92 | 0.81 | 0.74 | 0.66 | 0.55 | 0.40 | 0.25 | 0.10 |
| Label | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

- **(2 Marks)** A probabilistic classifier outputs a continuous score $s(x) \in [0,1]$. As the decision threshold $t$ is swept from $1 \to 0$, each value of $t$ produces a confusion matrix and hence a $(FPR(t), TPR(t))$ coordinate. Connecting these coordinates in descending-threshold order yields the ROC curve.
- **(2 Marks) Interpretation of points:**
  - **(0, 0):** Threshold is so high that no sample is classified as positive → $TP = FP = 0$, so $TPR = 0$, $FPR = 0$. Classifier predicts *everything* as negative.
  - **(1, 1):** Threshold is so low that every sample is classified as positive → all actuals are captured but also all negatives are flagged. $TPR = 1$, $FPR = 1$.
  - **(0, 1):** **Perfect classification** point. $FPR = 0$ means no false alarms; $TPR = 1$ means every true positive is caught.
- **(2 Marks)** The **diagonal line $y = x$** represents a random classifier. The further the ROC curve pushes towards the top-left corner (0, 1), the better the model. The **steepness** of the initial rise indicates how quickly the classifier achieves high sensitivity without sacrificing specificity.
- **(1 Mark)** The ROC curve is **threshold-independent**, making it robust to changes in class prior probabilities — unlike accuracy.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Sort by descending score.**

| Rank | Score | Label | Cum TP | Cum FP | TPR | FPR |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.92 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0.25 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 0.81 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0.25 | 0.25 |
| 3 | 0.74 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 0.50 | 0.25 |
| 4 | 0.66 | 0 | 2 | 2 | 0.50 | 0.50 |
| 5 | 0.55 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 0.75 | 0.50 |
| 6 | 0.40 | 0 | 3 | 3 | 0.75 | 0.75 |
| 7 | 0.25 | 0 | 3 | 4 | 0.75 | 1.00 |
| 8 | 0.10 | 1 | 4 | 4 | 1.00 | 1.00 |

Total positives $P = 4$, Total negatives $N = 4$. The ROC points (FPR, TPR) are:
$(0, 0.25)$, $(0.25, 0.25)$, $(0.25, 0.50)$, $(0.50, 0.50)$, $(0.50, 0.75)$, $(0.75, 0.75)$, $(1.00, 0.75)$, $(1.00, 1.00)$.

**Step 2: Trapezoidal AUC computation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{AUC} &= \sum_{i=1}^{7} \tfrac{1}{2}(TPR_{i+1} + TPR_i)(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i) \\
\Delta A_1 &= \tfrac{1}{2}(0.25 + 0.25)(0.25 - 0.00) = 0.0625 \\
\Delta A_2 &= \tfrac{1}{2}(0.25 + 0.50)(0.25 - 0.25) = 0.0000 \\
\Delta A_3 &= \tfrac{1}{2}(0.50 + 0.50)(0.50 - 0.25) = 0.1250 \\
\Delta A_4 &= \tfrac{1}{2}(0.50 + 0.75)(0.50 - 0.50) = 0.0000 \\
\Delta A_5 &= \tfrac{1}{2}(0.75 + 0.75)(0.75 - 0.50) = 0.1875 \\
\Delta A_6 &= \tfrac{1}{2}(0.75 + 0.75)(1.00 - 0.75) = 0.1875 \\
\Delta A_7 &= \tfrac{1}{2}(0.75 + 1.00)(1.00 - 1.00) = 0.0000 \\
\text{AUC} &= 0.0625 + 0.0000 + 0.1250 + 0.0000 + 0.1875 + 0.1875 + 0.0000 \\
&= 0.5625
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Youden's J Statistic for Optimal Threshold.**

$$J = TPR - FPR$$

| Threshold (Score) | J |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0.92 | 0.25 − 0.00 = 0.25 |
| 0.81 | 0.25 − 0.25 = 0.00 |
| 0.74 | 0.50 − 0.25 = 0.25 |
| 0.66 | 0.50 − 0.50 = 0.00 |
| 0.55 | 0.75 − 0.50 = **0.25** |
| 0.40 | 0.75 − 0.75 = 0.00 |
| 0.25 | 0.75 − 1.00 = −0.25 |
| 0.10 | 1.00 − 1.00 = 0.00 |

The **optimal threshold** is at any of $t \in \{0.92,\ 0.74,\ 0.55\}$ where $J$ is maximum (0.25). Conventionally, the lowest such score ($t^* = 0.55$) is chosen to maximize coverage.

**Final Answer:** $\text{AUC} = \mathbf{0.5625}$ and $t^* = \mathbf{0.55}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** What is the Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC)? Explain the Mann-Whitney U interpretation of AUC and prove the equivalence between the geometric area and the rank-based probability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two classifiers A and B are evaluated on the same dataset. Classifier A gives AUC = 0.91 and Classifier B gives AUC = 0.87. In what scenarios is AUC alone insufficient for model selection? Discuss two such scenarios with justification, and mention the alternative metrics one should consider. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

- **(2 Marks)** **Definition of AUC:** The Area Under the ROC Curve is the two-dimensional integral under the ROC curve over the unit square $[0,1]^2$. Formally,

$$\text{AUC} = \int_{0}^{1} \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}) \, d(\text{FPR})$$

In practice, for a piecewise-linear ROC, this is computed via the trapezoidal sum shown earlier. AUC is a **threshold-independent** single-number summary of classifier performance.

- **(2 Marks) Mann-Whitney U Definition:** Given positive scores $S^+ = \{s_1^+, \ldots, s_{n_+}^+\}$ and negative scores $S^- = \{s_1^-, \ldots, s_{n_-}^-\}$, the Mann-Whitney U statistic counts the number of positive-negative pairs where the positive score exceeds the negative score (with half-credit for ties):

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n_+} \sum_{j=1}^{n_-} \left[ \mathbb{1}(s_i^+ > s_j^-) + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathbb{1}(s_i^+ = s_j^-) \right]$$

- **(3 Marks) Equivalence Proof Sketch:** Consider $n_+ \cdot n_-$ total positive-negative pairs. Each pair contributes:
  - **+1** to AUC area if $s^+ > s^-$ (positive ranked above negative → contributes area in the trapezoid),
  - **+0.5** if $s^+ = s^-$ (boundary case),
  - **+0** if $s^+ < s^-$ (mis-ranked → contributes nothing).

Summing these contributions across all pairs and dividing by the total pair count $n_+ \cdot n_-$ yields exactly the fraction of correctly ranked pairs, which is the **rank-probability interpretation** of AUC:

$$\text{AUC} = \frac{U}{n_+ \cdot n_-} = P(s^+ > s^-)$$

This is the **Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney** rank-sum equivalence, a foundational result in non-parametric statistics, also known as the **two-sample rank test**.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

- **(2 Marks) Scenario 1 — Class Imbalance:** AUC is computed from TPR and FPR, both of which normalize by the *true* class counts. In heavily imbalanced datasets (e.g., fraud detection with 0.1% positives), a high AUC may mask poor performance on the minority class. Two models with identical AUC = 0.90 may have drastically different **precision** values. In such cases, the **Precision-Recall AUC (PR-AUC)** and the **F1-score at the optimal threshold** are better discriminators.
- **(2 Marks) Scenario 2 — Cost-Sensitive Applications:** AUC treats all misclassifications equally. In medical diagnosis, a **False Negative** (missing a cancer case) is far more costly than a False Positive. AUC does not encode this asymmetry. Better alternatives include:
  - **Expected Cost** minimization,
  - **F-beta score** (with $\beta$ chosen to weight recall higher),
  - **Net Benefit / Decision Curve Analysis** in clinical contexts.
- **(2 Marks) Scenario 3 — Calibration Needs:** AUC measures *ranking* ability, not *probability calibration*. If downstream decisions depend on the *actual predicted probabilities* (e.g., expected loss estimation), a model with AUC = 0.87 but well-calibrated outputs (e.g., via **Platt Scaling** or **Isotonic Regression**) may be preferred over a model with AUC = 0.91 but miscalibrated scores. The relevant metric here is the **Brier Score** or **Log-Loss**.
- **(1 Mark)** **Conclusion:** AUC is a powerful **first-cut** ranking metric, but final model selection in production-grade ML must integrate AUC with **calibration metrics**, **cost-sensitive loss functions**, and **domain-specific business KPIs**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for ROC & AUC**

- ✅ The **ROC curve** plots **TPR (Y-axis)** vs **FPR (X-axis)** as the threshold is varied.
- ✅ **AUC = 1** is a perfect classifier; **AUC = 0.5** is a random classifier; **AUC < 0.5** means the model has learned the wrong pattern (just invert its predictions to recover a useful classifier with AUC > 0.5).
- ✅ AUC is **threshold-independent** and **scale-invariant** — it measures ranking quality, not absolute probability correctness.
- ✅ AUC is **invariant to class prior shifts** — if the positive class prevalence changes, the ROC curve and AUC do not change (this is a major advantage over accuracy).
- ✅ For a *perfectly separable* dataset, AUC = 1.0. For a dataset where the score distributions of positives and negatives overlap completely, AUC = 0.5.
- ✅ The **optimal operating threshold** is found by maximizing **Youden's J statistic**: $J = TPR - FPR = \text{Sensitivity} + \text{Specificity} - 1$.
- ✅ The **Mann-Whitney U statistic** provides the probabilistic interpretation: $\text{AUC} = P(\text{score of random positive} > \text{score of random negative})$.
- ✅ The **Gini Coefficient** in credit scoring is related to AUC by $G = 2 \cdot \text{AUC} - 1$.
- ✅ For **imbalanced data**, prefer **PR-AUC** (Precision-Recall AUC) over ROC-AUC because the PR curve is more sensitive to changes in the minority class.
- ✅ Always **sort samples in descending order** of predicted score before plotting — this is the single most common procedural error in KTU exams.
- ✅ The **trapezoidal rule** formula: $\text{AUC} = \sum \frac{(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i) \cdot (TPR_{i+1} + TPR_i)}{2}$ — memorize this for the 14-mark derivation question.
- ✅ Remember the four base rates: $TPR + FNR = 1$ and $TNR + FPR = 1$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression Evaluation - Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), R Squared/Coefficient of Determination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT602 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regression Evaluation - Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), R Squared/Coefficient of Determination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Regression Evaluation?

**Regression Evaluation** is the systematic process of quantifying how accurately a trained regression model predicts continuous numerical outcomes. Once a model is fitted on training data, we must mathematically certify its performance on unseen test data using standardized error and goodness-of-fit metrics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Regression Evaluation refers to a family of statistical measures that compute the deviation between the predicted values $\hat{y}_i$ generated by the model and the actual ground-truth values $y_i$ observed in the dataset. The three principal metrics mandated by the KTU Machine Learning syllabus are **MAE**, **RMSE**, and the **Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$)**.*

### 1.2 The Core Notation Vocabulary

Before diving into metrics, the following symbols are universal across all KTU Machine Learning examination questions:

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $n$ | Total number of observations (test samples) |
| $y_i$ | Actual (true) value for the $i^{th}$ observation |
| $\hat{y}_i$ | Predicted value for the $i^{th}$ observation |
| $\bar{y}$ | Mean of all actual values, $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$ |
| $\bar{\hat{y}}$ | Mean of all predicted values |

### 1.3 The Three Principal Metrics — A Bird's-Eye View

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Marksheet Grader:**
> Imagine you are a strict teacher grading 5 answer sheets. You have a **model answer key** (the actual values $y_i$) and **student responses** (the predictions $\hat{y}_i$).
> - **MAE** is like averaging the *absolute mark difference* per question. It treats every error as equally important (no bias toward outliers).
> - **RMSE** is like applying a **squared penalty** before averaging, then taking the square root. A student who loses 10 marks on one question is penalized *more than twice* as heavily as a student who loses 5 marks on two questions — it punishes **large mistakes harshly**.
> - **$R^2$** is the **overall score out of 1** that says: *"How much of the variation in the real marks is captured by your student's answers?"* A perfect 1.0 means the student nailed every trend; 0.0 means the student is no better than guessing the class average.

> [!TIP]
> **Why do we need multiple metrics?** No single metric tells the full story. **MAE** is robust, **RMSE** is sensitive, and **$R^2$** is normalized. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *compare and contrast* these three — knowing the trade-off is worth direct marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mean Absolute Error (MAE)

**MAE** computes the average of the absolute differences between the predicted and actual values. It is the most interpretable metric because it retains the same unit as the target variable.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

- For every data point, compute the **residual**: $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$
- Take the **absolute value** $\vert e_i \vert$ to eliminate sign cancellation (an error of +5 and -5 are both equally bad).
- Sum all $\vert e_i \vert$ across $n$ samples.
- Divide by $n$ to get the **mean**.

### 2.2 Mean Squared Error (MSE) and Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)

**MSE** squares the residuals before averaging. **RMSE** is simply the square root of MSE, which restores the original unit of measurement.

**Operational Logic — Why Square?**

- Squaring **amplifies large errors** disproportionately (an error of 10 contributes 100, not 10).
- Squaring makes the function **mathematically differentiable** (useful for gradient-based optimizers like gradient descent).
- The square root in RMSE brings the metric back to the same unit as $y$, making it **interpretable**.

### 2.3 Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$)

**$R^2$** is a *normalized* metric that indicates the proportion of variance in the dependent variable that is predictable from the independent variables.

**Operational Logic — The Variance Decomposition:**

- $SS_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ → **Residual Sum of Squares** (unexplained variance).
- $SS_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2$ → **Total Sum of Squares** (total variance in the data).
- A naive baseline that simply predicts $\bar{y}$ for every point would yield $SS_{tot}$ of error.
- $R^2$ measures the *fraction* of $SS_{tot}$ that the model successfully eliminated.

**Range Interpretation:**
- $R^2 = 1.0$ → Perfect model (zero residual error).
- $R^2 = 0.0$ → Model is no better than predicting the mean.
- $R^2 < 0$ → Model is *worse* than the mean baseline (a strong sign of overfitting or wrong model choice).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **highest-yield reference** for KTU 2024 ESE questions. Memorize every row.

| Metric | Mathematical Formula | Unit | Range | Penalizes Outliers? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **MAE** | $\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Same as $y$ | $[0, \infty)$ | No (linear) |
| **MSE** | $\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Squared unit of $y$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Yes (heavily) |
| **RMSE** | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | Same as $y$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Yes (heavily) |
| **$R^2$** | $1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Dimensionless (unit-less) | $(-\infty, 1]$ | Indirectly |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these metrics are used in industry:**
> - **MAE** → Used in **demand forecasting** and **inventory planning** where every unit of error is treated equally in cost.
> - **RMSE** → Used in **autonomous vehicle trajectory prediction** and **medical dosage prediction**, where large errors can be catastrophic.
> - **$R^2$** → Reported in **civil engineering structural load models** and **biostatistics** to communicate explained variance to non-technical stakeholders in a normalized 0–1 scale.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — The KTU Classic

Consider a regression model that has produced the following predictions on a test set of $n = 5$ samples:

| Sample ($i$) | Actual $y_i$ | Predicted $\hat{y}_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 3 | 2.8 |
| 2 | 5 | 5.2 |
| 3 | 7 | 6.5 |
| 4 | 9 | 8.8 |
| 5 | 11 | 10.5 |

We will exhaustively compute all three KTU-mandated metrics.

---

### 3.2 Computing the Mean of Actual Values

The mean $\bar{y}$ is a prerequisite for $R^2$.

$$\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = \frac{1}{5}(3 + 5 + 7 + 9 + 11)$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{35}{5} = 7.0$$

The mean of actual values is $\bar{y} = 7.0$.

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Computation of MAE

We first build the residual table.

| $i$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ | Residual $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | Absolute $\vert e_i \vert$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 3 | 2.8 | $+0.2$ | 0.2 |
| 2 | 5 | 5.2 | $-0.2$ | 0.2 |
| 3 | 7 | 6.5 | $+0.5$ | 0.5 |
| 4 | 9 | 8.8 | $+0.2$ | 0.2 |
| 5 | 11 | 10.5 | $+0.5$ | 0.5 |

Summing the absolute residuals:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert = 0.2 + 0.2 + 0.5 + 0.2 + 0.5 = 1.6$$

Applying the MAE formula:

$$MAE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert = \frac{1.6}{5} = 0.32$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** On average, the model's predictions deviate from the true values by **0.32 units** — since the target variable is unit-less in this example, the model is highly accurate.

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Computation of MSE and RMSE

We now square each residual.

| $i$ | $\vert e_i \vert$ | Squared Residual $e_i^2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.2 | $0.04$ |
| 2 | 0.2 | $0.04$ |
| 3 | 0.5 | $0.25$ |
| 4 | 0.2 | $0.04$ |
| 5 | 0.5 | $0.25$ |

Summing the squared residuals:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = 0.04 + 0.04 + 0.25 + 0.04 + 0.25 = 0.62$$

Applying the MSE formula:

$$MSE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \frac{0.62}{5} = 0.124$$

Applying the RMSE formula:

$$RMSE = \sqrt{MSE} = \sqrt{0.124} \approx 0.3521$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial KTU Observation:** Notice that **RMSE (0.3521) ≥ MAE (0.32)**. This is a mathematical *inequality* (Jensen's inequality) that holds in general — RMSE is always greater than or equal to MAE, and they are equal *only* when every residual is identical. KTU examiners love asking: *"Prove that RMSE ≥ MAE."*

---

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Computation of $R^2$ (Coefficient of Determination)

We need two sums of squares.

**Step A — Residual Sum of Squares ($SS_{res}$):** Already computed above as $\sum e_i^2 = 0.62$.

$$SS_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = 0.62$$

**Step B — Total Sum of Squares ($SS_{tot}$):** Compute $(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ for each $i$ using $\bar{y} = 7$.

| $i$ | $y_i$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 3 | $-4$ | 16 |
| 2 | 5 | $-2$ | 4 |
| 3 | 7 | $0$ | 0 |
| 4 | 9 | $+2$ | 4 |
| 5 | 11 | $+4$ | 16 |

$$SS_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = 16 + 4 + 0 + 4 + 16 = 40$$

**Step C — Substitute into the $R^2$ formula:**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{0.62}{40} = 1 - 0.0155 = 0.9845$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** Approximately **98.45%** of the variance in the actual values is explained by the model. This is an exceptionally strong fit.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | MAE | RMSE | $R^2$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Differentiability at 0** | No (not smooth) | Yes (smooth) | Yes (smooth) |
| **Sensitivity to outliers** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Interpretation difficulty** | Easy (same unit) | Easy (same unit) | Abstract (variance fraction) |
| **Useful in gradient descent** | Poor | Excellent | Not directly |
| **Recommended for** | Reporting to non-experts | Optimization & training | Variance explanation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Regression Evaluation Pipeline

The following block-level flow diagram illustrates the operational sequence a KTU machine learning engineer follows when evaluating any regression model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Trained Regression Model] --> B[Collect Test Dataset]
    B --> C[Run Model to Generate Predictions]
    C --> D[Compute Residuals e_i = y_i - y_hat_i]
    D --> E{Choose Evaluation Metric}
    E --> F[MAE: Average of Absolute Residuals]
    E --> G[MSE: Average of Squared Residuals]
    E --> H[RMSE: Square Root of MSE]
    E --> I[R Squared: Variance Reduction Ratio]
    F --> J[Aggregate Results into Report]
    G --> H
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Decision: Accept or Reject Model]
    K --> L[End]
```

### 4.2 Mathematical Relationship Between the Three Metrics

The following diagram clarifies the **derivation chain** between MSE, RMSE, MAE, and $R^2$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Residuals e_i] --> B[Absolute Values]
    A --> C[Squared Values]
    B --> D[MAE = Mean of Absolute Values]
    C --> E[MSE = Mean of Squared Values]
    E --> F[RMSE = Square Root of MSE]
    C --> G[SS_res = Sum of Squared Residuals]
    H[Actual Values y_i] --> I[Compute Mean y_bar]
    I --> J[SS_tot = Sum of y_i minus y_bar squared]
    G --> K[R Squared = 1 minus SS_res over SS_tot]
    J --> K
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic — Which Metric to Report?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Question: Which Metric Should I Use?] --> B{Is the dataset full of outliers?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Prefer MAE: Robust to Outliers]
    B -- No --> D{Do you need a unitless normalized score?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Prefer R Squared: 0 to 1 scale]
    D -- No --> F{Are you training via gradient descent?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Prefer RMSE: Smooth and Differentiable]
    F -- No --> H[Prefer MAE: Most Interpretable]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Architecture Insight:** In a production ML system, MAE is typically logged for stakeholder dashboards (interpretability), RMSE is used as the **training loss** for gradient-based optimizers (smoothness), and $R^2$ is reported in academic publications and regulatory submissions (normalized goodness-of-fit).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 2]**

**Q1. Define the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) for a regression model. State its formula and one key advantage over RMSE.**
*(3 Marks — Blooms: Remember/Understand — CO2)*

**Model Answer:**
Mean Absolute Error (MAE) is defined as the average of the absolute differences between the actual values $y_i$ and the predicted values $\hat{y}_i$ over $n$ test samples.

$$MAE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$$

**Key Advantage over RMSE:** MAE is **less sensitive to outliers** because it does not square the residuals — a single large error contributes linearly rather than quadratically, providing a more robust measure when the dataset contains extreme values. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. What is the Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$)? What does an $R^2$ value of 0.85 signify?**
*(3 Marks — Blooms: Understand — CO2)*

**Model Answer:**
The Coefficient of Determination, denoted $R^2$, is a normalized metric that measures the proportion of variance in the dependent variable that is explained by the regression model. It is computed as:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$$

An $R^2$ value of **0.85** signifies that **85% of the total variance** in the actual target variable is successfully captured by the model's predictions, while the remaining 15% is unexplained residual variance. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Estimation Pattern]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *MAE, RMSE, and Comparative Analysis*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]**

**Q-A (a)** Compute the MAE and RMSE for a regression model that produced the following predictions on a test set of $n=4$ samples:
Actual values: $y = [10, 20, 30, 40]$
Predicted values: $\hat{y} = [12, 18, 28, 42]$
*(7 Marks — Blooms: Apply — CO2)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Build the residual table:**

| $i$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ | $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | $\vert e_i \vert$ | $e_i^2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 10 | 12 | $-2$ | 2 | 4 |
| 2 | 20 | 18 | $+2$ | 2 | 4 |
| 3 | 30 | 28 | $+2$ | 2 | 4 |
| 4 | 40 | 42 | $-2$ | 2 | 4 |

**Step 2 — Compute MAE:**

$$MAE = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{i=1}^{4} \vert e_i \vert = \frac{1}{4}(2 + 2 + 2 + 2) = \frac{8}{4} = 2.0$$

**Step 3 — Compute MSE:**

$$MSE = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{i=1}^{4} e_i^2 = \frac{1}{4}(4 + 4 + 4 + 4) = \frac{16}{4} = 4.0$$

**Step 4 — Compute RMSE:**

$$RMSE = \sqrt{MSE} = \sqrt{4.0} = 2.0$$

**Conclusion:** MAE = **2.0**, RMSE = **2.0**. Note that MAE and RMSE are equal in this case because every residual has the same magnitude. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Q-A (b)** With a neat comparison, explain the differences between MAE and RMSE. Under what condition is RMSE strictly greater than MAE? *(7 Marks — Blooms: Analyze — CO2)*

**Model Solution:**

| Property | MAE | RMSE |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Formula | $\frac{1}{n}\sum \vert e_i \vert$ | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum e_i^2}$ |
| Penalty for outliers | Linear | Quadratic |
| Differentiability at zero | Not differentiable | Differentiable everywhere |
| Unit | Same as $y$ | Same as $y$ |
| Gradient behavior | Constant magnitude | Increases with error size |

**Condition for RMSE > MAE:** By Jensen's inequality applied to the strictly convex function $f(x) = x^2$, the square of the mean is less than or equal to the mean of squares, with strict inequality when **not all residuals are equal in magnitude**. Hence:

$$RMSE \geq MAE$$

The inequality is **strict** (RMSE > MAE) when the residuals $\vert e_i \vert$ are **not all identical** — i.e., when the dataset contains varying error magnitudes. RMSE inflates the contribution of large errors due to the squaring operation, pulling its value above the linear average. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *$R^2$, Adjusted $R^2$, and Worked Example*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified Pattern]**

**Q-B (a)** Define the Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$). Compute $R^2$ for a model with the following data: $y = [2, 4, 6, 8, 10]$, $\hat{y} = [2.5, 4.0, 5.5, 8.5, 9.5]$. *(7 Marks — Blooms: Apply — CO2)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\bar{y}$:**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{2+4+6+8+10}{5} = \frac{30}{5} = 6.0$$

**Step 2 — Build the residual and deviation table:**

| $i$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ | $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | $e_i^2$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(y_i-\bar{y})^2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 2 | 2.5 | $-0.5$ | 0.25 | $-4$ | 16 |
| 2 | 4 | 4.0 | $0.0$ | 0.00 | $-2$ | 4 |
| 3 | 6 | 5.5 | $+0.5$ | 0.25 | $0$ | 0 |
| 4 | 8 | 8.5 | $-0.5$ | 0.25 | $+2$ | 4 |
| 5 | 10 | 9.5 | $+0.5$ | 0.25 | $+4$ | 16 |

**Step 3 — Compute the sums:**

$$SS_{res} = \sum e_i^2 = 0.25 + 0 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 = 1.00$$

$$SS_{tot} = \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = 16 + 4 + 0 + 4 + 16 = 40$$

**Step 4 — Compute $R^2$:**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{1.00}{40} = 1 - 0.025 = 0.975$$

**Conclusion:** $R^2 = $ **0.975**, meaning the model explains 97.5% of the variance. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Q-B (b)** Explain the limitation of $R^2$ when additional features are added to a multiple regression model. How does Adjusted $R^2$ overcome this limitation? Provide its formula. *(7 Marks — Blooms: Analyze — CO2)*

**Model Solution:**

**Limitation of $R^2$:** The standard $R^2$ metric **never decreases** when new features (independent variables) are added to a multiple regression model, even if those features are completely irrelevant or pure noise. This is because $R^2$ is computed only from the residual sum of squares and total sum of squares — adding a feature can only *reduce* $SS_{res}$ (or leave it unchanged), artificially inflating $R^2$ and misleading the analyst into believing the model is improving.

**Adjusted $R^2$ Formula:**

$$R^2_{adj} = 1 - \left(1 - R^2\right) \cdot \frac{n-1}{n-k-1}$$

Where:
- $n$ = number of observations
- $k$ = number of independent variables (features)
- $R^2$ = standard coefficient of determination

**How it overcomes the limitation:** Adjusted $R^2$ includes a **penalty term** $\frac{n-1}{n-k-1}$ that grows as $k$ (the number of features) increases. If an added feature does not sufficiently improve the model fit, the penalty term dominates, and Adjusted $R^2$ *decreases*. Thus, Adjusted $R^2$ only increases when the new feature provides a meaningful improvement that outweighs the complexity penalty — making it a much more reliable metric for **feature selection** in multiple regression. **(7 Marks)**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value** when computing MAE — students often write $\frac{1}{n}\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)$ which yields 0 trivially due to cancellation. **Always** use $\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$.
> 2. **Confusing $SS_{res}$ with $SS_{tot}$** in the $R^2$ formula. $SS_{res}$ uses *predictions* $\hat{y}_i$, while $SS_{tot}$ uses the *mean* $\bar{y}$. Swapping them gives a nonsensical $R^2$.
> 3. **Not specifying the unit** of MAE/RMSE — KTU examiners award a half-mark for stating *"the unit is the same as the target variable."*
> 4. **Reporting negative $R^2$ as a valid score** — it is valid, but students should explicitly state that it means *"the model is worse than predicting the mean."*
> 5. **Forgetting the square root** when transitioning from MSE to RMSE — a frequent 1-mark deduction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 (Regression Evaluation):**
> - **MAE** = average of **absolute** residuals → robust to outliers, same unit as $y$, not differentiable at 0.
> - **MSE** = average of **squared** residuals → differentiable, penalizes outliers heavily, *not* interpretable in original units.
> - **RMSE** = $\sqrt{MSE}$ → same unit as $y$, differentiable, and always **≥ MAE** (Jensen's inequality).
> - **$R^2$** = $1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ → unitless, range $(-\infty, 1]$, measures explained variance.
> - **$R^2 = 1$** is perfect; **$R^2 = 0$** is equivalent to the mean baseline; **$R^2 < 0$** means the model is worse than naive guessing.
> - **Adjusted $R^2$** = $1 - (1-R^2)\cdot\frac{n-1}{n-k-1}$ → penalizes unnecessary features, used for model selection.
> - The **Total Sum of Squares** $SS_{tot}$ uses the *mean of actual values* $\bar{y}$, not the *mean of predictions*.
> - MAE is preferred for **reporting**; RMSE is preferred for **training** (gradient descent); $R^2$ is preferred for **explained variance communication**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SVM - Linear SVM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT602 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SVM - Linear SVM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Support Vector Machine (SVM) — Formal Definition**
> A **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a supervised discriminative classifier formally defined by a separating **hyperplane**. For a binary classification problem with linearly separable training data, the SVM algorithm finds the **optimal hyperplane** that maximizes the **margin** — the perpendicular distance between the hyperplane and the closest data points (called **support vectors**) from either class.

A **Linear SVM** specifically handles data that is **linearly separable** in the original feature space (no kernel trick required). It solves a **convex optimization problem** to find the maximum-margin hyperplane.

### 1.1.1 Mathematical Statement of the Linear Classifier

Given training data:

$$(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, N$$

where $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is a feature vector and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ is the class label, a linear classifier is defined as:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$

The decision rule is:

$$\hat{y} = \text{sign}(f(\mathbf{x})) = \text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)$$

where $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the **weight (normal) vector** and $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **bias term**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Road Between Two Villages" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Widest Road Between Two Crowded Cities"**

Imagine two enemy villages (Class +1 and Class -1) located on a flat plain. The houses of each village represent data points. We need to build a **straight road** (the hyperplane) that **separates the two villages**.

- A *bad* road builder draws a road that just barely passes between the villages — touching the nearest houses. This is risky: any new house (test data) built near the boundary will be misclassified.
- A *good* (SVM) road builder finds the **widest possible road** that fits between the two villages. The road is bounded by "fence lines" on either side, and the houses that **touch these fence lines** are the **support vectors** (the most critical, hardest-to-classify data points).

**Why maximize the margin?**
> Because a wider road (larger margin) is **more robust**. Tiny perturbations in new data points will still leave them on the correct side of the road, improving **generalization** on unseen test data.

| Component | Real-World Analogy | Mathematical Role |
|---|---|---|
| Road center | Decision hyperplane | $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ |
| Road edges | Margin boundaries | $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = \pm 1$ |
| Width of road | Margin | $\dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ |
| Houses touching the edge | Support vectors | Points where $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$ |
| Village label | Class | $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ |

---

## 1.3 Why Linear SVM? — Syllabus Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Points for Linear SVM**
> 1. SVM is a **maximum-margin classifier** (not minimum-error).
> 2. Only **support vectors** influence the final decision boundary — all other training points are effectively irrelevant.
> 3. The optimization is a **convex quadratic program (QP)**, guaranteeing a **global optimum**.
> 4. The solution is **sparse** in the training data (only support vectors are retained).
> 5. Linear SVM is the foundation for **kernel SVM** (covered later in the syllabus via the kernel trick).

---

## 1.4 Linearly Separable vs. Non-Separable Data

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear Separability** means there exists at least one hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ such that all positive samples lie on one side and all negative samples on the other.

- **Hard-margin Linear SVM** → used when data is *perfectly* linearly separable.
- **Soft-margin Linear SVM** → used when data is *not* perfectly separable (introduces slack variables $\xi_i$).
- **Module 3 focus** → primarily the **hard-margin** case; soft-margin is the natural extension.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometric Setup — Hyperplane, Margins, and Functional Distance

For a hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$:

1. **Geometric (Euclidean) distance** of a point $\mathbf{x}_i$ from the hyperplane:

$$d_i = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

2. **Functional distance** (signed, scale-dependent):

$$\hat{d}_i = y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b)$$

The functional distance is positive when the point is correctly classified.

### 2.1.1 The Canonical Hyperplane Form

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Canonical Form Assumption** — We scale $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ so that the points closest to the hyperplane satisfy $\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert = 1$.

Under this scaling:
- Points on the margin boundaries satisfy: $y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$
- The geometric margin becomes: $\dfrac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$
- The **total margin width** = $\dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$

---

## 2.2 The Optimization Problem — Primal Form (Hard Margin)

### 2.2.1 Maximize the Margin

To maximize the margin $\dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ is equivalent to **minimizing** $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ (squaring is monotonic for positive quantities and removes the square root).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard-Margin Linear SVM Primal Problem:**

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \quad \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$$

subject to the constraints:

$$y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall \, i = 1, 2, \dots, N$$

**Why the factor $\frac{1}{2}$?** It is a pure convention to simplify differentiation (the derivative of $\frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ is $\mathbf{w}$, not $2\mathbf{w}$).

### 2.2.2 Intuition Behind the Constraints

- $y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ means: every training point must lie **on or beyond** the margin boundary on the correct side.
- For support vectors, this becomes an **equality**: $y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$.

---

## 2.3 Lagrangian Formulation & The Dual Problem

### 2.3.1 Why Move to the Dual?

> [!NOTE]
> - The **primal** is a quadratic program in $\mathbf{w}$ (a $d$-dimensional problem).
> - The **dual** is a quadratic program in $\alpha_i$ (an $N$-dimensional problem).
> - The dual is **independent of dimension $d$** — this is what enables the **kernel trick** in non-linear SVM (Module extension).
> - The dual exposes the **support vectors** as the non-zero $\alpha_i$.

### 2.3.2 Lagrangian of the Primal

Introduce **Lagrange multipliers** $\alpha_i \geq 0$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \big[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \big]$$

### 2.3.3 KKT Conditions (Karush-Kuhn-Tucker)

For optimality:

1. **Stationarity**:
   $$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} = 0 \implies \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$
   $$\nabla_{b} \mathcal{L} = 0 \implies \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

2. **Primal feasibility**:
   $$y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$$

3. **Dual feasibility**:
   $$\alpha_i \geq 0$$

4. **Complementary slackness**:
   $$\alpha_i \big[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \big] = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Complementary slackness** is the key insight: $\alpha_i > 0 \iff$ point $i$ is a **support vector** (lies on the margin boundary). All other points have $\alpha_i = 0$ and do not affect $\mathbf{w}$.

### 2.3.4 The Dual Optimization Problem

Substitute the stationarity conditions back into $\mathcal{L}$:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard-Margin Linear SVM Dual Problem:**

$$\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \quad L_D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j)$$

subject to:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad \alpha_i \geq 0, \quad i = 1, \dots, N$$

This is a **convex QP** with a unique global solution.

---

## 2.4 The Decision Function

After solving the dual, recover:

$$\mathbf{w}^* = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i^* y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

where $SV$ is the set of support vectors. The bias $b^*$ is recovered from any support vector $\mathbf{x}_k$:

$$b^* = y_k - \mathbf{w}^{*T} \mathbf{x}_k$$

The decision function is:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i^* y_i (\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}) + b^*$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Notice:** The decision function depends on $\mathbf{x}$ only through **inner products** with support vectors. This is the gateway to the **kernel trick** for non-linear SVM.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TABLE 2.1 — Linear SVM Master Formula Reference (KTU Board Exam Ready)**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Decision rule | $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)$ | Output: $\{-1, +1\}$ |
| 2 | Hyperplane equation | $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | $d$-dimensional separator |
| 3 | Functional margin (point) | $\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b)$ | Signed, scale-dependent |
| 4 | Geometric margin (point) | $\gamma_i = \dfrac{\hat{\gamma}_i}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | True Euclidean distance |
| 5 | Total margin width | $\dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Distance between margin boundaries |
| 6 | Primal objective | $\min \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ | Subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ |
| 7 | Dual objective | $\max \sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_i\sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \mathbf{x}_i^T\mathbf{x}_j$ | Subject to $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$, $\alpha_i \geq 0$ |
| 8 | Weight vector recovery | $\mathbf{w} = \sum \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ | Sum over all $i$ |
| 9 | Bias recovery | $b = y_k - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_k$ | For any support vector $\mathbf{x}_k$ |
| 10 | Support vector condition | $\alpha_i > 0$ and $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$ | From complementary slackness |
| 11 | Number of support vectors | $\leq N$ (typically much fewer) | Sparse solution |
| 12 | Hinge loss (for soft-margin) | $\max(0, 1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b))$ | $0$ if correctly classified on margin |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of Linear SVM

> [!NOTE]
> - **Text classification** (spam detection, sentiment analysis) — high-dimensional sparse features.
> - **Bioinformatics** — gene expression classification, cancer detection.
> - **Image classification** (baseline) — particularly when features are already engineered.
> - **Fault detection** in mechanical/electrical systems.
> - **Edge deployment** — linear SVMs are computationally lightweight at inference time: $O(d)$ per prediction (just a dot product).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Maximize Margin to Minimize Norm

### Step 1: Set Up the Margin Expression

For a linear classifier with hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$, the perpendicular distance from a point $\mathbf{x}_i$ to the hyperplane is:

$$d_i = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

### Step 2: Define the Margin

The margin $M$ is the **minimum** perpendicular distance from any training point to the hyperplane:

$$M = \min_{i} \frac{\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

### Step 3: Apply Canonical Scaling

We are free to rescale $(\mathbf{w}, b) \to (k\mathbf{w}, kb)$ without changing the hyperplane. Choose $k$ such that:

$$\min_{i} \vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert = 1$$

This forces all points to satisfy $\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert \geq 1$, i.e., $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$. The margin then becomes:

$$M = \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

The **total margin width** between the two parallel margin boundaries $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = +1$ and $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = -1$ is:

$$2M = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

### Step 4: Convert Maximization to Minimization

Maximizing $\dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ is equivalent to minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$, and squaring is monotonic for non-negative quantities, so we minimize $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$. Adding the standard $\frac{1}{2}$ factor:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i$$

**Done.** ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Primal to Dual Transformation

### Step 1: Form the Lagrangian

The Lagrangian combines objective and constraints via multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \big[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \big]$$

### Step 2: Minimize $\mathcal{L}$ with Respect to $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$ (Stationarity)

**Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 0$$

Solving:

$$\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

**Differentiate w.r.t. $b$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

Solving:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

### Step 3: Substitute Back Into the Lagrangian

Substitute $\mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ into $\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{w}$:

$$\frac{1}{2} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j)$$

Substitute into the constraint summation term:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \big[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \big] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i$$

Using $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$, the middle term vanishes. The first term becomes:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \Big( \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_j y_j \mathbf{x}_j \Big)^T \mathbf{x}_i = \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j)$$

### Step 4: Combine to Get the Dual

Putting it all together:

$$\mathcal{L}_D(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j) - \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i$$

Simplify (the first two terms combine to give $-\frac{1}{2}$):

$$\mathcal{L}_D(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Dual Form:**

$$\boxed{\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j)}$$

subject to $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ and $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for all $i$.

**Done.** ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Consider 4 linearly separable 2-D training points:
> - $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1, 1), \; y_1 = +1$
> - $\mathbf{x}_2 = (2, 2), \; y_2 = +1$
> - $\mathbf{x}_3 = (0, -1), \; y_3 = -1$
> - $\mathbf{x}_4 = (-1, -1), \; y_4 = -1$
>
> Find the optimal separating hyperplane and the margin.

### Step 1: Set Up the Dual

We have $N = 4$ Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3, \alpha_4$. Compute the Gram matrix $K_{ij} = \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$:

$$K = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 4 & -1 & -2 \\ 4 & 8 & -2 & -4 \\ -1 & -2 & 1 & 1 \\ -2 & -4 & 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

The dual objective is:

$$L_D = \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K_{ij}$$

Constraint: $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - \alpha_3 - \alpha_4 = 0$.

### Step 2: Use the Geometric Insight

By symmetry of the data (points 1, 2 are positive; points 3, 4 are negative; the data is symmetric about the origin), we expect:

$$\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 =: \alpha, \quad \alpha_3 = \alpha_4 =: \beta$$

The constraint gives: $2\alpha - 2\beta = 0 \implies \alpha = \beta$.

### Step 3: Substitute and Maximize

With $y_i y_j$ terms, the objective becomes (after substitution and simplification):

$$L_D = 2\alpha - \frac{1}{2} \big[ 18 \alpha^2 \big] = 2\alpha - 9\alpha^2$$

Differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{dL_D}{d\alpha} = 2 - 18\alpha = 0 \implies \alpha = \frac{1}{9}$$

So $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \alpha_3 = \alpha_4 = \dfrac{1}{9}$.

### Step 4: Recover $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$

$$\mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = \frac{1}{9} \big[ (+1)(1,1) + (+1)(2,2) + (-1)(0,-1) + (-1)(-1,-1) \big]$$

$$\mathbf{w} = \frac{1}{9} \big[ (1,1) + (2,2) - (0,-1) - (-1,-1) \big] = \frac{1}{9} (4, 5)$$

So $w_1 = \dfrac{4}{9}, \; w_2 = \dfrac{5}{9}$.

For $b$, use any support vector. Using $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1,1)$ with $y_1 = +1$:

$$b = y_1 - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_1 = 1 - \Big( \frac{4}{9} \cdot 1 + \frac{5}{9} \cdot 1 \Big) = 1 - \frac{9}{9} = 0$$

### Step 5: Final Hyperplane and Margin

$$\boxed{\frac{4}{9} x_1 + \frac{5}{9} x_2 = 0}$$

The margin is:

$$M = \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(4/9)^2 + (5/9)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{41}/9} = \frac{9}{\sqrt{41}} \approx 1.405$$

**Done.** ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Equivalence Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TABLE 3.1 — Primal vs. Dual Variable Interpretation**

| Symbol | Domain | Role | Special Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{w}$ | $\mathbb{R}^d$ | Weight vector of hyperplane | Unique solution at optimum |
| $b$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | Bias of hyperplane | Unique solution at optimum |
| $\alpha_i$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | Lagrange multiplier for point $i$ | $\alpha_i > 0 \iff \mathbf{x}_i$ is a support vector |
| $\gamma$ (margin) | $\mathbb{R}_{>0}$ | Geometric margin | Maximized by SVM |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Linear SVM Architecture and Decision Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Training Data x_i, y_i in R^d] --> B[Linearly Separable?]
    B -- Yes --> C[Hard-Margin Linear SVM]
    B -- No --> D[Soft-Margin Linear SVM / Kernel SVM]
    C --> E[Formulate Primal: min 0.5 ||w||^2]
    E --> F[Apply Lagrangian with multipliers alpha_i]
    F --> G[KKT Stationarity Conditions]
    G --> H[Derive Dual QP in alpha]
    H --> I[Solve Dual via QP Solver]
    I --> J{Identify Support Vectors}
    J -- alpha_i greater than 0 --> K[Support Vector Set SV]
    J -- alpha_i equals 0 --> L[Non-Critical Points: Discarded]
    K --> M[Recover w star equals sum alpha_i y_i x_i]
    M --> N[Recover b star from any support vector]
    N --> O[Final Hyperplane: w^T x + b equals 0]
    O --> P[Decision Function: f of x equals sign of w^T x + b]
    P --> Q[Predict on Test Data]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Margin Geometry (2-D Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Class -1 Region: w^T x + b less than -1] --> B[Margin Boundary: w^T x + b equals -1]
    B --> C[Hyperplane: w^T x + b equals 0]
    C --> D[Margin Boundary: w^T x + b equals +1]
    D --> E[Class +1 Region: w^T x + b greater than +1]
    B -. Support Vector SV_neg .-> B
    D -. Support Vector SV_pos .-> D
    C --> F[Geometric Margin equals 1 over ||w||]
    B --> F
    D --> F
    F --> G[Total Margin Width equals 2 over ||w||]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Primal-Dual Relationship Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Primal Problem: min 0.5 ||w||^2] --> P2[Constraints: y_i w^T x_i + b greater or equal 1]
    P2 --> P3[Form Lagrangian L of w, b, alpha]
    P3 --> P4[Stationarity: dL/dw equals 0, dL/db equals 0]
    P4 --> P5[Get w equals sum alpha_i y_i x_i]
    P4 --> P6[Get sum alpha_i y_i equals 0]
    P5 --> P7[Substitute into L to eliminate w and b]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Dual Problem: max sum alpha_i minus 0.5 sum_ij alpha_i alpha_j y_i y_j x_i^T x_j]
    P8 --> P9[Solve QP: find alpha star]
    P9 --> P10[Recover w star and b star]
    P10 --> P11[Decision Function: f of x]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Support Vector Identification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[All Training Points] --> S2{Compute alpha_i from Dual QP}
    S2 -- alpha_i equals 0 --> S3[Non-Support Vectors: Irrelevant to Boundary]
    S2 -- alpha_i greater than 0 --> S4[Support Vectors: Define the Margin]
    S4 --> S5[Lies on Margin Boundary: y_i w^T x_i + b equals 1]
    S5 --> S6[Only these points determine w and b]
    S3 --> S7[Move freely within margin zone: No effect on boundary]
    S6 --> S8[Move them: Boundary shifts]
    S7 --> S8
```
