# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Support Vector Machine (SVM). What are support vectors in the context of a linear SVM?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a supervised machine learning algorithm used for binary classification. For a linearly separable dataset, it finds the **optimal separating hyperplane** that maximizes the **margin** — the perpendicular distance between the hyperplane and the nearest data points of each class.
>
> **Support vectors** are the training data points that lie **closest to the decision hyperplane**, i.e., on the margin boundaries. Mathematically, they are the points for which the Lagrange multiplier $\alpha_i > 0$, and they satisfy:
>
> $$y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$$
>
> Support vectors are the **only** training points that determine the position and orientation of the hyperplane. Removing any non-support vector does not change the solution; removing a support vector does.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**State and explain the primal optimization problem of a hard-margin linear SVM.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **primal optimization problem** of a hard-margin linear SVM is formulated as follows:
>
> $$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \quad \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$$
>
> subject to:
>
> $$y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall \, i = 1, 2, \dots, N$$
>
> **Explanation:**
> - The objective $\frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ minimizes the norm of the weight vector, which is equivalent to **maximizing the margin** $\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.
> - The constraints ensure that every training point is correctly classified and lies **at or beyond** the margin boundary.
> - This is a **convex quadratic programming (QP)** problem with linear constraints, guaranteeing a **unique global optimum**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the **dual optimization problem** of a hard-margin linear SVM starting from the primal form. Clearly state the KKT conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-D dataset with the following points: $\mathbf{x}_1 = (3, 3), y_1 = +1$; $\mathbf{x}_2 = (4, 3), y_2 = +1$; $\mathbf{x}_3 = (1, 0), y_3 = -1$; $\mathbf{x}_4 = (0, 1), y_4 = -1$, **find the optimal hyperplane** and the **margin width** by solving the dual problem. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1: Primal Form**

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$$

**Step 2: Lagrangian**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \big[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \big], \quad \alpha_i \geq 0$$

**Step 3: KKT Conditions**

(i) **Stationarity:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0 \implies \mathbf{w}^* = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0 \implies \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

(ii) **Primal feasibility:** $y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$

(iii) **Dual feasibility:** $\alpha_i \geq 0$

(iv) **Complementary slackness:** $\alpha_i \big[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \big] = 0$

**Step 4: Substitute into Lagrangian to Get Dual**

Substitute $\mathbf{w}^* = \sum \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$:

$$\mathcal{L}_D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j)$$

subject to $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$ and $\alpha_i \geq 0$.

This is the **dual problem**. **(7 marks — full marks awarded)**

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Set Up the Dual**

Compute inner products:

$$\mathbf{x}_1^T \mathbf{x}_1 = 18, \quad \mathbf{x}_2^T \mathbf{x}_2 = 25, \quad \mathbf{x}_3^T \mathbf{x}_3 = 1, \quad \mathbf{x}_4^T \mathbf{x}_4 = 1$$

$$\mathbf{x}_1^T \mathbf{x}_2 = 21, \quad \mathbf{x}_1^T \mathbf{x}_3 = 3, \quad \mathbf{x}_1^T \mathbf{x}_4 = 3, \quad \mathbf{x}_2^T \mathbf{x}_3 = 4, \quad \mathbf{x}_2^T \mathbf{x}_4 = 4, \quad \mathbf{x}_3^T \mathbf{x}_4 = 0$$

**Step 2: Symmetry Argument**

By approximate symmetry, set $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \alpha$ (positive class) and $\alpha_3 = \alpha_4 = \beta$ (negative class).

Constraint: $2\alpha - 2\beta = 0 \implies \alpha = \beta$.

**Step 3: Compute the Dual Objective**

$$L_D = (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \alpha_3 + \alpha_4) - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$$

With $\alpha = \beta$ and using $y_i y_j = +1$ for same-class pairs and $-1$ for cross-class pairs, after simplification:

$$L_D = 4\alpha - \alpha^2 (18 + 25 + 1 + 1 - 2(21) + 2(3+3) - 2(0))$$

**Step 4: Maximize**

Differentiate: $\dfrac{dL_D}{d\alpha} = 4 - 2 \cdot 18\alpha = 0$ (after collecting coefficients), giving $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{9}$.

**Step 5: Recover $\mathbf{w}$**

$$\mathbf{w} = \frac{1}{9} \big[ (3,3) + (4,3) - (1,0) - (0,1) \big] = \frac{1}{9} (6, 5)$$

**Step 6: Recover $b$**

Using $\mathbf{x}_3 = (1, 0), y_3 = -1$:

$$b = -1 - \frac{1}{9}(6 \cdot 1 + 5 \cdot 0) = -1 - \frac{6}{9} = -\frac{15}{9} = -\frac{5}{3}$$

**Step 7: Hyperplane and Margin**

$$\frac{6}{9} x_1 + \frac{5}{9} x_2 - \frac{5}{3} = 0 \implies 2x_1 + \frac{5}{3} x_2 = 5$$

Margin width: $\dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{36/81 + 25/81}} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{61}/9} = \dfrac{18}{\sqrt{61}}$ **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **concept of margin** in a linear SVM. Derive the relationship between the margin width and the weight vector $\mathbf{w}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of **support vectors** in determining the SVM decision boundary. Why are non-support vectors irrelevant? How does the **complementary slackness** condition of KKT prove this? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Concept of Margin:**

The margin is the **perpendicular distance** from the decision hyperplane to the nearest training data points of either class. It represents a "buffer zone" of empty space around the decision boundary, providing robustness against noise and improving generalization.

**Derivation of Margin Width:**

For a hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$, the perpendicular distance from any point $\mathbf{x}_i$ is:

$$d_i = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

Under **canonical scaling** (where the closest points satisfy $\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert = 1$), the closest point of each class lies at distance $\dfrac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ from the hyperplane. Hence the **total margin width** between the two parallel boundaries $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = +1$ and $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = -1$ is:

$$M_{\text{total}} = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

To **maximize** the margin, we **minimize** $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$, which is equivalent to minimizing $\frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ — the standard SVM objective. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Role of Support Vectors:**

Support vectors are the training data points that lie **on the margin boundaries** (i.e., exactly at distance $\dfrac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ from the hyperplane). They are the **critical points** of the training set — if any one of them moves, the optimal hyperplane shifts. They are the points that "support" the margin.

**Why Non-Support Vectors are Irrelevant:**

Points strictly outside the margin satisfy $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) > 1$. According to the **complementary slackness** condition of KKT:

$$\alpha_i \big[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \big] = 0$$

For a non-support vector, $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 > 0$, which forces $\alpha_i = 0$. Since:

$$\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

points with $\alpha_i = 0$ contribute **nothing** to $\mathbf{w}$. Therefore, moving them within their feasible region does not affect the decision boundary. Conversely, support vectors have $\alpha_i > 0$ and directly determine $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$.

This **sparsity property** is a major computational advantage of SVMs — prediction depends only on the support vectors, not on the entire training set. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$** in the objective — leads to a wrong dual (it is a convention, not strictly required, but inconsistency loses marks).
> 2. **Mixing up functional margin and geometric margin** — functional margin depends on scaling, geometric margin does not. SVM optimizes the geometric margin.
> 3. **Sign error in complementary slackness** — write it as $\alpha_i \geq 0$ AND $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ AND their product equals zero.
> 4. **Forgetting the equality constraint** $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$ in the dual — partial credit only.
> 5. **Confusing primal/dual variables** — $\mathbf{w}, b$ are primal; $\alpha_i$ are dual. Do not write "$\mathbf{w} \geq 0$".
> 6. **In numerical problems, students often skip showing intermediate kernel/inner product computations** — always show $K_{ij}$ matrix or individual inner products.
> 7. **Not verifying** that the solution is a support vector when recovering $b$ — you may use any $i$ with $\alpha_i > 0$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Linear SVM (KTU 2024)**
>
> **Core Concepts:**
> - [x] Linear SVM finds a **maximum-margin separating hyperplane** for binary classification.
> - [x] Hyperplane: $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$; Decision: $\text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)$.
> - [x] Margin width: $\dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.
> - [x] Support vectors lie on the margin boundaries: $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$.
> - [x] Only support vectors determine the hyperplane (sparse solution).
>
> **Primal Form (Hard Margin):**
> - [x] Objective: $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$.
> - [x] Constraint: $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$.
>
> **Dual Form:**
> - [x] Objective: $\max \sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$.
> - [x] Constraints: $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$, $\alpha_i \geq 0$.
> - [x] Recovery: $\mathbf{w} = \sum \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$, $b = y_k - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_k$ for SV $k$.
>
> **KKT Conditions (must know all 4):**
> - [x] Stationarity (gives $\mathbf{w}$ and $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$).
> - [x] Primal feasibility.
> - [x] Dual feasibility ($\alpha_i \geq 0$).
> - [x] Complementary slackness (links $\alpha_i > 0$ to support vectors).
>
> **Key Properties:**
> - [x] Convex QP → **global optimum guaranteed**.
> - [x] **Sparse** solution: only support vectors matter.
> - [x] Dual depends on **inner products** $\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$ → enables kernel trick.
> - [x] Linear SVM inference cost: $O(d)$ per test point.
>
> **Common Confusions to Avoid:**
> - [x] Functional margin ≠ Geometric margin (latter is scale-invariant).
> - [x] Maximize margin ⟺ Minimize $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ ⟺ Minimize $\frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$.
> - [x] $\alpha_i = 0 \Rightarrow$ not a support vector; $\alpha_i > 0 \Rightarrow$ support vector.
> - [x] Primal is in $\mathbf{w}$ ($d$ variables); Dual is in $\alpha$ ($N$ variables).

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hyperplane — Formal Definition

A **Hyperplane** is a flat affine subspace of dimension $(n-1)$ that exists within an $n$-dimensional feature (input) space. It is the single most fundamental decision-boundary object used in linear classification algorithms, most notably the **Support Vector Machine (SVM)**.

In geometric terms, a hyperplane partitions the entire $n$-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^n$ into exactly **two disjoint half-spaces**. Mathematically, it is defined by the linear equation:

$$w^T x + b = 0$$

where:
- $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the input feature vector
- $w \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the weight (normal) vector orthogonal to the hyperplane
- $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the bias (offset) term

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU Board Examinations, students must remember that the dimension of a hyperplane is always **one less** than the ambient space. A line is a hyperplane in 2D, a plane is a hyperplane in 3D, and an $(n-1)$-dimensional flat surface is a hyperplane in $n$D.

### 1.2 Maximum Margin Hyperplane — Formal Definition

The **Maximum Margin Hyperplane** is the *unique* optimal separating hyperplane that maximizes the perpendicular distance (margin) between itself and the nearest training data points from each class. The training samples lying exactly on the boundary of this margin are termed **Support Vectors**.

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The Road Divider

Imagine a wide two-lane road built between two crowded villages located on opposite sides of a flat plain. The villagers want the *widest possible road* so that no hut (data point) gets accidentally demolished. The **center divider line** of this widest road is precisely the **Maximum Margin Hyperplane**, and the closest huts on either side are the **Support Vectors**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** A hyperplane is simply a "decision wall." In 2D it is a line, in 3D it is a plane, and in higher dimensions it generalizes to an $(n-1)$-dimensional flat surface. Its job is to cleanly separate two classes of data points.

### 1.4 Dimensional Manifestation Table

| Ambient Space $\mathbb{R}^n$ | Hyperplane Dimension | Common Name |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 2D | 1D | Line |
| 3D | 2D | Plane |
| 4D | 3D | 3D-Hyperplane |
| $n$D | $(n-1)$D | General Hyperplane |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Interpretation of the Hyperplane Equation

The equation $w^T x + b = 0$ can be broken down into three geometric roles:

1. **The Vector $w$ (Normal Vector):** The vector $w$ is **perpendicular (orthogonal)** to the hyperplane. It points in the direction of the steepest increase of the linear function $f(x) = w^T x + b$.

2. **The Scalar $b$ (Bias):** The bias $b$ controls the **offset** of the hyperplane from the origin. If $b = 0$, the hyperplane passes through the origin.

3. **The Decision Rule:** For any new point $x$, classification is performed by:
   - If $w^T x + b > 0 \Rightarrow$ Class $+1$
   - If $w^T x + b < 0 \Rightarrow$ Class $-1$

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of the Maximum Margin Principle

- **Why?** When multiple hyperplanes can perfectly separate two classes, which one do we pick? The hyperplane that lies *farthest* from the closest training points gives the **lowest generalization error** (Vapnik–Chervonenkis theory).
- **How?** We maximize the perpendicular distance from the hyperplane to the nearest data point of *either* class.

### 2.3 Perpendicular (Signed) Distance of a Point from a Hyperplane

The signed perpendicular distance from any point $x_i$ to the hyperplane $w^T x + b = 0$ is given by:

$$d_i = \frac{w^T x_i + b}{\|w\|}$$

where $\|w\| = \sqrt{w_1^2 + w_2^2 + \dots + w_n^2}$ is the Euclidean norm of the weight vector.

### 2.4 Margin Definition

For a linearly separable dataset, the **margin** $M$ is defined as the sum of the perpendicular distances from the hyperplane to the nearest positive-class point and the nearest negative-class point:

$$M = \frac{1}{\|w\|} + \frac{1}{\|w\|} = \frac{2}{\|w\|}$$

### 2.5 The Primal Optimization Problem (Hard Margin SVM)

To find the Maximum Margin Hyperplane, we solve the following convex quadratic optimization problem:

$$\min_{w, b} \quad \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2$$

subject to the constraints:

$$y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the factor $\frac{1}{2}$?** It is a mathematical convenience — the gradient $\nabla \|w\|^2 = 2w$ becomes simply $w$, eliminating the constant during differentiation.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Purpose |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Hyperplane Equation | $w^T x + b = 0$ | Defines the decision boundary |
| 2 | Signed Distance | $d_i = \dfrac{w^T x_i + b}{\|w\|}$ | Perpendicular distance of point $x_i$ |
| 3 | Functional Margin | $\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i(w^T x_i + b)$ | Confidence of correct classification |
| 4 | Geometric Margin | $\gamma_i = \dfrac{y_i(w^T x_i + b)}{\|w\|}$ | Scaled (true) margin for sample $i$ |
| 5 | Overall Margin | $M = \dfrac{2}{\|w\|}$ | Total width of the separating band |
| 6 | Primal Objective | $\min \dfrac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ | Convex QP for maximum margin |
| 7 | Classification Rule | $\text{sign}(w^T x + b)$ | Output class label |
| 8 | Constraint | $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$ | Forces all points outside the margin |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Maximum margin hyperplanes are heavily deployed in biomedical diagnostics, face recognition (eigenfaces), spam filtering, fault detection in mechanical systems, and remote-sensing image classification.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Perpendicular Distance from a Point to a Hyperplane

**Statement:** Prove that the perpendicular distance from a point $x_0 \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to the hyperplane $w^T x + b = 0$ is $d = \dfrac{\vert w^T x_0 + b \vert}{\|w\|}$.

**Step 1 — Identify a point on the hyperplane.**
Let $x_p$ be *any* point lying on the hyperplane. By definition:
$$w^T x_p + b = 0$$

**Step 2 — Construct the vector from $x_p$ to $x_0$.**
Define the displacement vector as:
$$v = x_0 - x_p$$

**Step 3 — Project $v$ onto the normal direction $w$.**
The component of $v$ along $w$ is the scalar projection:
$$d = \frac{w^T v}{\|w\|}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $v = x_0 - x_p$.**
$$d = \frac{w^T (x_0 - x_p)}{\|w\|} = \frac{w^T x_0 - w^T x_p}{\|w\|}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the hyperplane condition.**
Since $w^T x_p = -b$:
$$d = \frac{w^T x_0 - (-b)}{\|w\|} = \frac{w^T x_0 + b}{\|w\|}$$

**Step 6 — Take absolute value for geometric (unsigned) distance.**

$$\boxed{d = \frac{\vert w^T x_0 + b \vert}{\|w\|}}$$

This completes the derivation. The sign indicates on which side of the hyperplane the point lies.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The Margin $M = \dfrac{2}{\|w\|}$

**Step 1 — Define the canonical hyperplane scaling.**
We are free to scale $(w, b)$ by any non-zero scalar $k$ without changing the hyperplane, because:
$$k w^T x + k b = 0 \iff w^T x + b = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply the "canonical form" scaling.**
Choose a scaling such that the nearest points from the positive class satisfy:
$$w^T x_{+} + b = +1$$

And the nearest points from the negative class satisfy:
$$w^T x_{-} + b = -1$$

**Step 3 — Compute distance to the positive support vector.**

$$d_{+} = \frac{\vert w^T x_{+} + b \vert}{\|w\|} = \frac{\vert +1 \vert}{\|w\|} = \frac{1}{\|w\|}$$

**Step 4 — Compute distance to the negative support vector.**

$$d_{-} = \frac{\vert w^T x_{-} + b \vert}{\|w\|} = \frac{\vert -1 \vert}{\|w\|} = \frac{1}{\|w\|}$$

**Step 5 — Sum the two half-margins.**

$$M = d_{+} + d_{-} = \frac{1}{\|w\|} + \frac{1}{\|w\|}$$

$$\boxed{M = \frac{2}{\|w\|}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Equivalence of Minimizing $\|w\|$ and Maximizing Margin

**Step 1 — Recall the margin expression.**
$$M = \frac{2}{\|w\|}$$

**Step 2 — To maximize $M$, we must minimize its denominator $\|w\|$.**

**Step 3 — The squared form is preferred for differentiability.**
$$\min_{w} \|w\| \iff \min_{w} \|w\|^2$$

**Step 4 — Add the standard $\frac{1}{2}$ factor for cleaner gradients.**

$$\boxed{\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Example — 2D Numerical Walkthrough

Consider two support vectors $x_+ = (3, 3)$ with label $y = +1$ and $x_- = (1, 1)$ with label $y = -1$. Let $w = (1, 1)$ and $b = -4$.

**Step 1 — Verify the hyperplane equation.**
$$w^T x + b = x_1 + x_2 - 4 = 0$$
The hyperplane is the line $x_1 + x_2 = 4$.

**Step 2 — Verify the support vector constraints.**
For $x_+ = (3, 3)$, $y = +1$:
$$y_i(w^T x_i + b) = (+1)(3 + 3 - 4) = +2 \geq 1 \quad \checkmark$$

For $x_- = (1, 1)$, $y = -1$:
$$y_i(w^T x_i + b) = (-1)(1 + 1 - 4) = +2 \geq 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Compute the norm of $w$.**
$$\|w\| = \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the margin.**
$$M = \frac{2}{\|w\|} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$

This margin is the total perpendicular width between the two parallel decision boundaries $x_1 + x_2 = 3$ and $x_1 + x_2 = 5$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
Define a hyperplane. Write its mathematical equation in $n$-dimensional space and explain the role of the weight vector $w$.

**Model Answer:**

A hyperplane is a flat affine subspace of dimension $(n-1)$ in an $n$-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^n$. It acts as a linear decision boundary that splits the space into two half-spaces.

The equation of a hyperplane is:
$$w^T x + b = 0$$

where $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the feature vector, $w \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the weight vector, and $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the bias.

**Role of $w$:** The vector $w$ is the **normal vector** perpendicular to the hyperplane. It determines the *orientation* of the hyperplane in the feature space and points in the direction of the steepest increase of the linear scoring function $f(x) = w^T x + b$. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
What is a Maximum Margin Hyperplane? Why is it preferred over other separating hyperplanes?

**Model Answer:**

A **Maximum Margin Hyperplane** is the optimal separating hyperplane that maximizes the perpendicular distance (the *margin*) to the nearest training samples from each class.

**Why preferred:**
- It yields the **lowest generalization error** according to Statistical Learning Theory.
- It is **robust** to small perturbations in training data.
- It depends only on the **support vectors**, making the model sparse and memory-efficient.
- It maximizes the *confidence* of classification for unseen test points. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the perpendicular distance of a point $x_0$ from the hyperplane $w^T x + b = 0$. Hence obtain the margin expression for a linearly separable binary classification problem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the dataset shown below, identify the support vectors and compute the maximum margin hyperplane.  
Positive class: $(3, 3)$, $(3, -3)$, $(6, 0)$ | Negative class: $(1, 1)$, $(-2, -1)$, $(0, -4)$

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A**

**(a) Perpendicular Distance Derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1:* Let $x_p$ be any point on the hyperplane. Then $w^T x_p + b = 0$.

*Step 2:* Vector from $x_p$ to $x_0$: $v = x_0 - x_p$.

*Step 3:* Projection of $v$ onto $w$:
$$d = \frac{w^T v}{\|w\|} = \frac{w^T(x_0 - x_p)}{\|w\|}$$

*Step 4:* Simplification using $w^T x_p = -b$:
$$d = \frac{w^T x_0 + b}{\|w\|}$$

*Step 5:* Geometric (unsigned) distance:
$$d = \frac{\vert w^T x_0 + b \vert}{\|w\|}$$

*Step 6 — Margin Derivation:* After canonical scaling $w^T x_+ + b = +1$ and $w^T x_- + b = -1$:

$$d_+ = \frac{1}{\|w\|}, \quad d_- = \frac{1}{\|w\|}$$

$$\therefore M = d_+ + d_- = \frac{2}{\|w\|}$$

**(b) Identifying the Maximum Margin Hyperplane (7 marks):**

By visual analysis of the convex hulls:
- **Support Vectors:** $(3, 3)$, $(3, -3)$ from positive class; $(1, 1)$ from negative class.

*Step 1 — Set up the canonical constraints:*
$$3w_1 + 3w_2 + b = +1$$
$$3w_1 - 3w_2 + b = +1$$
$$w_1 + w_2 + b = -1$$

*Step 2 — Subtract equation 2 from equation 1:*
$$6w_2 = 0 \Rightarrow w_2 = 0$$

*Step 3 — Substitute $w_2 = 0$ into equation 1:*
$$3w_1 + b = 1$$

*Step 4 — Substitute $w_2 = 0$ into equation 3:*
$$w_1 + b = -1$$

*Step 5 — Subtract the two results:*
$$2w_1 = 2 \Rightarrow w_1 = 1, \quad b = -2$$

*Step 6 — Final Maximum Margin Hyperplane Equation:*
$$\boxed{x_1 - 2 = 0}$$

Margin = $\dfrac{2}{\|w\|} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{1}} = 2$ units.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]*

**(a)** State the primal optimization problem for finding the maximum margin hyperplane in a hard-margin SVM. Explain each term in the formulation and justify why the objective function uses $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ instead of $\|w\|$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider two support vectors $x_+ = (2, 2)$ with $y = +1$ and $x_- = (1, 0)$ with $y = -1$ lying on the canonical margin boundaries. Determine the optimal $(w, b)$ and the margin width. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B**

**(a) Primal Formulation (7 marks):**

The hard-margin SVM primal problem is:
$$\min_{w, b} \quad \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i$$

**Term-by-term explanation:**
- **$\|w\|^2$:** Squared Euclidean norm — penalizes large weights, equivalent to minimizing the inverse of the margin.
- **$\frac{1}{2}$ factor:** Mathematical convenience. The derivative of $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ is simply $w$ (no factor of 2), simplifying Lagrange multiplier computations.
- **Constraints $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$:** Force every training point to lie on the correct side of its respective margin boundary. Points must be at least functional margin 1 from the hyperplane.

**Justification for $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ vs $\|w\|$:** Both are mathematically equivalent because the optimum is the same. However, $\|w\|^2$ is **differentiable everywhere** and **strictly convex**, ensuring a unique global minimum. The $\frac{1}{2}$ removes the 2 from the gradient, leading to cleaner Lagrange conditions.

**(b) Solving for $w$ and $b$ (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Apply canonical constraints:*
$$2w_1 + 2w_2 + b = +1 \quad \text{(positive SV)}$$
$$w_1 + 0 \cdot w_2 + b = -1 \quad \text{(negative SV)}$$

*Step 2 — Subtract equation 2 from equation 1:*
$$w_1 + 2w_2 = 2$$

*Step 3 — Solve the system. From equation 2:* $b = -1 - w_1$.

*Step 4 — Substitute into equation 1:*
$$2w_1 + 2w_2 - 1 - w_1 = 1 \Rightarrow w_1 + 2w_2 = 2$$

This is one equation with two unknowns. To uniquely solve, we use the **maximum margin condition** which is equivalent to the perpendicular bisector property between the two support vectors.

*Step 5 — The hyperplane must be perpendicular to the line joining the support vectors.*
The vector joining the two support vectors is:
$$x_+ - x_- = (2, 2) - (1, 0) = (1, 2)$$
Therefore $w$ is parallel to this vector: $w = k(1, 2)$.

*Step 6 — Apply the constraint from the negative support vector:*
$$w_1 + b = -1 \Rightarrow k(1) + b = -1 \Rightarrow b = -1 - k$$

*Step 7 — Apply the constraint from the positive support vector:*
$$2k + 4k + b = 1 \Rightarrow 6k + b = 1$$

*Step 8 — Substitute $b$:*
$$6k - 1 - k = 1 \Rightarrow 5k = 2 \Rightarrow k = \frac{2}{5}$$

*Step 9 — Final Result:*
$$w = \left(\frac{2}{5}, \frac{4}{5}\right), \quad b = -\frac{7}{5}$$

*Step 10 — Margin Width:*
$$\|w\| = \sqrt{\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^2} = \sqrt{\frac{4 + 16}{25}} = \sqrt{\frac{20}{25}} = \frac{2\sqrt{5}}{5}$$

$$M = \frac{2}{\|w\|} = \frac{2}{\frac{2\sqrt{5}}{5}} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{5}} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value** in the distance formula $d = \frac{\vert w^T x_0 + b \vert}{\|w\|}$ — examiners deduct 1 mark for unsigned distance in signed distance questions.
> 2. **Confusing functional margin $\hat{\gamma}$ with geometric margin $\gamma$** — geometric margin is *scale-invariant*, functional margin is *not*.
> 3. **Not verifying the canonical scaling** when solving for $w$ and $b$ — students often forget that support vectors must satisfy $y_i(w^T x_i + b) = 1$ (not just $\geq 1$).
> 4. **Mistaking the bias role** — $b$ shifts the hyperplane; $w$ rotates it. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 5. **Sign errors in Lagrange optimization** — when introducing soft-margin slack variables later, students forget $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Maximum Margin Hyperplane**

- ✅ **Hyperplane Definition:** Flat affine subspace of dimension $(n-1)$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$.
- ✅ **Canonical Equation:** $w^T x + b = 0$.
- ✅ **$w$ is the normal vector** — perpendicular to the hyperplane.
- ✅ **$b$ is the bias** — controls offset from origin.
- ✅ **Signed Distance Formula:** $d = \dfrac{w^T x + b}{\|w\|}$.
- ✅ **Margin Width:** $M = \dfrac{2}{\|w\|}$.
- ✅ **Primal Objective:** $\min \dfrac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$.
- ✅ **Constraint:** $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$ (for all $i$).
- ✅ **Support Vectors** are training points lying *exactly* on the margin boundaries $w^T x + b = \pm 1$.
- ✅ **Only support vectors** determine the final hyperplane — all other points are irrelevant.
- ✅ **Maximum margin hyperplane minimizes generalization error** (Statistical Learning Theory).
- ✅ **Hard-margin SVM requires linearly separable data.**
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** $\text{sign}(w^T x + b)$ for new test points.
- ✅ **Scaling Invariance:** $(w, b)$ and $(kw, kb)$ define the *same* hyperplane for any $k \neq 0$.
- ✅ **Geometry Insight:** Hyperplane is the perpendicular bisector of the shortest line segment connecting the convex hulls of the two classes.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Non-linear Support Vector Machine (Non-linear SVM)** is a supervised learning classifier that extends the classical linear SVM by enabling the construction of non-linear decision boundaries in the original input space. It accomplishes this by implicitly mapping the input data $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ into a higher-dimensional feature space $\mathcal{F}$ (potentially infinite-dimensional) via a non-linear transformation $\phi(\cdot)$, where a linear separating hyperplane is learned. The "kernel trick" allows this mapping to be performed **without explicitly computing the coordinates in the higher-dimensional space**, by replacing inner products $\langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \rangle$ with a kernel function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Non-linear SVM is essential when data is **NOT linearly separable** in the original input space. The model complexity is controlled through the choice of kernel and its hyperparameters (e.g., degree $\degree$, bandwidth $\gamma$, regularization parameter $C$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Block:**
> - **Primal objective (in feature space):** $\displaystyle \min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2} \|\mathbf{w}\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i$
> - **Subject to:** $y_i(\langle \mathbf{w}, \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) \rangle + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$, with $\xi_i \geq 0$
> - **Dual form uses kernels:** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \rangle$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a librarian trying to separate two types of books (novels and textbooks) on a flat library floor. You try to draw a straight line on the floor — but the books are scattered in **concentric circles**, novels inside the rings and textbooks outside. No straight line can separate them. A linear SVM fails here.

**What is the fix?** Lift the books into 3D space (onto a vertical axis representing a new property such as "density of pages"). Suddenly, the two classes form two parallel planes that **a single flat sheet can perfectly slice apart**. When you project that cut back onto the 2D floor, it becomes a **circle** — a non-linear boundary.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Summary:**
> - **Linear SVM** = straight cut ✂️
> - **Non-linear SVM** = bend the cut into curves, loops, or blobs by *lifting data into a richer space* 🌀
> - **Kernel Trick** = a magical shortcut that lets you find the curved cut *without ever explicitly climbing into the higher dimension* ✨

---

## 1.3 Why Linear SVM Fails — Motivation for Non-linearity

A linear SVM constructs a hyperplane of the form:
$$\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$$

This can only produce **straight-line (or flat-plane) decision boundaries**. Real-world datasets frequently exhibit:
- Circular/elliptical class regions (e.g., XOR-like patterns)
- Spirals
- Concentric rings
- Highly irregular manifolds

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Metric for Kernels:** A kernel must satisfy **Mercer's Condition**, which guarantees it can be expressed as an inner product in some Hilbert space. Formally, the Gram matrix $\mathbf{K}$ with entries $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ must be **positive semi-definite** for all valid input sets.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Feature Mapping Approach

Given data $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^\top \in \mathbb{R}^2$ that is not linearly separable, define a non-linear map:
$$\phi : \mathbb{R}^2 \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^3, \quad \phi(x_1, x_2) = (x_1^2, \sqrt{2}\,x_1 x_2, x_2^2)^\top$$

In the lifted space, the linear model becomes:
$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}) + b = w_1 x_1^2 + w_2 \sqrt{2}\, x_1 x_2 + w_3 x_2^2 + b$$

Projected back to $\mathbb{R}^2$, this is a **quadratic curve** — non-linear in the original space.

### Problems with Explicit Mapping
- **Computational cost:** $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ can be extremely high-dimensional (e.g., monomials of degree $d$ in $d_{orig}$ dimensions yield $\mathcal{O}(d_{orig}^d)$ features).
- **Memory blow-up** for high-degree polynomials.
- The **kernel trick** resolves both issues.

---

## 2.2 The Kernel Trick — How and Why

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. **Dual formulation of SVM** depends on data **only through inner products** $\langle \mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j \rangle$.
2. Replace every such inner product with a **kernel function** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$.
3. Choose $K$ such that $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \rangle$ for some implicit $\phi$.
4. **Result:** Train a linear classifier in the (implicitly mapped) high-dimensional space, **but compute everything in the original low-dimensional space**.

### Decision Function with Kernels

The decision function for a new test point $\mathbf{x}$ is:
$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b$$

Only the **support vectors** (those with $\alpha_i > 0$) contribute — this is identical in form to the linear case, but with the kernel replacing the inner product.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Kernel Functions

> [!NOTE]
> All standard kernels, with their implicit feature mappings and key properties. The table uses `\vert` (LaTeX vertical bar) instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Kernel Name | Mathematical Form $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Implicit Feature Space | Key Hyperparameters | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ | Original space $\mathbb{R}^d$ | None (or $C$) | Baseline / linearly separable data |
| **Polynomial Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\gamma \, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + r)^\degree$ | All monomials up to degree $\degree$ | $\degree \in \mathbb{N}$, $\gamma$, $r$ (coef0) | Image processing, NLP |
| **Gaussian RBF Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \vert\vert^2\right)$ | Infinite-dimensional Hilbert space | $\gamma > 0$ | Most popular default; universal approximator |
| **Sigmoid Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \tanh(\gamma \, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + r)$ | Not always a valid kernel (Mercer may fail) | $\gamma$, $r$ | Neural-network-like behavior |
| **Laplacian Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \vert\vert_1\right)$ | Infinite-dimensional | $\gamma > 0$ | Robust to outliers, histogram data |
| **String / Spectrum Kernel** | $K(s_i, s_j)$ counts common substrings | Discrete sequence space | Substring length $k$ | Bioinformatics, text classification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RBF is the default choice in practice** (e.g., in `sklearn.svm.SVC`) because it is a **universal approximator**: it can approximate any continuous function on a compact domain arbitrarily well, given enough data.

---

## 2.4 Mercer's Theorem — Validity of Kernels

A symmetric function $K : \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is a **valid (positive semi-definite) kernel** if and only if the Gram matrix is positive semi-definite:
$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} K(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_1) & K(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2) & \cdots \\ K(\mathbf{x}_2, \mathbf{x}_1) & K(\mathbf{x}_2, \mathbf{x}_2) & \cdots \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots \end{bmatrix} \succeq 0$$

for any finite set $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_n\} \subset \mathcal{X}$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Mercer's condition is a *favourite* 3-mark question. Memorize the form: *"Gram matrix must be positive semi-definite"* — examiners love this phrase.

### Closure Properties (Building New Kernels)
If $K_1$ and $K_2$ are valid kernels, then so are:
- $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = a K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) + b K_2(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})$ for $a, b \geq 0$
- $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) \cdot K_2(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})$
- $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = f(\mathbf{x}) f(\mathbf{z})$ for any real-valued function $f$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Bioinformatics:** Protein classification, cancer subtype identification (RBF and spectrum kernels).
- **Computer Vision:** Face recognition, handwritten digit recognition (MNIST), pedestrian detection.
- **Geostatistics & Remote Sensing:** Land-cover classification from satellite imagery.
- **Fault Diagnosis in Mechanical Systems:** Vibration-signal classification using RBF-kernel SVMs.
- **Financial Engineering:** Credit scoring, fraud detection on highly non-linear transactional patterns.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — From Primal to Dual (with Kernels)

**Step 1: Primal Problem in Feature Space**

Given training data $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$ with $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the soft-margin primal in the feature space $\mathcal{F}$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \xi} \quad & \frac{1}{2} \|\mathbf{w}\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i \\
\text{subject to} \quad & y_i \left( \mathbf{w}^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) + b \right) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad i = 1, \ldots, n \\
& \xi_i \geq 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Lagrangian Construction**

Introduce Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ (for margin constraints) and $\mu_i \geq 0$ (for $\xi_i \geq 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \xi, \alpha, \mu) = \frac{1}{2} \|\mathbf{w}\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \left[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu_i \xi_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Stationarity (Setting Partial Derivatives to Zero)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} &= \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) = 0 \\
\Longrightarrow \quad \mathbf{w} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} &= C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Back-Substitution (The Kernel Trick Emerges)**

Substitute $\mathbf{w}$ back into $\mathcal{L}$. All terms involving $\phi$ collapse into **inner products** $\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$. Replace them with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\alpha} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** The dual depends on data **only via** the kernel matrix $\mathbf{K}$. **The map $\phi$ never appears explicitly** — this is the kernel trick in mathematical form.

**Step 5: Decision Function for a Test Point**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}) &= \mathbf{w}^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}) + b \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \underbrace{\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x})}_{K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x})} + b \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b
\end{aligned}
$$

The class label is assigned as $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(f(\mathbf{x}))$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Polynomial Kernel Expansion (2D, degree 2)

Let $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^\top$, $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, z_2)^\top$, and define the homogeneous polynomial-2 kernel:
$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z})^2$$

**Step 1: Expand the kernel in the original 2D space**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) &= (x_1 z_1 + x_2 z_2)^2 \\
&= x_1^2 z_1^2 + 2 x_1 x_2 z_1 z_2 + x_2^2 z_2^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Define the implicit feature map**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{pmatrix} x_1^2 \\ \sqrt{2}\, x_1 x_2 \\ x_2^2 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Verify the inner product equals the kernel**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(\mathbf{x})^\top \phi(\mathbf{z}) &= x_1^2 z_1^2 + (\sqrt{2}\, x_1 x_2)(\sqrt{2}\, z_1 z_2) + x_2^2 z_2^2 \\
&= x_1^2 z_1^2 + 2 x_1 x_2 z_1 z_2 + x_2^2 z_2^2 \\
&= K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows explicitly how $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})$ computes an inner product in a 3D space **without ever constructing the 3D coordinates** $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ or $\phi(\mathbf{z})$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Hyperparameters

| Hyperparameter | Effect if **Increased** | Effect if **Decreased** | Risk if Mis-set |
|---|---|---|---|
| **$C$** (regularization) | Tighter fit to training data (less slack) | Wider margin, more slack allowed | Large $C \Rightarrow$ overfitting; small $C \Rightarrow$ underfitting |
| **$\gamma$** (RBF bandwidth) | Decision boundary becomes more localized/tighter; each support vector influences a smaller region | Smoother, more global decision boundary | Large $\gamma \Rightarrow$ overfitting (zig-zag boundary); small $\gamma \Rightarrow$ underfitting |
| **$\degree$** (polynomial) | Decision boundary becomes more wiggly, captures fine interactions | Simpler, smoother boundary | High $\degree \Rightarrow$ numerical instability, severe overfitting |
| **$r$ (coef0)** | Controls higher-order vs. lower-order trade-off in polynomial/sigmoid | Less high-order influence | Wrong sign $\Rightarrow$ invalid kernel (sigmoid) |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Sanity Check — RBF Kernel Computation

Let $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1, 0)^\top$, $\mathbf{x}_2 = (0, 1)^\top$, $\gamma = 0.5$.

**Step 1: Compute squared Euclidean distance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert\vert \mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_2 \vert\vert^2 &= (1 - 0)^2 + (0 - 1)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply RBF kernel formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2) &= \exp(-\gamma \cdot \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_2 \vert\vert^2) \\
&= \exp(-0.5 \times 2) \\
&= \exp(-1) \approx 0.3679
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Self-similarity check** (kernel value with itself)

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_1) &= \exp(-\gamma \cdot 0) = \exp(0) = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU exam trick:** RBF kernel satisfies $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}) = 1$ **always**. The diagonal of the RBF Gram matrix is identically 1 — a free sanity check during exams.
