$$\delta^l_j = \sum_k \delta^{l+1}_k \cdot w^{l+1}_{kj} \cdot \sigma'(z^l_j)$$

**Step 6.** Factoring:
$$\delta^l_j = \left(\sum_k w^{l+1}_{kj} \, \delta^{l+1}_k\right) \sigma'(z^l_j)$$

**Step 7.** In matrix form (with transpose):
$$\delta^l = \left((w^{l+1})^T \delta^{l+1}\right) \odot \sigma'(z^l) \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### Derivation 3 — Bias Gradient

**Step 1.** Since $z^l_j = \sum_k w^l_{jk} a^{l-1}_k + b^l_j$:
$$\frac{\partial z^l_j}{\partial b^l_j} = 1$$

**Step 2.** Using the chain rule:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial b^l_j} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial z^l_j} \cdot \frac{\partial z^l_j}{\partial b^l_j} = \delta^l_j \cdot 1$$

**Step 3.** Therefore:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial b^l_j} = \delta^l_j \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### Derivation 4 — Weight Gradient

**Step 1.** Using the chain rule again:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial w^l_{jk}} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial z^l_j} \cdot \frac{\partial z^l_j}{\partial w^l_{jk}}$$

**Step 2.** Since $z^l_j = \sum_k w^l_{jk} a^{l-1}_k + b^l_j$:
$$\frac{\partial z^l_j}{\partial w^l_{jk}} = a^{l-1}_k$$

**Step 3.** Substituting:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial w^l_{jk}} = \delta^l_j \cdot a^{l-1}_k \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Complete Worked Numerical Example

**Network Architecture:** 2 inputs $\rightarrow$ 2 hidden neurons $\rightarrow$ 1 output neuron (2-2-1 MLP).
**Activation:** Sigmoid for hidden and output layers.
**Learning rate:** $\eta = 0.5$.

### Step 1 — Initial Weights and Input

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $w^2$ (input to hidden) | $\begin{bmatrix} 0.15 & 0.20 \\ 0.25 & 0.30 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| $b^2$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0.35 \\ 0.35 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| $w^3$ (hidden to output) | $\begin{bmatrix} 0.40 \\ 0.45 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| $b^3$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0.60 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| Input $\mathbf{x}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0.05 \\ 0.10 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| Target $y$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0.01 \\ 0.99 \end{bmatrix}$ *(only second output is target here)* |

### Step 2 — Forward Pass

**Hidden layer net input:**
$$z^2_1 = (0.15)(0.05) + (0.20)(0.10) + 0.35 = 0.3775$$
$$z^2_2 = (0.25)(0.05) + (0.30)(0.10) + 0.35 = 0.3925$$

**Hidden layer activation (sigmoid):**
$$a^2_1 = \sigma(0.3775) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.3775}} \approx 0.5933$$
$$a^2_2 = \sigma(0.3925) \approx 0.5969$$

**Output layer net input:**
$$z^3_1 = (0.40)(0.5933) + (0.45)(0.5969) + 0.60 = 0.2373 + 0.2686 + 0.60 \approx 1.1059$$

**Output layer activation:**
$$a^3_1 = \sigma(1.1059) \approx 0.7514$$

**Cost (MSE, only one output considered):**
$$C = \frac{1}{2}(0.7514 - 0.99)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.0570) \approx 0.0285$$

### Step 3 — Backward Pass

**Output error:**
$$\delta^3_1 = (a^3_1 - y_1) \cdot \sigma'(z^3_1) = (0.7514 - 0.99) \cdot (0.7514)(1 - 0.7514)$$
$$\delta^3_1 = (-0.2386)(0.1868) \approx -0.0446$$

**Hidden layer error:**
$$\delta^2_1 = w^3_{11} \cdot \delta^3_1 \cdot \sigma'(z^2_1) = (0.40)(-0.0446)(0.5933)(1-0.5933)$$
$$\delta^2_1 = (0.40)(-0.0446)(0.2414) \approx -0.00430$$

$$\delta^2_2 = (0.45)(-0.0446)(0.5969)(1-0.5969) = (0.45)(-0.0446)(0.2407) \approx -0.00483$$

### Step 4 — Weight and Bias Updates

**Update output layer weights:**
$$\Delta w^3_{11} = -\eta \cdot \delta^3_1 \cdot a^2_1 = -(0.5)(-0.0446)(0.5933) = 0.01323$$
$$w^3_{11,\text{new}} = 0.40 + 0.01323 = 0.41323$$

$$\Delta w^3_{21} = -(0.5)(-0.0446)(0.5969) = 0.01331$$
$$w^3_{21,\text{new}} = 0.45 + 0.01331 = 0.46331$$

$$\Delta b^3_1 = -(0.5)(-0.0446) = 0.02230$$
$$b^3_{1,\text{new}} = 0.60 + 0.02230 = 0.62230$$

**Update hidden layer weights:**
$$\Delta w^2_{11} = -(0.5)(-0.00430)(0.05) = 0.000108$$
$$w^2_{11,\text{new}} = 0.15 + 0.000108 = 0.150108$$

$$\Delta w^2_{12} = -(0.5)(-0.00430)(0.10) = 0.000215$$
$$w^2_{12,\text{new}} = 0.20 + 0.000215 = 0.200215$$

> **Result:** Cost decreased from $0.0285$ to $\approx 0.0278$ in one iteration. After thousands of epochs, the network learns to map $\mathbf{x} \to y$ accurately.

---

## 3.3 Algorithmic Summary (Step-by-Step Pseudocode Style — Conceptual)

| Step | Operation | Equation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Input activation $a^1 = x$ | — |
| 2 | Feedforward: compute $z^l, a^l$ for $l = 2, \dots, L$ | $a^l = \sigma(w^l a^{l-1} + b^l)$ |
| 3 | Compute output error $\delta^L$ | $\delta^L = \nabla_a C \odot \sigma'(z^L)$ |
| 4 | Backpropagate: compute $\delta^l$ for $l = L-1, \dots, 2$ | $\delta^l = ((w^{l+1})^T \delta^{l+1}) \odot \sigma'(z^l)$ |
| 5 | Compute gradients | $\nabla_{w^l} C = \delta^l (a^{l-1})^T$, $\nabla_{b^l} C = \delta^l$ |
| 6 | Update parameters | $w^l \leftarrow w^l - \eta \nabla_{w^l} C$ |
| 7 | Repeat for next training example / mini-batch | — |

> [!TIP]
> The matrix-form equations are **non-trivial to derive but trivial to code**. In KTU exams, focus on showing every chain-rule step and clearly labeling matrix dimensions.

---

## 3.4 Why the Algorithm is Called "Back" Propagation

The error signal $\delta$ flows from the output layer (where the error is measurable) **backwards** to the input layer. At each hidden layer, the error is **re-weighted** by the transposed weight matrix of the subsequent layer — this is because the weights of layer $l+1$ determine how much each neuron in layer $l$ "influenced" the final error.

> [!NOTE]
> Mathematically, $(w^{l+1})^T$ in the recursion is the key term that makes BPA an **efficient $O(W)$ algorithm**. Without it, the chain rule applied naively would re-compute the same derivatives multiple times.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Architecture of Backpropagation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Layer X] --> B[Forward Pass Computation]
    B --> C[Hidden Layer Activations]
    C --> D[Output Layer Prediction]
    D --> E[Loss Function Computation]
    E --> F[Compute Output Error Delta L]
    F --> G[Backpropagate Error to Hidden Layer]
    G --> H[Compute Hidden Error Delta L Minus 1]
    H --> I[Compute Weight and Bias Gradients]
    I --> J[Update Weights using Gradient Descent]
    J --> K{Convergence Check}
    K -->|Not Converged| B
    K -->|Converged| L[Trained Network]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style D fill:#fff4e1
    style E fill:#ffe1e1
    style F fill:#e1ffe1
    style G fill:#e1ffe1
    style H fill:#e1ffe1
    style I fill:#f5e1ff
    style J fill:#f5e1ff
    style K fill:#ffffcc
    style L fill:#ccffcc
```

## 4.2 Detailed Neural Network Data Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[Input x1] --> Z1H[Hidden Neuron h1]
    X2[Input x2] --> Z1H
    X1 --> Z2H[Hidden Neuron h2]
    X2 --> Z2H
    Z1H --> A1H[Activation a1 h]
    Z2H --> A2H[Activation a2 h]
    A1H --> OUT[Output y hat]
    A2H --> OUT
    OUT --> LOSS[Loss Function C]
    TARGET[True Label y] --> LOSS
    LOSS --> DELTA_OUT[Error delta out]
    DELTA_OUT --> DELTA_H1[Error delta h1]
    DELTA_OUT --> DELTA_H2[Error delta h2]
    DELTA_H1 --> GRAD1[Gradients w h1]
    DELTA_H2 --> GRAD2[Gradients w h2]
    DELTA_OUT --> GRAD3[Gradients w out]
    GRAD1 --> UPD1[Update w h1]
    GRAD2 --> UPD2[Update w h2]
    GRAD3 --> UPD3[Update w out]
    UPD1 --> Z1H
    UPD2 --> Z2H
    UPD3 --> OUT

    style X1 fill:#cce5ff
    style X2 fill:#cce5ff
    style LOSS fill:#ffcccc
    style DELTA_OUT fill:#ffd699
    style DELTA_H1 fill:#d5e8d4
    style DELTA_H2 fill:#d5e8d4
    style UPD1 fill:#e1d5e1
    style UPD2 fill:#e1d5e1
    style UPD3 fill:#e1d5e1
```

## 4.3 Algorithm Phase Flow (Sequential View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start Training] --> INIT[Initialize Weights Randomly]
    INIT --> EPOCH{For Each Epoch}
    EPOCH --> FWD[Phase 1: Forward Pass]
    FWD --> CALC[Compute Activations Layer by Layer]
    CALC --> COMP[Compare with Target via Loss]
    COMP --> BWD[Phase 2: Backward Pass]
    BWD --> OUTERR[Compute Output Error Delta L]
    OUTERR --> HIDERR[Propagate Error to Hidden Layers]
    HIDERR --> GRAD[Compute Gradients for Weights and Biases]
    GRAD --> UPD[Update Weights and Biases]
    UPD --> EPOCH
    EPOCH -->|All Epochs Done| END[Training Complete]

    style START fill:#cce5ff
    style INIT fill:#fff2cc
    style FWD fill:#d5e8d4
    style BWD fill:#fad7ac
    style GRAD fill:#e1d5e1
    style UPD fill:#e1d5e1
    style END fill:#d5e8d4
```

## 4.4 Gradient Flow Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Output Layer L] -->|Delta L equals gradient a C times sigma prime z L| H1[Hidden Layer L Minus 1]
    H1 -->|Delta L Minus 1 equals W L transpose times Delta L times sigma prime| H2[Hidden Layer L Minus 2]
    H2 -->|Delta L Minus 2 equals W L Minus 1 transpose times Delta L Minus 1 times sigma prime| H3[Continue Until Layer 2]
    H3 -->|Compute final gradients| W[Weight and Bias Updates]

    style O fill:#ffcccc
    style H1 fill:#ffd699
    style H2 fill:#d5e8d4
    style H3 fill:#cce5ff
    style W fill:#e1d5e1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation of the Diagrams:** The **green blocks** represent the forward computation, the **orange blocks** represent error computation, the **purple blocks** represent gradient updates, and the **yellow blocks** represent control flow. This is the standard KTU-recommended representation for BPA flowcharts in exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the backpropagation algorithm. List its advantages and limitations.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Definition (1 mark):** Backpropagation is a supervised learning algorithm used to train multi-layer neural networks. It works in two phases — a **forward pass** where inputs are propagated through the network to produce an output, and a **backward pass** where the error is propagated backwards to update weights using gradient descent.

**Working (1 mark):** The error at the output layer is computed using the cost function. This error is then propagated backwards layer-by-layer using the chain rule of calculus, and weights are updated as:
$$w_{new} = w_{old} - \eta \frac{\partial C}{\partial w}$$

**Advantages (0.5 mark):** Computationally efficient, works for any number of hidden layers, foundation of modern deep learning.

**Limitations (0.5 mark):** Slow convergence, susceptible to local minima, vanishing gradient problem with sigmoid activation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
**Differentiate between Forward Pass and Backward Pass in backpropagation.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Aspect | Forward Pass | Backward Pass |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Input $\to$ Output | Output $\to$ Input |
| **Purpose** | Compute activations and predicted output | Compute error gradients and update weights |
| **Mathematical Tool** | Matrix multiplication + activation function | Chain rule of calculus |
| **Information Flow** | Activations $a^l$ and weighted inputs $z^l$ | Error terms $\delta^l$ |
| **Output** | Predicted output $\hat{y}$ | Weight and bias updates |

(Each distinct valid point: **1 mark**, maximum **3 marks**.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

### Question A
**(a)** Derive the four fundamental equations of the backpropagation algorithm using the chain rule of calculus. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 2-2-1 neural network with sigmoid activation. Given the input $x = [0.1, 0.2]^T$, target output $y = 0.5$, and initial weights/biases as shown below, perform **one complete iteration** of backpropagation with learning rate $\eta = 0.5$:

$$w^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad b^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad w^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad b^{(2)} = 0.2$$

Show the forward pass, compute the loss, perform the backward pass, and update all weights and biases. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the Four Equations (7 marks)

**Assumption 1 (0.5 mark):** Cost function $C = \frac{1}{2n}\sum_x (y - a^L)^2$.

**Assumption 2 (0.5 mark):** All neurons use a differentiable activation function $\sigma$.

**Equation 1 — Output Error (1.5 marks):** Define $\delta^L_j = \frac{\partial C}{\partial z^L_j}$. Using chain rule and $a^L_j = \sigma(z^L_j)$:
$$\delta^L_j = \frac{\partial C}{\partial a^L_j} \cdot \sigma'(z^L_j) \implies \delta^L = \nabla_a C \odot \sigma'(z^L)$$

**Equation 2 — Hidden Error (1.5 marks):** Express $\delta^l_j$ via layer $l+1$:
$$\delta^l_j = \sum_k \delta^{l+1}_k \cdot w^{l+1}_{kj} \cdot \sigma'(z^l_j) \implies \delta^l = ((w^{l+1})^T \delta^{l+1}) \odot \sigma'(z^l)$$

**Equation 3 — Bias Gradient (1 mark):** Since $\frac{\partial z^l_j}{\partial b^l_j} = 1$:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial b^l_j} = \delta^l_j$$

**Equation 4 — Weight Gradient (1 mark):** Since $\frac{\partial z^l_j}{\partial w^l_{jk}} = a^{l-1}_k$:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial w^l_{jk}} = a^{l-1}_k \cdot \delta^l_j$$

**Update Rule (1 mark):** $w^l_{jk} \leftarrow w^l_{jk} - \eta \cdot \delta^l_j \cdot a^{l-1}_k$.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Worked Example (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Forward Pass (2 marks):**

Hidden net inputs:
$$z^2_1 = (0.1)(0.1) + (0.2)(0.2) + 0.1 = 0.15$$
$$z^2_2 = (0.3)(0.1) + (0.4)(0.2) + 0.1 = 0.21$$

Hidden activations (sigmoid):
$$a^2_1 = \sigma(0.15) = 0.5374, \quad a^2_2 = \sigma(0.21) = 0.5523$$

Output net input:
$$z^3_1 = (0.5)(0.5374) + (0.6)(0.5523) + 0.2 = 0.2687 + 0.3314 + 0.2 = 0.8001$$

Output activation:
$$a^3_1 = \sigma(0.8001) = 0.6900$$

**Loss:** $C = \frac{1}{2}(0.6900 - 0.5)^2 = 0.0181$.

**Step 2 — Backward Pass (2 marks):**

Output error:
$$\delta^3_1 = (0.6900 - 0.5) \cdot (0.6900)(1-0.6900) = (0.1900)(0.2139) = 0.0406$$

Hidden errors:
$$\delta^2_1 = (0.5)(0.0406)(0.5374)(1-0.5374) = (0.5)(0.0406)(0.2486) = 0.00505$$
$$\delta^2_2 = (0.6)(0.0406)(0.5523)(1-0.5523) = (0.6)(0.0406)(0.2472) = 0.00602$$

**Step 3 — Weight Updates (2 marks):**

Output layer:
$$\Delta w^2_{11} = (0.5)(0.0406)(0.5374) = 0.01091 \Rightarrow w^2_{11,\text{new}} = 0.51091$$
$$\Delta w^2_{21} = (0.5)(0.0406)(0.5523) = 0.01121 \Rightarrow w^2_{21,\text{new}} = 0.61121$$
$$\Delta b^2 = (0.5)(0.0406) = 0.0203 \Rightarrow b^2_{\text{new}} = 0.2203$$

Hidden layer:
$$\Delta w^1_{11} = (0.5)(0.00505)(0.1) = 0.000253 \Rightarrow w^1_{11,\text{new}} = 0.100253$$
$$\Delta w^1_{12} = (0.5)(0.00505)(0.2) = 0.000505 \Rightarrow w^1_{12,\text{new}} = 0.200505$$
$$\Delta w^1_{21} = (0.5)(0.00602)(0.1) = 0.000301 \Rightarrow w^1_{21,\text{new}} = 0.300301$$
$$\Delta w^1_{22} = (0.5)(0.00602)(0.2) = 0.000602 \Rightarrow w^1_{22,\text{new}} = 0.400602$$
$$\Delta b^1_1 = 0.5 \cdot 0.00505 = 0.00253, \quad \Delta b^1_2 = 0.5 \cdot 0.00602 = 0.00301$$

**Step 4 — Result Summary (1 mark):** All weights and biases have been updated. The cost decreases after each iteration; after sufficient epochs, the network learns the mapping $x \to 0.5$.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

### Question B
**(a)** Explain the role of activation functions in backpropagation. Compare sigmoid, tanh, and ReLU activation functions in terms of their derivatives and the vanishing gradient problem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **vanishing and exploding gradient problems** in deep neural networks. Explain at least three techniques to mitigate them. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Role of Activation Functions (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** Activation functions introduce non-linearity into the network, enabling it to learn complex patterns. Crucially for backpropagation, the activation function must be **differentiable** so that gradients can be computed.

**Sigmoid (1.5 marks):**
$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}, \quad \sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$$
- Output range: $(0, 1)$.
- **Problem:** $\sigma'(z) \le 0.25$, causing **vanishing gradients** in deep networks.
- Not zero-centered — slows convergence.

**Tanh (1.5 marks):**
$$\tanh(z) = \frac{e^z - e^{-z}}{e^z + e^{-z}}, \quad \tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^2(z)$$
- Output range: $(-1, 1)$ — zero-centered, faster convergence than sigmoid.
- Still suffers from vanishing gradients for large $\vert z \vert$.

**ReLU (1.5 marks):**
$$\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z), \quad \text{ReLU}'(z) = \begin{cases} 1 & z > 0 \\ 0 & z \le 0 \end{cases}$$
- **Derivative is constant (=1) for positive inputs** — solves vanishing gradient for positive activations.
- Computationally efficient.
- **Drawback:** "Dying ReLU" — neurons can get stuck at 0.

**Comparative Table (1 mark):**

| Property | Sigmoid | Tanh | ReLU |
|---|---|---|---|
| Range | $(0,1)$ | $(-1,1)$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| Max Derivative | $0.25$ | $1.0$ | $1.0$ |
| Vanishing Gradient | Severe | Moderate | None (for $z>0$) |
| Computation | Expensive | Expensive | Cheap |
| Zero-Centered | No | Yes | No |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** ReLU is the default choice in modern deep learning, with variants like Leaky ReLU and ELU for specialized use cases.

---

#### Part (b) — Vanishing & Exploding Gradients (7 marks)

**Vanishing Gradient Problem (2 marks):** When backpropagating, gradients are repeatedly multiplied by derivatives of activation functions. For sigmoid, $\sigma'(z) \le 0.25$, so in deep networks, $\delta^l$ shrinks exponentially:
$$\delta^l \propto (0.25)^{L-l} \cdot \delta^L$$
This causes **earlier layers to learn very slowly or not at all**.

**Exploding Gradient Problem (1.5 marks):** When weights are initialized to large values, repeated multiplication causes $\delta^l$ to **grow exponentially**:
$$\delta^l \propto (\text{large weight})^{L-l} \cdot \delta^L$$
This causes unstable training, NaN losses, and weight overflow.

**Mitigation Techniques (3.5 marks total):**

1. **Use ReLU Activation (1 mark):** Since $\text{ReLU}'(z) \in \{0, 1\}$, gradients don't shrink for positive inputs.

2. **Proper Weight Initialization (1 mark):**
   - **Xavier Initialization:** $w \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \frac{2}{n_{in} + n_{out}})$.
   - **He Initialization (for ReLU):** $w \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \frac{2}{n_{in}})$.
   These keep variance stable across layers.

3. **Batch Normalization (0.75 mark):** Normalizes layer inputs to have zero mean and unit variance per mini-batch, stabilizing gradient flow.

4. **Gradient Clipping (0.75 mark):** For exploding gradients, clip the gradient norm:
   $$\mathbf{g} \leftarrow \frac{\mathbf{g} \cdot \text{threshold}}{\vert\vert \mathbf{g} \vert\vert}$$
   if $\vert\vert \mathbf{g} \vert\vert >$ threshold.

5. **Residual Connections / Skip Connections** *(bonus, 0.5 mark):** Used in ResNet architecture to allow gradients to flow directly across layers.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Modern deep learning relies on the combination of these techniques to train networks with hundreds of layers successfully.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the chain rule expansion (–2 marks):** Many students write $\delta^l = \delta^{l+1} \cdot w^{l+1}$ without the Hadamard product and activation derivative. **Always show:** $\delta^l = ((w^{l+1})^T \delta^{l+1}) \odot \sigma'(z^l)$.
>
> 2. **Confusing the order of matrix multiplication (–1.5 marks):** In the hidden layer error, the weight matrix must be **transposed** — students often forget the transpose sign and lose credit.
>
> 3. **Sign errors in weight updates (–1 mark):** The update rule is $w_{new} = w_{old} - \eta \cdot \nabla C$. Forgetting the **negative sign** (or applying it incorrectly) gives wrong numerical values.
>
> 4. **Not using $\odot$ (Hadamard product) and using regular multiplication instead (–1 mark):** Sigmoid derivatives must be multiplied **element-wise**, not as a matrix product.
>
> 5. **Skipping units and not showing the working (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU examiners award step marks. Always show:
>    - Forward pass equations
>    - Numerical computation at each step
>    - Backward pass equations
>    - Final updated weights
>
> 6. **Misinterpreting indices in $w^l_{jk}$ (–1 mark):** $j$ = current layer neuron, $k$ = previous layer neuron. Conflating these gives wrong equations.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning vanishing gradient when using sigmoid (–0.5 mark):** A common KTU question. Always state limitations.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Back Propagation Algorithm**

- **Definition:** BPA is the use of the **chain rule** to efficiently compute gradients of the cost function in a neural network, enabling **gradient-based optimization** of weights.

- **Two Phases:**
  - *Forward Pass:* Compute activations using $a^l = \sigma(w^l a^{l-1} + b^l)$.
  - *Backward Pass:* Compute errors using the four equations, then update weights.

- **The Four Master Equations (memorize the matrix forms):**
  - $\delta^L = \nabla_a C \odot \sigma'(z^L)$
  - $\delta^l = ((w^{l+1})^T \delta^{l+1}) \odot \sigma'(z^l)$
  - $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial b^l} = \delta^l$
  - $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial w^l} = \delta^l (a^{l-1})^T$

- **Update Rule:** $w \leftarrow w - \eta \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial w}$, where $\eta$ is the **learning rate**.

- **Activation Function Derivatives:**
  - Sigmoid: $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ — max value $0.25$.
  - Tanh: $\tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^2(z)$ — max value $1$.
  - ReLU: $\text{ReLU}'(z) = 1$ for $z > 0$, else $0$.

- **Cost Functions:** MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification (preferred with sigmoid output).

- **Vanishing Gradient:** Occurs with sigmoid/tanh in deep networks. Solutions: ReLU, He/Xavier initialization, batch normalization.

- **Exploding Gradient:** Occurs with large weights. Solutions: gradient clipping, proper initialization, batch normalization.

- **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD):** Uses mini-batches for efficient gradient approximation — KTU frequently asks the difference between batch GD, SGD, and mini-batch GD.

- **Local Minima:** Backpropagation with gradient descent can get stuck in local minima. Solutions include momentum, Adam optimizer, random restarts.

- **Epoch vs. Iteration vs. Batch Size:**
  - **Epoch** = one full pass through the entire training set.
  - **Iteration** = one update step.
  - **Batch Size** = number of samples per gradient computation.

- **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT):** Variant of BPA for recurrent neural networks (RNNs) — unrolls the network through time and applies standard BPA.

- **Computational Complexity:** BPA is $O(W)$ where $W$ is the number of weights — a quadratic improvement over naive gradient computation.

- **History:** First formalized in 1970 (Linnainmaa), popularized for neural networks by Rumelhart, Hinton, Williams in **1986** — a frequent KTU short-answer question.

- **Why "Back" Propagation:** The error signal flows from output to input, allowing efficient computation of gradients at all layers in a single backward sweep.

- **Order of Operations in Exam Numerical:**
  1. Compute $z^l$ and $a^l$ for all layers (forward).
  2. Compute loss $C$.
  3. Compute $\delta^L$ at output.
  4. Backpropagate to get $\delta^l$ for all hidden layers.
  5. Compute $\Delta w$ and $\Delta b$.
  6. Apply update: $w_{\text{new}} = w_{\text{old}} - \eta \Delta w$.

> **End of Topic Notes — Back Propagation Algorithm (PCCDT602, Module 3)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised Learning - Clustering: Similarity Measures, Hierarchical Clustering (Agglomerative Clustering), Partitional Clustering, K-Means Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT602 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Unsupervised Learning - Clustering: Similarity Measures, Hierarchical Clustering (Agglomerative Clustering), Partitional Clustering, K-Means Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Unsupervised Learning — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Unsupervised Learning** is a paradigm of Machine Learning in which the model is trained on a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_n\}$ that contains **no labeled outputs** (no target vector $\mathbf{y}$). The objective is to discover hidden structure, intrinsic groupings, or low-dimensional representations directly from the input feature space.

In contrast to supervised learning, there is **no teacher signal** and no reward/punishment mechanism. The algorithm must infer organization purely from the geometric and statistical properties of the data points.

## 1.2 Clustering — The Core Unsupervised Task

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clustering** is the process of partitioning a set of $n$ unlabeled data objects into $k$ groups (clusters) such that objects in the **same group (intra-cluster)** are highly similar to each other, while objects in **different groups (inter-cluster)** are highly dissimilar.

Mathematically, clustering seeks a mapping $C : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$ such that the within-cluster similarity is maximized and the between-cluster similarity is minimized.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Sorting Mangoes Without a Guide"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a fruit vendor receives a basket of **unlabeled** mangoes of varying sizes and ripeness. Without anyone telling him the categories, he instinctively sorts them into:
> 1. A pile of **small, green** ones,
> 2. A pile of **medium, yellowish** ones,
> 3. A pile of **large, fully ripe** ones.
>
> He uses only the *intrinsic features* (size, color, softness) and the *rule* — "similar things go together, dissimilar things go apart." This is exactly what clustering algorithms do mathematically.

**Key terminology** standardized for the KTU 2024 scheme:

- **Intra-cluster distance** — distance between points **within** the same cluster (should be **small**).
- **Inter-cluster distance** — distance between points **across** different clusters (should be **large**).
- **Cluster centroid** — the mean vector of all points belonging to a cluster.
- **Similarity measure** — a function $s(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ that quantifies how alike two points are.
- **Dissimilarity (distance) measure** — a function $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ that quantifies how different two points are (inverse concept to similarity).

## 1.4 Why Clustering Matters — Engineering Relevance

Clustering is foundational in:

- **Customer segmentation** in marketing analytics.
- **Document/topic grouping** in Natural Language Processing.
- **Image compression** via vector quantization.
- **Anomaly detection** (points far from any centroid).
- **Gene expression analysis** in bioinformatics.
- **Image segmentation** in computer vision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 Machine Learning syllabus, **Clustering falls under Unsupervised Learning** and is the principal topic of Module 4, with **Similarity Measures, Hierarchical Clustering, Partitional Clustering, and K-Means** as the four sub-areas of focus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Similarity and Dissimilarity Measures

The choice of similarity/distance function is **the single most important design decision** in any clustering algorithm. Below are the four canonical measures mandated by the KTU syllabus.

### 2.1.1 Euclidean Distance (L2 Norm) — *The Default*

For two $m$-dimensional vectors $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m)$ and $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_m)$:

$$d_{E}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_i - y_i)^2}$$

- Most intuitive, corresponds to the **straight-line distance** in $m$-D space.
- **Sensitive to scale** — features must be normalized (e.g., min-max or z-score) before use.

### 2.1.2 Manhattan Distance (L1 Norm) — *City-Block Distance*

$$d_{M}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$$

- Sum of absolute differences — the distance a taxi would travel on a grid.
- **More robust to outliers** than Euclidean.

### 2.1.3 Minkowski Distance (Lp Norm) — *The Generalized Form*

$$d_{P}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{m} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p}$$

- $p = 1$ reduces to Manhattan, $p = 2$ reduces to Euclidean, $p = \infty$ reduces to Chebyshev distance $\max_i \vert x_i - y_i \vert$.

### 2.1.4 Cosine Similarity — *Directional Measure*

$$s_{C}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\|\mathbf{x}\| \, \|\mathbf{y}\|} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i y_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i^2} \, \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i^2}}$$

- Measures the **angle** between vectors, not magnitude.
- Range: $[-1, +1]$. Used heavily in **text/document clustering**.

> [!NOTE]
> The corresponding **cosine distance** (dissimilarity) is $d_{C} = 1 - s_{C}$.

## 2.2 Properties of a Valid Distance Metric

A function $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a valid metric iff for all points $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z}$:

1. **Non-negativity:** $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \geq 0$
2. **Identity of indiscernibles:** $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = 0 \iff \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{y}$
3. **Symmetry:** $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = d(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{x})$
4. **Triangle inequality:** $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) \leq d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) + d(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z})$

## 2.3 Hierarchical Clustering — Conceptual Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchical Clustering** produces a nested sequence of clusters organized as a **dendrogram** (tree structure). There are two variants:
> 1. **Agglomerative (bottom-up):** Each point starts as its own cluster; pairs are successively merged.
> 2. **Divisive (top-down):** All points start in one cluster; clusters are recursively split.
>
> The KTU syllabus focuses on **Agglomerative Clustering** (also called **AGNES — AGglomerative NESting**).

### Agglomerative Clustering Algorithm Steps

1. Initialize: Treat each of the $n$ data points as a singleton cluster → $n$ clusters.
2. Compute the $n \times n$ **proximity (distance) matrix**.
3. **Repeat** until only one cluster remains:
   a. Merge the two closest clusters $C_i$ and $C_j$.
   b. Update the proximity matrix to reflect the new cluster-to-cluster distances.
4. Output: the dendrogram representing the merging history.

### Linkage Criteria — How to Define "Closest Clusters"

| Linkage Type | Formula (distance between $C_A$ and $C_B$) | Intuition |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Linkage** | $d_{\text{single}}(C_A, C_B) = \min_{\mathbf{x} \in C_A, \mathbf{y} \in C_B} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ | Closest pair — tends to form *chaining* |
| **Complete Linkage** | $d_{\text{complete}}(C_A, C_B) = \max_{\mathbf{x} \in C_A, \mathbf{y} \in C_B} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ | Farthest pair — produces compact, equal-sized clusters |
| **Average Linkage** | $d_{\text{avg}}(C_A, C_B) = \frac{1}{\vert C_A \vert \vert C_B \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_A}\sum_{\mathbf{y} \in C_B} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ | Mean pairwise distance — compromise between single and complete |
| **Ward's Linkage** | Minimizes increase in total within-cluster SSE on merging | Variance-minimizing — produces balanced, tight clusters |

## 2.4 Partitional Clustering

> [!NOTE]
> **Partitional Clustering** produces a **single, flat partition** of the data into $k$ clusters (where $k$ is a user-specified input), *without* a hierarchical structure. The two dominant families are **K-Means** (centroid-based) and **K-Medoids** (medoid-based).

**Comparison with Hierarchical:**

- Hierarchical → dendrogram, no need to pre-specify $k$, $O(n^3)$ or $O(n^2 \log n)$ time.
- Partitional → flat, $k$ must be pre-specified, generally $O(n \cdot k \cdot i)$ (linear in $n$).

## 2.5 K-Means Clustering — The Workhorse

K-Means is the most influential partitional clustering algorithm, attributed to **Lloyd (1957)** and **Forgy (1965)**.

### Objective Function

Given $k$ cluster centroids $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2, \ldots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ and an assignment vector $z_i \in \{1, \ldots, k\}$ for each point $\mathbf{x}_i$, K-Means minimizes the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** also called **inertia**:

$$J(\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_c\}, \{z_i\}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{c=1}^{k} \mathbb{I}(z_i = c) \, \|\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c\|^2$$

where $\mathbb{I}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function (1 if true, 0 otherwise).

### Lloyd's Algorithm — Two-Step Iterative Optimization

**Step 1 — Assignment (Expectation-like step):** Assign each point to the nearest centroid.

$$z_i \leftarrow \arg\min_{c \in \{1, \ldots, k\}} \| \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c \|^2$$

**Step 2 — Update (Maximization-like step):** Recompute each centroid as the mean of all points assigned to it.

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_c \leftarrow \frac{1}{n_c} \sum_{i : z_i = c} \mathbf{x}_i, \quad \text{where } n_c = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{I}(z_i = c)$$

**Iterate Steps 1 and 2 until convergence** (centroids no longer move, or change falls below a tolerance $\epsilon$).

### K-Means Convergence Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The K-Means objective $J$ is **non-increasing** at every iteration and is **bounded below** (by 0). Therefore, Lloyd's algorithm **converges in a finite number of steps** to a local minimum of $J$. (Note: it does *not* guarantee a global minimum.)

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Euclidean distance | $d_E = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_i - y_i)^2}$ | Default; scale-sensitive |
| Manhattan distance | $d_M = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| Minkowski (Lp) | $d_P = \left(\sum \vert x_i - y_i \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalized form |
| Cosine similarity | $s_C = \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\|\mathbf{x}\| \cdot \|\mathbf{y}\|}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$; good for text |
| WCSS (K-Means obj.) | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{c=1}^{k}\mathbb{I}(z_i=c)\|\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c\|^2$ | Minimized by Lloyd's algo |
| Centroid update | $\boldsymbol{\mu}_c = \frac{1}{n_c}\sum_{i:z_i=c}\mathbf{x}_i$ | Mean of assigned points |
| Single linkage | $d_{\min}$ between two clusters | Chaining effect |
| Complete linkage | $d_{\max}$ between two clusters | Compact clusters |
| Elbow method | Plot $J$ vs $k$; choose the "elbow" | Heuristic for choosing $k$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Never use the **vertical pipe `|`** character inside a Markdown table cell. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead, otherwise the table parser breaks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Centroid Update Rule (K-Means M-Step)

We want to find the centroid $\boldsymbol{\mu}_c$ that minimizes the sum of squared distances from all points assigned to cluster $c$. Define:

$$J_c(\boldsymbol{\mu}_c) = \sum_{i : z_i = c} \| \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c \|^2$$

Expanding the squared norm:

$$J_c(\boldsymbol{\mu}_c) = \sum_{i : z_i = c} \left( \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c \right)^{\top} \left( \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c \right)$$

$$= \sum_{i : z_i = c} \left( \mathbf{x}_i^{\top} \mathbf{x}_i - 2 \mathbf{x}_i^{\top} \boldsymbol{\mu}_c + \boldsymbol{\mu}_c^{\top} \boldsymbol{\mu}_c \right)$$

Now take the gradient with respect to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_c$ and set it to zero:

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\mu}_c} J_c = \sum_{i : z_i = c} \left( -2 \mathbf{x}_i + 2 \boldsymbol{\mu}_c \right) = 0$$

Solving for $\boldsymbol{\mu}_c$:

$$2 n_c \boldsymbol{\mu}_c = 2 \sum_{i : z_i = c} \mathbf{x}_i \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_c = \frac{1}{n_c} \sum_{i : z_i = c} \mathbf{x}_i$$

This is the **mean of all points in cluster $c$** — the optimal centroid in the least-squares sense.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — K-Means on 2-D Data

Let us trace K-Means on a small dataset with $k = 2$:

| Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 1 | 1 |
| $P_2$ | 1 | 2 |
| $P_3$ | 2 | 1 |
| $P_4$ | 8 | 8 |
| $P_5$ | 9 | 9 |
| $P_6$ | 8 | 9 |

**Initialization (Forgy method):** randomly pick $P_1 = (1,1)$ and $P_4 = (8,8)$ as initial centroids.

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)} = (1, 1), \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(0)} = (8, 8)$$

### Iteration 1 — Assignment Step

For each point, compute squared Euclidean distance to each centroid and assign to the nearest.

**For $P_1 = (1,1)$:**

$$d^2(P_1, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1) = (1-1)^2 + (1-1)^2 = 0$$

$$d^2(P_1, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = (1-8)^2 + (1-8)^2 = 49 + 49 = 98$$

→ Assign to **Cluster 1**.

**For $P_4 = (8,8)$:** (the reverse) → Assign to **Cluster 2**.

**For $P_2 = (1,2)$:**

$$d^2(P_2, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1) = (1-1)^2 + (2-1)^2 = 1$$

$$d^2(P_2, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = (1-8)^2 + (2-8)^2 = 49 + 36 = 85$$

→ Assign to **Cluster 1**.

**For $P_3 = (2,1)$:**

$$d^2(P_3, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1) = (2-1)^2 + (1-1)^2 = 1$$

$$d^2(P_3, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = (2-8)^2 + (1-8)^2 = 36 + 49 = 85$$

→ Assign to **Cluster 1**.

**For $P_5 = (9,9)$:**

$$d^2(P_5, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1) = (9-1)^2 + (9-1)^2 = 64 + 64 = 128$$

$$d^2(P_5, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = (9-8)^2 + (9-8)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$$

→ Assign to **Cluster 2**.

**For $P_6 = (8,9)$:**

$$d^2(P_6, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1) = (8-1)^2 + (9-1)^2 = 49 + 64 = 113$$

$$d^2(P_6, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = (8-8)^2 + (9-8)^2 = 0 + 1 = 1$$

→ Assign to **Cluster 2**.

**Iteration 1 Assignment Summary:**

- Cluster 1: $\{P_1, P_2, P_3\}$
- Cluster 2: $\{P_4, P_5, P_6\}$

### Iteration 1 — Update Step

Recompute centroids as the mean of assigned points:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(1,1) + (1,2) + (2,1)\right] = \frac{1}{3}(4, 4) = (1.33, 1.33)$$

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(8,8) + (9,9) + (8,9)\right] = \frac{1}{3}(25, 26) = (8.33, 8.67)$$

### Iteration 2 — Re-assignment

Compute centroid shift:

$$\|\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)}\| = \sqrt{(1.33-1)^2 + (1.33-1)^2} = \sqrt{0.22} \approx 0.47$$

This is non-trivial, so we continue. Repeating the assignment step with the new centroids yields the **same cluster membership** (no point changes group). The algorithm has converged.

**Final WCSS:**

$$J = 3 \cdot \|\mathbf{x} - (1.33, 1.33)\|^2 + 3 \cdot \|\mathbf{x} - (8.33, 8.67)\|^2$$

$$J \approx 0.67 + 0.67 + 0.67 + 0.67 + 0.67 + 1.33 \approx 4.67$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — K-Means vs. Agglomerative

| Property | K-Means (Partitional) | Agglomerative (Hierarchical) |
|---|---|---|
| Cluster shape assumption | Spherical, equal-size | Arbitrary |
| Number of clusters $k$ | Must be specified | Determined post-hoc from dendrogram |
| Time complexity | $O(n \cdot k \cdot i \cdot d)$ | $O(n^3)$ naïve, $O(n^2 \log n)$ optimized |
| Space complexity | $O(n \cdot d + k \cdot d)$ | $O(n^2)$ for the distance matrix |
| Sensitivity to initialization | High — multiple restarts advised | None (deterministic) |
| Sensitivity to outliers | High (centroid pulled) | Moderate (depends on linkage) |
| Scalability to large $n$ | Excellent | Poor |
| Result reproducibility | Stochastic (depends on init) | Deterministic |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Clustering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawData["Raw Unlabeled Dataset Xn by d"] --> norm["Preprocessing Normalization Scaling"]
    norm --> sim["Similarity Measure Selection Euclidean or Cosine"]
    sim --> algo["Choose Algorithm Type"]
    algo --> part["Partitional K Means K Medoids"]
    algo --> hier["Hierarchical Agglomerative Divisive"]
    part --> km["K Means Lloyd Iteration Assign and Update Centroids"]
    hier --> ag["Agglomerative Single Complete Average Ward Linkage"]
    km --> out1["Flat Partition K Clusters Centroids"]
    ag --> out2["Dendrogram Tree of Merges"]
    out1 --> eval["Cluster Validation Elbow Silhouette"]
    out2 --> eval
    eval --> final["Final Cluster Labels"]
```

## 4.2 K-Means Iterative Process Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Input X1 to Xn Choose K"] --> initA["Initialize K Centroids Forgy or Random"]
    initA --> assignStep["Assignment Step Assign Xi to nearest Centroid"]
    assignStep --> updateStep["Update Step Recompute Centroid as Mean"]
    updateStep --> checkConverge{"Centroids Unchanged or below tolerance"}
    checkConverge --> assignStep
    checkConverge --> outputA["Output Final Clusters and Centroids"]
```

## 4.3 Agglomerative Clustering — Merging Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["Step 0 N Singleton Clusters One per Point"] --> step2["Step 1 Compute N by N Distance Matrix"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 2 Find Two Closest Clusters via Linkage Criterion"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 3 Merge the Two Closest Clusters"]
    step4 --> step5["Step 4 Update Distance Matrix with Linkage Rule"]
    step5 --> checkN{"Number of Clusters equals 1"}
    checkN --> step3
    checkN --> step6["Step 5 Cut Dendrogram at Desired Level to obtain K Clusters"]
```

## 4.4 Cluster Taxonomy Overview

```mermaid

    cluster["Clustering Algorithms"] --> part["Partitional Clustering"]
    cluster --> hier["Hierarchical Clustering"]
    cluster --> dens["Density Based DBSCAN OPTICS"]
    cluster --> grid["Grid Based STING CLIQUE"]
    cluster --> model["Model Based GMM EM"]
    part --> km["K Means"]
    part --> kmed["K Medoids PAM"]
    hier --> agnes["Agglomerative AGNES"]
    hier --> diana["Divisive DIANA"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish between supervised and unsupervised learning. Give two real-world applications of clustering.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|---|---|---|
| Labeled data? | Yes (features + targets) | No (features only) |
| Goal | Learn mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ | Discover structure in $\mathcal{X}$ |
| Evaluation | Accuracy, MSE, F1 | Silhouette, WCSS, Davies–Bouldin |
| Examples | Classification, Regression | Clustering, Dimensionality Reduction |

**Two real-world applications of clustering:**
1. **Customer segmentation** — grouping buyers by purchase behavior for targeted marketing.
2. **Document organization** — clustering news articles by topic using TF-IDF and cosine similarity.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]`

**Q: Define the cosine similarity measure. State its range and explain why it is preferred over Euclidean distance for text clustering.**

**Model Answer:**

$$s_C(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\|\mathbf{x}\|\|\mathbf{y}\|}$$

- **Range:** $[-1, +1]$ (1 = identical direction, 0 = orthogonal, $-1$ = opposite).
- **Why preferred for text:**
  1. Text vectors are **high-dimensional and sparse**; Euclidean distance is dominated by zeros.
  2. Cosine measures **angular alignment** — two documents of different lengths on the same topic still have cosine $\approx 1$.
  3. It is **invariant to scaling** of vectors (magnitudes are normalized away).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: each Module contains a full 14-mark question. The student answers **one full question** from the module (which may have two sub-parts of 7 marks each). We provide **TWO alternative choices** below.

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering** algorithm in detail. Illustrate the **single, complete, and average linkage** criteria with suitable formulas and a note on the chaining effect.

**Model Solution:**

**Agglomerative clustering** builds a hierarchy bottom-up.

1. **Initialization:** Start with $n$ clusters, each containing one data point.
2. **Proximity matrix:** Compute the $n \times n$ matrix of pairwise distances.
3. **Iterative merging:** At each step, find the pair of clusters with the smallest inter-cluster distance, merge them, and update the matrix.
4. **Termination:** Stop when a single cluster containing all points remains (or when a user-specified number of clusters is reached).
5. **Output:** A **dendrogram** — a binary tree showing the merge order and the distance at which each merge occurred.

**Linkage Criteria — Formulas:**

For two clusters $C_A$ and $C_B$:

- **Single linkage:** $L_{\text{single}}(C_A, C_B) = \min_{\mathbf{x} \in C_A, \mathbf{y} \in C_B} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$
- **Complete linkage:** $L_{\text{complete}}(C_A, C_B) = \max_{\mathbf{x} \in C_A, \mathbf{y} \in C_B} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$
- **Average linkage:** $L_{\text{avg}}(C_A, C_B) = \frac{1}{\vert C_A \vert \cdot \vert C_B \vert}\sum_{\mathbf{x}\in C_A}\sum_{\mathbf{y}\in C_B} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$

**Chaining effect:** Single linkage uses only the *minimum* pairwise distance, so clusters can be "linked" through a chain of nearby points, producing long, straggly, non-compact clusters. Complete and average linkage avoid this by considering the worst-case or average-case separation, yielding more spherical and balanced clusters.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Elbow Method** for determining the optimal number of clusters in K-Means. How does the Silhouette Coefficient complement this method?

**Model Solution:**

**Elbow Method:**

1. Run K-Means for $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, K_{\max}$.
2. For each $k$, record the **WCSS (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares)**:

$$J(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{c=1}^{k} \mathbb{I}(z_i = c)\, \|\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c\|^2$$

3. Plot $J(k)$ vs $k$.
4. The **optimal $k$** is at the "elbow" — the point where adding more clusters yields only a marginal reduction in $J$. Beyond the elbow, clusters begin to fragment and overfit.

**Silhouette Coefficient — complementary validation:**

For each point $\mathbf{x}_i$, let $a_i$ = mean distance to all other points in its own cluster, and $b_i$ = mean distance to all points in the *nearest* other cluster. Then:

$$s(i) = \frac{b_i - a_i}{\max(a_i, b_i)} \in [-1, +1]$$

The **overall silhouette score** is the mean of $s(i)$ across all points. The $k$ that **maximizes** this score is considered optimal. Unlike the elbow method (which is visual/subjective), the silhouette provides a **scalar quantitative** measure of clustering quality — high values indicate well-separated, compact clusters.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the **K-Means clustering algorithm**. State its objective function and prove the centroid update rule.

**Model Solution:**

**Flowchart** (see Section 4.2 of these notes for the Mermaid schematic — reproduced in words here):

1. Choose $k$.
2. Initialize $k$ centroids.
3. **Repeat** until convergence:
   - **Assignment step:** assign each $\mathbf{x}_i$ to nearest centroid.
   - **Update step:** recompute each centroid as the cluster mean.
4. **Output:** final assignments and centroids.

**Objective function** (WCSS):

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{c=1}^{k}\mathbb{I}(z_i = c)\,\|\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c\|^2$$

**Proof of centroid update rule:** (full derivation in Section 3.1)

Differentiate $J_c(\boldsymbol{\mu}_c) = \sum_{i:z_i=c}\|\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c\|^2$ with respect to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_c$ and set to zero:

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\mu}_c}J_c = -2\sum_{i:z_i=c}\mathbf{x}_i + 2n_c\boldsymbol{\mu}_c = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boldsymbol{\mu}_c = \frac{1}{n_c}\sum_{i:z_i=c}\mathbf{x}_i$$

Hence, the optimal centroid is the **arithmetic mean** of the points currently assigned to the cluster.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given the 2-D dataset below, perform **one full iteration** of K-Means with $k = 2$ and initial centroids $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (2, 10)$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (5, 8)$. Use Euclidean distance. Compute the new centroids.

| Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| $A$ | 2 | 10 |
| $B$ | 2 | 5 |
| $C$ | 8 | 4 |
| $D$ | 5 | 8 |
| $E$ | 7 | 5 |
| $F$ | 6 | 4 |

**Model Solution:**

**Initial centroids:** $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (2, 10)$, $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (5, 8)$.

**Step 1 — Assignment using Euclidean distance:**

For $A(2,10)$: $d(A,\mu_1) = 0$, $d(A,\mu_2) = \sqrt{9 + 4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$ → **Cluster 1**.

For $B(2,5)$: $d(B,\mu_1) = \sqrt{0 + 25} = 5$, $d(B,\mu_2) = \sqrt{9 + 9} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.24$ → **Cluster 2**.

For $C(8,4)$: $d(C,\mu_1) = \sqrt{36 + 36} = \sqrt{72} \approx 8.49$, $d(C,\mu_2) = \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5$ → **Cluster 2**.

For $D(5,8)$: $d(D,\mu_1) = \sqrt{9 + 4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$, $d(D,\mu_2) = 0$ → **Cluster 2**.

For $E(7,5)$: $d(E,\mu_1) = \sqrt{25 + 25} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07$, $d(E,\mu_2) = \sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$ → **Cluster 2**.

For $F(6,4)$: $d(F,\mu_1) = \sqrt{16 + 36} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.21$, $d(F,\mu_2) = \sqrt{1 + 16} = \sqrt{17} \approx 4.12$ → **Cluster 2**.

**Cluster 1:** $\{A\}$, **Cluster 2:** $\{B, C, D, E, F\}$.

**Step 2 — Centroid Update:**

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{\text{new}} = A = (2, 10)$$

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{5}\left[(2,5) + (8,4) + (5,8) + (7,5) + (6,4)\right] = \frac{1}{5}(28, 26) = (5.6, 5.2)$$

**New centroids:** $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (2, 10)$, $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (5.6, 5.2)$. **WCSS after this iteration:**

$$J = 0 + \left[(2-5.6)^2 + (5-5.2)^2\right] + \cdots \approx 32.4$$

The next iteration would re-assign points, but only one iteration is required by the question.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to show the centroid derivation** — many students only state the formula. Always show $\nabla J = 0$ and the resulting mean computation.
> 2. **Mixing up linkage types** — single vs. complete vs. average linkage is a frequent 3-mark question. Memorize the min/max/mean definitions.
> 3. **Confusing similarity with distance** — cosine *similarity* is high when documents are similar, but cosine *distance* $= 1 - $ similarity is what enters the K-Means objective.
> 4. **Not normalizing features** before Euclidean K-Means — features with larger scales will dominate the distance.
> 5. **Stating "K-Means always converges" without the caveat** — it converges to a *local* minimum, not necessarily the global one.
> 6. **Skipping the convergence proof** — for 7-mark questions, derivations carry substantial weight.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Unsupervised learning** uses **no labels**; the goal is structure discovery.
- **Clustering** partitions data so that intra-cluster similarity $\uparrow$ and inter-cluster similarity $\downarrow$.
- **Euclidean, Manhattan, Minkowski, Cosine** are the four canonical similarity/distance measures in the KTU syllabus.
- A valid distance metric must satisfy **non-negativity, identity, symmetry, triangle inequality**.
- **Agglomerative clustering** is bottom-up; produces a **dendrogram**; uses **single/complete/average/Ward** linkage.
- **Partitional clustering** produces a **flat** partition; $k$ must be specified in advance.
- **K-Means** minimizes **WCSS (inertia)** via Lloyd's two-step iteration: **(i) assign to nearest centroid, (ii) update centroid as mean**.
- The optimal centroid is provably the **arithmetic mean** of cluster members.
- K-Means converges in finite steps to a **local minimum** (not global).
- K-Means assumes **spherical, equal-sized** clusters and is **sensitive to initialization and outliers**.
- The **Elbow Method** and **Silhouette Coefficient** are the two principal heuristics for choosing $k$.
- Cosine similarity is preferred for **text/high-dimensional sparse** data.
- **Single linkage** suffers from the **chaining effect**; complete and Ward linkage produce more compact clusters.
- K-Means complexity is $O(n \cdot k \cdot i \cdot d)$; agglomerative is $O(n^3)$ naïve.
- **Always normalize/standardize features** before applying distance-based clustering.
- For KTU 2024, focus on **derivations (centroid update), linkage formulas, K-Means worked example, and the elbow/silhouette methods**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensionality Reduction - Principal Component Analysis, Multidimensional Scaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT602 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dimensionality Reduction - Principal Component Analysis, Multidimensional Scaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Dimensionality Reduction — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dimensionality Reduction** is the mathematical process of transforming high-dimensional data $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times D}$ (where $D$ is large) into a lower-dimensional representation $\mathbf{Y} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ (where $k \ll D$) while preserving the most *informative structural properties* of the original data — such as variance, pairwise distances, neighbourhood topology, or local manifold geometry.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCDT602 — Machine Learning)**, dimensionality reduction is positioned as a **pre-processing and visualization pillar** of unsupervised learning, with two flagship linear techniques mandated in Module 4:

1. **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** — *Variance-preserving projection*
2. **Multidimensional Scaling (MDS)** — *Distance-preserving embedding*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shadow" Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine a 3D transparent sculpture placed under a bright lamp on a white wall. The **shadow** it casts on the wall is a **2D representation** of the 3D object. The shadow loses one dimension (depth), but it still conveys the *shape, spread, and orientation* of the original object.
>
> - **PCA** chooses the *orientation* of the lamp so that the shadow is the *most spread-out* (maximum variance) — it tries to retain as much "spread" of the data as possible.
> - **MDS** chooses the lamp orientation so that the *distances between any two shadow points* best match the *real 3D distances* between the original points.
>
> Both are *projections*, but they optimize **different objectives** — that is the crucial difference.

---

## 1.3 The Curse of Dimensionality — Why Reduce Dimensions?

| Phenomenon | Effect in High Dimensions | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Data sparsity | Volume of space grows exponentially with $D$ | Points become equidistant — no clustering meaningful |
| Computational cost | Distance calculations scale as $O(D)$ per pair | KNN, K-Means become slow |
| Overfitting | Number of features $\approx$ number of samples | Model memorizes noise |
| Visualization | Humans can visualize only 2D or 3D | Cannot inspect high-D clusters |
| Storage / Memory | $n \times D$ matrix dominates RAM | Infeasible for streaming data |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"Why is dimensionality reduction needed?"*, the **three keyword answers** are: (i) **Curse of Dimensionality**, (ii) **Visualization**, (iii) **Computational Efficiency / Noise Reduction**.

---

## 1.4 Formal Definitions of the Two Techniques

### Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Pearson, 1901; Hotelling, 1933):** PCA is an **orthogonal linear transformation** that maps the data onto a new coordinate system such that the **greatest variance** lies along the **first axis** (the *first principal component*), the **second greatest variance** along the **second axis**, and so on. The new axes are the **eigenvectors of the data covariance matrix**.

### Multidimensional Scaling (MDS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Torgerson, 1952):** MDS is a **non-parametric embedding technique** that finds a low-dimensional configuration of $n$ points whose **pairwise Euclidean distances** best match a given matrix of **dissimilarities** (or distances) in the original high-dimensional space. The classical (metric) MDS solution is obtained via the **eigendecomposition of a double-centered distance-squared matrix**.

---

## 1.5 Side-by-Side Comparison (Quick Glance)

| Attribute | **PCA** | **MDS** |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Raw data matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times D}$ | Dissimilarity matrix $\mathbf{D} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ |
| **What it preserves** | Maximum **variance** (global structure) | Pairwise **distances** (inter-point geometry) |
| **Objective function** | Maximize $\text{Var}(\mathbf{Y})$ | Minimize **Stress** = $\sum_{ij}(d_{ij} - \delta_{ij})^2$ |
| **Mathematical core** | Eigendecomposition of **Covariance matrix** | Eigendecomposition of **double-centered Gram matrix** |
| **Requires raw data?** | **Yes** (computes covariance) | **No** (only distances needed) |
| **Linearity** | Linear | Linear (classical MDS); non-linear (non-metric) |
| **Typical use** | Feature compression, denoising, whitening | Visualization, psychometrics, similarity analysis |

---

## 1.6 Module 4 Roadmap in KTU Syllabus

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 PCCDT602 syllabus for **Module 4** lists the following sub-topics in order:
> 1. Need for Dimensionality Reduction
> 2. **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** — derivation, algorithm, reconstruction
> 3. **Multidimensional Scaling (MDS)** — classical / metric MDS, stress function
> 4. Brief mention of extensions (t-SNE, Isomap) — usually 1-mark questions

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principal Component Analysis (PCA) — Theory Breakdown

PCA answers one core question: *Given a centred data cloud, in which direction does the data vary the most?*

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Mean-Centring (Pre-processing — non-negotiable):**  
   Subtract the column means so that every feature has zero mean.  
   $$\mathbf{x}_i^{\text{centred}} = \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbf{x}_i$$
   > If you skip this, the first principal component will simply point toward the mean, which is meaningless.

2. **Covariance Matrix Construction:**  
   The covariance matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma} \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times D}$ captures how features co-vary.
   $$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c$$
   where $\mathbf{X}_c$ is the mean-centred data matrix.

3. **Eigendecomposition:**  
   Solve the characteristic equation for eigenvalues $\lambda_k$ and eigenvectors $\mathbf{v}_k$:
   $$\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{v}_k = \lambda_k \mathbf{v}_k, \quad \|\mathbf{v}_k\| = 1$$
   The eigenvectors are mutually orthogonal (because $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ is symmetric positive semi-definite).

4. **Variance Sort and Rank Selection:**  
   Sort eigenpairs in descending order of $\lambda$. Choose top $k$ components such that the *cumulative explained variance ratio* exceeds a threshold (commonly 0.95):
   $$\text{Cumulative Variance Retained} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{D}\lambda_i} \geq 0.95$$

5. **Projection (Dimensionality Reduction):**  
   Construct the projection matrix $\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_k]$ and project:
   $$\mathbf{Y}_{n \times k} = \mathbf{X}_{c, n \times D} \, \mathbf{W}_{D \times k}$$

6. **Reconstruction (Inverse Mapping):**  
   $$\hat{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{Y}\mathbf{W}^{\top} + \boldsymbol{\mu}$$
   The **reconstruction error** is:
   $$J = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\|\mathbf{x}_i - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i\|^2 = \sum_{j=k+1}^{D}\lambda_j$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core "Why" of PCA:** Eigenvectors of the covariance matrix = directions of maximum variance. Eigenvalues = the amount of variance along those directions. By keeping only the eigenvectors with the largest eigenvalues, we **retain the most variance** in the lowest possible number of dimensions.

---

## 2.2 Multidimensional Scaling (MDS) — Theory Breakdown

MDS answers a different question: *Given only a table of pairwise distances $\delta_{ij}$, reconstruct coordinates $\mathbf{y}_i$ in a low-D space that reproduce those distances?*

### Variants of MDS

| Variant | Preserves | Objective |
|---|---|---|
| **Classical / Metric MDS** (Torgerson) | Exact Euclidean distances (when possible) | Minimize $\sum_{ij}(d_{ij}^{(Y)} - \delta_{ij})^2$ |
| **Non-metric MDS** (Kruskal) | Ordinal ranking of distances | Minimize $\sum_{ij}(\theta(d_{ij}^{(Y)}) - \delta_{ij})^2$ with monotone regression |
| **Weighted MDS** | Per-pair weighted distances | Weighted stress |

### Algorithm: Classical MDS (the KTU-relevant version)

1. **Start with the squared distance matrix** $\mathbf{D}^{(2)}$ where $D^{(2)}_{ij} = \delta_{ij}^{2}$.

2. **Apply double centring** using the centring matrix $\mathbf{J} = \mathbf{I} - \frac{1}{n}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^{\top}$:
   $$\mathbf{B} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{J}\mathbf{D}^{(2)}\mathbf{J}$$
   $\mathbf{B}$ is the **Gram matrix** of the inner products of the (centred) data points.

3. **Eigendecompose $\mathbf{B}$:**  
   $$\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{\Lambda}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$$
   where $\mathbf{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_n)$.

4. **Select top $k$ positive eigenvalues** $\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_k$ and corresponding eigenvectors $\mathbf{v}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_k$.

5. **Compute the embedding:**
   $$\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{V}_k \, \mathbf{\Lambda}_k^{1/2}$$
   Equivalently, $Y_{ij} = \sqrt{\lambda_j}\, V_{ij}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "double centring"?** Subtracting the row-mean and column-mean of $\mathbf{D}^{(2)}$ from a distance-squared matrix yields inner products of centred vectors. This is the algebraic bridge between *distances* and *coordinates*.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Every formula below has appeared (or is a direct variant) in KTU question papers.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean of $j$-th feature | $\mu_j = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_{ij}$ | Scalar |
| 2 | Centred data | $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu}\mathbf{1}^{\top}$ | $n \times D$ matrix |
| 3 | Covariance matrix (PCA) | $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c$ | $D \times D$ symmetric PSD |
| 4 | Eigen equation (PCA) | $\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}$ | $\lambda \geq 0$ (PSD) |
| 5 | Proportion of variance (PC $k$) | $\eta_k = \frac{\lambda_k}{\sum_{i=1}^{D}\lambda_i}$ | Unitless, in $[0,1]$ |
| 6 | PCA projection | $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W}$ | $n \times k$ |
| 7 | Reconstruction error (PCA) | $J = \sum_{j=k+1}^{D}\lambda_j$ | Squared-distance units |
| 8 | Centring matrix | $\mathbf{J} = \mathbf{I} - \frac{1}{n}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^{\top}$ | $n \times n$ idempotent |
| 9 | Double-centred matrix (MDS) | $\mathbf{B} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{J}\mathbf{D}^{(2)}\mathbf{J}$ | Symmetric |
| 10 | Classical MDS embedding | $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{V}_k \mathbf{\Lambda}_k^{1/2}$ | $n \times k$ |
| 11 | MDS Stress (Kruskal) | $\text{Stress} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{ij}(d_{ij}-\delta_{ij})^2}{\sum_{ij}\delta_{ij}^2}}$ | Unitless, lower is better |
| 12 | Explained variance ratio | $\text{EVR}_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{D}\lambda_i}$ | In $[0,1]$, target $\geq 0.95$ |

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PCA from the Max-Variance Principle

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times D}$ be the mean-centred data (so $\sum_i \mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{0}$).  
We seek a **unit vector** $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$ ($\|\mathbf{v}\| = 1$) such that the **variance of the projected data** is maximized.

The projection of $\mathbf{x}_i$ onto $\mathbf{v}$ is the scalar:
$$z_i = \mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i$$

The variance of $z_i$ across all $n$ samples is:
$$\text{Var}(z) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(z_i - \bar{z})^2$$

Since the data is mean-centred, $\bar{z} = \mathbf{v}^{\top}\boldsymbol{\mu} = 0$, so:
$$\text{Var}(z) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(\mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i)^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i\mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{v}$$

Pulling $\mathbf{v}$ out of the sum (it is constant w.r.t. $i$):
$$\text{Var}(z) = \mathbf{v}^{\top}\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbf{x}_i\mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\right]\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{v}$$

where $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$ is the (population) covariance matrix. *(We will switch to $\frac{1}{n-1}$ for the sample version, but the derivation is identical.)*

### 3.1.2 Constrained Optimization

We now solve:
$$\max_{\mathbf{v}} \quad \mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{v} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{v} = 1$$

Using the **Lagrange multiplier** $\lambda$:
$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{v}, \lambda) = \mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{v} - \lambda(\mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{v} - 1)$$

Differentiating with respect to $\mathbf{v}$ and setting the gradient to zero:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{v}} = 2\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{v} - 2\lambda\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$

This is the **defining eigenvalue equation**:
$$\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}$$

The maximum variance is achieved by taking the **eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue**. The maximum value itself is:
$$\text{Var}(z^*) = \mathbf{v}^{*\top}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{v}^{*} = \mathbf{v}^{*\top}\lambda^{*}\mathbf{v}^{*} = \lambda^{*}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Deduction:** The eigenvalue $\lambda$ *is* the variance captured by that principal component. Eigenvectors of the covariance matrix are aligned with the directions of maximal variance. This is the complete first-principles derivation of PCA.

### 3.1.3 Multiple Components

For the top $k$ components, the eigenvectors $\mathbf{v}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_k$ are stacked into a matrix $\mathbf{W}$. The total variance retained is:
$$\text{Var}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=1}^{k}\lambda_j$$

This is a **maximization** because the spectral theorem guarantees that the $k$ largest eigenvalues maximize the trace of $\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{W}$ among all rank-$k$ orthogonal projections.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Classical MDS from the Distance Matrix

### 3.2.1 Goal

Given squared Euclidean distances $\delta_{ij}^{2}$ between $n$ points in an unknown $D$-dimensional space, recover the centred coordinate matrix $\mathbf{Y} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$.

### 3.2.2 From Distances to Inner Products

The squared Euclidean distance between $\mathbf{y}_i$ and $\mathbf{y}_j$ is:
$$\delta_{ij}^{2} = \|\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j\|^2 = \|\mathbf{y}_i\|^2 + \|\mathbf{y}_j\|^2 - 2\mathbf{y}_i^{\top}\mathbf{y}_j$$

Let us denote the *Gram matrix* (matrix of inner products) as $\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{Y}\mathbf{Y}^{\top}$, with entries $b_{ij} = \mathbf{y}_i^{\top}\mathbf{y}_j$. Expanding:
$$\delta_{ij}^{2} = b_{ii} + b_{jj} - 2b_{ij}$$

### 3.2.3 Double Centring Trick

**Row sums:** Sum the above over $i$:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n}\delta_{ij}^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}b_{ii} + n b_{jj} - 2\sum_{i=1}^{n}b_{ij}$$

If the rows of $\mathbf{Y}$ are centred (i.e., $\sum_i \mathbf{y}_i = \mathbf{0}$), then $\sum_i b_{ij} = \sum_i \mathbf{y}_i^{\top}\mathbf{y}_j = 0$, so:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n}\delta_{ij}^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}b_{ii} + n b_{jj} = \text{tr}(\mathbf{B}) + n b_{jj}$$

**Column sums:** Similarly, summing over $j$ and using $\sum_j b_{ij} = 0$:
$$\sum_{j=1}^{n}\delta_{ij}^{2} = n b_{ii} + \text{tr}(\mathbf{B})$$

**Grand sum:** Summing over both $i$ and $j$:
$$\sum_{i,j}\delta_{ij}^{2} = 2n\,\text{tr}(\mathbf{B})$$

### 3.2.4 Recovering $b_{ij}$

Solving the system for $b_{ij}$:
$$b_{ij} = -\frac{1}{2}\left[\delta_{ij}^{2} - \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i'}\delta_{i'j}^{2} - \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j'}\delta_{ij'}^{2} + \frac{1}{n^{2}}\sum_{i',j'}\delta_{i'j'}^{2}\right]$$

In matrix form, this is exactly the **double centring** operation:
$$\mathbf{B} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{J}\mathbf{D}^{(2)}\mathbf{J}, \quad \mathbf{J} = \mathbf{I} - \frac{1}{n}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^{\top}$$

where $\mathbf{D}^{(2)}$ has entries $\delta_{ij}^{2}$ and $\mathbf{J}$ is the centring matrix.

### 3.2.5 Spectral Decomposition for Coordinates

$\mathbf{B}$ is symmetric positive semi-definite (it is a Gram matrix). Eigendecompose:
$$\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{\Lambda}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$$

If the original data is in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$, only $k$ eigenvalues are positive. Take the top $k$:
$$\mathbf{B} \approx \mathbf{V}_k \mathbf{\Lambda}_k \mathbf{V}_k^{\top}$$

Then the centred embedding is:
$$\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{V}_k \mathbf{\Lambda}_k^{1/2}$$

where $\mathbf{\Lambda}_k^{1/2} = \text{diag}(\sqrt{\lambda_1}, \ldots, \sqrt{\lambda_k})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection to PCA:** If you start with raw data $\mathbf{X}$ and compute squared Euclidean distances $D_{ij}^{(2)} = \|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2$, then the classical MDS solution $\mathbf{Y}$ is **identical** (up to rotation/reflection) to the PCA projection. This is a beautiful theorem often tested in KTU exams.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (PCA on 2D Data)

**Data (mean-centred):**
$$\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \\ 4 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad n = 4, \quad D = 2$$

**Step 1: Covariance matrix**
$$\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 1+4+9+16 & 2+2+12+12 \\ 2+2+12+12 & 4+1+16+9 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 30 & 28 \\ 28 & 30 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 30 & 28 \\ 28 & 30 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 9.333 \\ 9.333 & 10 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Characteristic equation**
$$\det(\mathbf{\Sigma} - \lambda\mathbf{I}) = (10-\lambda)^2 - 9.333^2 = 0$$
$$\lambda = 10 \pm 9.333$$
$$\lambda_1 = 19.333, \quad \lambda_2 = 0.667$$

**Step 3: First eigenvector** (for $\lambda_1 = 19.333$)
$$\begin{bmatrix} -9.333 & 9.333 \\ 9.333 & -9.333 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} \;\Rightarrow\; v_1 = v_2$$
Normalizing: $\mathbf{v}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 4: Variance explained**
$$\text{EVR}_1 = \frac{19.333}{19.333 + 0.667} = 0.9666 = 96.66\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The first principal component captures **96.66%** of the variance — projecting the data onto a single axis preserves almost all information. This is typical for data with strong diagonal correlation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | **PCA** | **Classical MDS** |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | $\mathbf{X}_{n \times D}$ | $\mathbf{D}^{(2)}_{n \times n}$ |
| **Core matrix** | $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$ | $\mathbf{B} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{J}\mathbf{D}^{(2)}\mathbf{J}$ |
| **Eigendecomposition of** | $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ | $\mathbf{B}$ |
| **Output coordinates** | $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W}$ | $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{V}_k \mathbf{\Lambda}_k^{1/2}$ |
| **Rank of core matrix** | $\leq \min(n-1, D)$ | $\leq n-1$ |
| **Identical when** | $\mathbf{D}^{(2)}$ computed from $\mathbf{X}$ with Euclidean metric | — |
| **Sensitive to scaling** | Yes (must standardize features) | No (operates on distances) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PCA Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the full data flow of PCA, from raw high-dimensional input to a low-dimensional projection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Matrix X] --> B[Step 1: Feature Standardisation]
    B --> C[Step 2: Mean Centring: Xc = X minus mu]
    C --> D[Step 3: Covariance Matrix: Sigma = XcT Xc by n minus 1]
    D --> E[Step 4: Eigendecomposition of Sigma]
    E --> F[Step 5: Sort Eigenpairs by Descending Lambda]
    F --> G[Step 6: Choose Top k Components by EVR Threshold 0.95]
    G --> H[Step 7: Form Projection Matrix W from Top k Eigenvectors]
    H --> I[Step 8: Compute Low-D Embedding Y = Xc W]
    I --> J[Final Output: Y in Rn times Rk]
    E --> K[Rejected Eigenvectors: lambda k plus 1 to lambda D]
    K --> L[Reconstruction Error: J = sum of rejected lambdas]
```

## 4.2 MDS Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Dissimilarity Matrix D] --> Q[Step 1: Square the Entries: D2 = delta ij squared]
    Q --> R[Step 2: Build Centring Matrix: J = I minus 1 over n times 11T]
    R --> S[Step 3: Double Centring: B = minus 0.5 times J times D2 times J]
    S --> T[Step 4: Eigendecomposition of B]
    T --> U[Step 5: Filter Top k Positive Eigenvalues]
    U --> V[Step 6: Compute Embedding: Y = Vk times Lambda k to the power 0.5]
    V --> W[Final Output: Y in Rn times Rk]
    T --> X[Negative or Near-Zero Eigenvalues]
    X --> Y[Numerical Noise: Indicates Non-Euclidean Distances]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Workflow — PCA vs. MDS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High-Dimensional Data X] -->|Method 1: PCA| B[Covariance Sigma]
    A -->|Method 2: MDS| C[Distance Matrix D]
    B --> D[Eigendecomposition]
    C --> E[Double Centring then Eigendecomposition]
    D --> F[Project onto Top k Eigenvectors]
    E --> G[Embed via V k Lambda k to the power 0.5]
    F --> H[Low-D Embedding Y PCA]
    G --> I[Low-D Embedding Y MDS]
    H -->|Equivalent when D is Euclidean| J[Equivalent up to Rotation]
    I -->|Equivalent when D is Euclidean| J
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
